$$1 - \frac{\phi}{\phi_m} = 1 - \frac{0.55}{0.74} = 1 - 0.7432 = 0.2568$$

**Step 2: Calculate the numerator term.**

$$k_E \cdot \phi = 2.5 \times 0.55 = 1.375$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{k_E \phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m} = \frac{1.375}{0.2568} = 5.354$$

**Step 4: Take the exponential to find $\eta_r$.**

$$\eta_r = e^{5.354} = 211.8$$

**Step 5: Multiply by $\eta_0$ to get the solution viscosity.**

$$\eta = 211.8 \times 1.0 = 211.8 \text{ cP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** At $\phi = 0.55$ (just 74% of the way to $\phi_m$), the viscosity is **~212 times** the serum viscosity. This demonstrates the **explosive nature** of the viscosity-concentration relationship in dispersions.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Regime | Concentration Range | Dominant Physics | Key Equation | Typical Exponent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dilute** | $C < C^*$ | Isolated coils/particles | Huggins / Einstein | 1 (linear) |
| **Semi-dilute unentangled** | $C^* < C < C_e$ | Overlap, no entanglement | Martin / Krieger-Dougherty | ~1.0–1.5 |
| **Semi-dilute entangled** | $C_e < C < C^{**}$ | Entanglement onset | $\eta \propto C^{1–2}$ | ~2 |
| **Concentrated** | $C \gg C_e$ | Reptation, dense packing | Doi-Edwards / Mooney | ~3.4 (theory), up to 5–6 (latices) |
| **Dispersion (Latex)** | $\phi < \phi_m$ | Particle crowding | Mooney | Diverges at $\phi_m$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Three Concentration Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Polymer Solution or Latex"] --> B{"Concentration C vs Critical C*"}
    B -->|"C less than C*"| C["Dilute Regime"]
    B -->|"C greater than C*"| D["Semi-Dilute Regime"]
    B -->|"C much greater than C*"| E["Concentrated Regime"]
    C --> C1["Isolated coils"]
    C --> C2["No chain overlap"]
    C --> C3["Newtonian flow"]
    C --> C4["Huggins Equation applicable"]
    C --> C5["eta = eta0 times 1 plus 2.5 phi"]
    D --> D1["Overlapping coils"]
    D --> D2["Hydrodynamic interactions"]
    D --> D3["Mild shear thinning"]
    D --> D4["Martin Equation or early power law"]
    D --> D5["eta proportional to C to the 1 to 2"]
    E --> E1["Entangled network"]
    E --> E2["Reptation dynamics"]
    E --> E3["Strong viscoelasticity"]
    E --> E4["Power law with exponent 3.4"]
    E --> E5["Mooney Equation for latices"]
    C1 --> F["Viscosity rises slowly and linearly"]
    D1 --> G["Viscosity rises sharply"]
    E1 --> H["Viscosity rises exponentially or as power law"]
    F --> I["KTU Key Insight: Viscosity-Concentration relationship is fundamentally NON-LINEAR"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

    A --> J["Volume fraction phi"]
    J --> K["Mooney Equation: ln eta_r equals kE phi divided by 1 minus phi divided by phi_m"]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define intrinsic viscosity and explain its significance in characterizing polymer solutions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta]$ is defined as the limiting value of the reduced viscosity (or inherent viscosity) as the concentration of the polymer approaches zero. Mathematically:

$$[\eta] = \lim_{C \to 0} \frac{\eta_{sp}}{C} = \lim_{C \to 0} \frac{\ln \eta_r}{C}$$

The units are typically **dL/g** (deciliters per gram).

**Significance:**
1. It is a measure of the **hydrodynamic volume** occupied by a single polymer chain in a given solvent at a given temperature.
2. It is directly related to the polymer's **molecular weight** through the **Mark-Houwink equation**: $[\eta] = K M_v^a$.
3. It defines the **critical overlap concentration** $C^* = 1/[\eta]$, which marks the transition from dilute to semi-dilute behavior.
4. It is a fundamental parameter in understanding the **viscosity-concentration relationship** in the dilute regime.
5. Unlike viscosity at finite concentration, $[\eta]$ is **independent of concentration** and depends only on polymer-solvent interactions and chain dimensions.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for significance]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the Huggins equation? Explain the significance of the Huggins constant.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Huggins equation** is an empirical relation that describes the concentration dependence of the reduced viscosity of a polymer solution in the dilute regime:

$$\frac{\eta_{sp}}{C} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 C$$

where $\eta_{sp}$ is the specific viscosity, $C$ is the concentration, $[\eta]$ is the intrinsic viscosity, and $k_H$ is the **Huggins constant**.

**Significance of $k_H$:**
1. It characterizes **polymer-solvent interactions**. A value of $k_H \approx 0.3$–$0.4$ indicates a **good solvent**, while higher values ($k_H > 0.5$) indicate a **poor solvent** or aggregation.
2. It provides information about **chain dimensions and intermolecular interactions** at finite concentrations.
3. A plot of $\eta_{sp}/C$ vs. $C$ yields a straight line with slope $k_H [\eta]^2$ and intercept $[\eta]$, allowing experimental determination of both parameters.
4. For most systems, $k_H + k_K \approx 0.5$, where $k_K$ is the Kraemer constant.

**[3 Marks: 1 for equation, 1 for definition of $k_H$, 1 for significance]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **Mooney equation** for the viscosity of a concentrated latex dispersion. Clearly state the assumptions, define all symbols, and explain the physical significance of the maximum packing fraction $\phi_m$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A natural rubber latex has the following data at 25 °C:

| Concentration $C$ (g/dL) | Relative Viscosity $\eta_r$ |
|---|---|
| 0.10 | 1.28 |
| 0.20 | 1.62 |
| 0.30 | 2.10 |
| 0.40 | 2.78 |

Determine the **intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta]$ and the **Huggins constant** $k_H$ using a Huggins plot. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of the Mooney Equation**

**Assumptions:**
1. The dispersed particles are **rigid, spherical, and monodisperse**.
2. The continuous phase is a **Newtonian fluid**.
3. The dispersion is **uniform** (no settling or creaming).
4. There is **no slip** at the particle-continuous phase interface.
5. The system is at **steady state** during viscosity measurement.

**Key Symbols:**
- $\eta_r = \eta / \eta_0$: relative viscosity
- $\phi$: volume fraction of dispersed polymer particles
- $\phi_m$: maximum packing fraction (≈ 0.74 for random close packing of monodisperse spheres)
- $k_E$: Einstein coefficient (≈ 2.5 for hard spheres)

**Derivation:**

Einstein's law for a very dilute suspension:

$$\eta = \eta_0 (1 + k_E \phi)$$

For higher concentrations, the effective contribution of each additional increment of dispersed phase is **amplified** by the proximity of neighboring particles. Mooney postulated that this amplification factor is proportional to $1 / (1 - \phi/\phi_m)$, representing the inverse of the available free volume.

Therefore, the incremental change in $\ln \eta_r$ due to an incremental change in $\phi$ is:

$$d(\ln \eta_r) = \frac{k_E \, d\phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m}$$

Integrating from $\phi = 0$ (where $\eta_r = 1$) to an arbitrary $\phi$:

$$\int_0^{\ln \eta_r} d(\ln \eta_r) = k_E \int_0^{\phi} \frac{d\phi'}{1 - \phi'/\phi_m}$$

Let $u = 1 - \phi'/\phi_m$, $du = -d\phi'/\phi_m$. Limits: $u$ goes from 1 to $1 - \phi/\phi_m$.

$$\ln \eta_r = k_E \phi_m \int_{1-\phi/\phi_m}^{1} \frac{du}{u} = k_E \phi_m \left[-\ln\left(1 - \frac{\phi}{\phi_m}\right)\right]$$

Since $k_E \phi_m$ is typically absorbed into a single constant, or recognizing that for the Einstein limit, $k_E \phi_m \approx k_E$ when $\phi_m$ is incorporated:

$$\boxed{\ln \eta_r = \frac{k_E \, \phi}{1 - \phi/\phi_m}}$$

**Physical Significance of $\phi_m$:**

The maximum packing fraction $\phi_m$ represents the **highest possible volume fraction** of spherical particles that can be packed into a given space without deformation. For monodisperse hard spheres:
- **Random close packing**: $\phi_m \approx 0.64$
- **Random loose packing**: $\phi_m \approx 0.55$–$0.60$
- **Maximum theoretical (FCC/HCP)**: $\phi_m \approx 0.74$

As $\phi \to \phi_m$, the denominator $1 - \phi/\phi_m \to 0$, causing $\eta_r \to \infty$. This corresponds to the **jamming transition**, where particles can no longer flow past one another and the dispersion becomes a solid-like gel.

**[7 Marks: 2 for assumptions/symbols, 3 for derivation, 2 for $\phi_m$ significance]**

---

**(b) Determining $[\eta]$ and $k_H$ from Data**

**Step 1: Compute $\eta_{sp}$ and $\eta_{sp}/C$ for each data point.**

| $C$ (g/dL) | $\eta_r$ | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_r - 1$ | $\eta_{sp}/C$ (dL/g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.10 | 1.28 | 0.28 | 2.80 |
| 0.20 | 1.62 | 0.62 | 3.10 |
| 0.30 | 2.10 | 1.10 | 3.667 |
| 0.40 | 2.78 | 1.78 | 4.45 |

**Step 2: Apply the Huggins equation.**

$$\frac{\eta_{sp}}{C} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 C$$

This is a linear equation in $C$: $y = b + m \cdot C$ where $y = \eta_{sp}/C$, $b = [\eta]$, and $m = k_H [\eta]^2$.

**Step 3: Use linear regression (or two-point method).**

Using the first and last points:

$$[\eta] = b = 2.80 \text{ dL/g (from } C = 0.10\text{)}$$

More accurately, perform a least-squares fit:

$$m = \frac{4.45 - 2.80}{0.40 - 0.10} = \frac{1.65}{0.30} = 5.50 \text{ dL/g}^2$$

$$b = 2.80 - 5.50 \times 0.10 = 2.25 \text{ dL/g}$$

So: $[\eta] \approx 2.25$ dL/g.

**Step 4: Calculate $k_H$.**

$$k_H = \frac{m}{[\eta]^2} = \frac{5.50}{(2.25)^2} = \frac{5.50}{5.0625} = 1.087$$

**Step 5: Verify with intermediate points.**

At $C = 0.20$: $\eta_{sp}/C = 2.25 + 1.087 \times (2.25)^2 \times 0.20 = 2.25 + 1.087 \times 5.0625 \times 0.20 = 2.25 + 1.101 = 3.35$

Experimental: 3.10. The discrepancy suggests a slightly non-linear regime or measurement error.

**Results:**
- $[\eta] \approx 2.25$ dL/g
- $k_H \approx 1.09$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The high $k_H$ value (well above 0.4) suggests either a **poor solvent**, **polymer aggregation**, or that the data extends beyond the strict dilute regime. A more careful fit over a lower concentration range would yield a more reliable $k_H$.

**[7 Marks: 2 for table/calculations, 2 for plot/regression, 1 for $[\eta]$, 1 for $k_H$, 1 for interpretation]**

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **three concentration regimes** of polymer solutions (dilute, semi-dilute, concentrated). For each regime, describe the chain conformation, the dominant interactions, the rheological behavior, and the applicable mathematical model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Mark-Houwink equation**, calculate the **viscosity-average molecular weight** $M_v$ of a polymer sample given the following data:

- $[\eta] = 3.50$ dL/g
- $K = 0.50 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g
- $a = 0.70$

Comment on whether the solvent is "good" or "poor" based on the value of $a$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) The Three Concentration Regimes**

| Feature | **Dilute** ($C < C^*$) | **Semi-Dilute** ($C^* < C < C_e$) | **Concentrated** ($C > C_e$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chain Conformation** | Isolated, non-overlapping random coils | Overlapping, interpenetrating coils | Densely packed, entangled network |
| **Dominant Interactions** | Polymer-solvent (intra-chain) | Polymer-polymer (inter-chain) begins | Entanglements, topological constraints |
| **Rheological Behavior** | Newtonian, low viscosity | Mildly shear-thinning, non-Newtonian | Strongly viscoelastic, shear-thinning |
| **Mathematical Model** | Huggins, Einstein, Kraemer | Martin, Krieger-Dougherty | Doi-Edwards reptation, Mooney |
| **Concentration Scaling** | $\eta \propto C^1$ (linear) | $\eta \propto C^{1-2}$ | $\eta \propto C^{3.4}$ (reptation) |
| **Typical Concentration** | $< 0.5$ g/dL (for $[\eta] = 2$) | $0.5$–$5$ g/dL | $> 5$ g/dL |

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Dilute Regime ($C < C^*$):** Each polymer chain exists as a **swollen random coil** in a sea of solvent. The chains do not "see" each other. The solution behaves as a collection of independent hydrodynamic spheres (for coils) or hard spheres (for latex particles). The viscosity increase over the pure solvent is small and linear in $C$. The flow is **Newtonian** (viscosity independent of shear rate).

2. **Semi-Dilute Regime ($C^* < C < C_e$):** Chains begin to **overlap** but are not yet fully entangled. The solution is a transient mesh. Hydrodynamic and topological interactions become significant. The viscosity rises more steeply (power-law with exponent 1–2). The system shows **mild shear-thinning** because shear can partially disentangle overlapping chains.

3. **Concentrated Regime ($C > C_e$):** Chains form a **dense entangled network**. The motion of each chain is constrained by its neighbors — this is the regime of **reptation** (Doi-Edwards theory). The viscosity scales as $C^{3.4}$. The system is highly **viscoelastic** and exhibits strong shear-thinning, yield stress, and elastic recovery.

> **For Latex:** Replace chains with particles. The regimes are governed by the volume fraction $\phi$ relative to $\phi_m$. The Mooney equation describes the full range.

**[7 Marks: 2 for dilute, 2 for semi-dilute, 2 for concentrated, 1 for synthesis]**

---

**(b) Calculating $M_v$ using the Mark-Houwink Equation**

**Step 1: State the Mark-Houwink equation.**

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^a$$

where $K = 0.50 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g, $a = 0.70$, and $[\eta] = 3.50$ dL/g.

**Step 2: Solve for $M_v$.**

$$M_v^a = \frac{[\eta]}{K} = \frac{3.50}{0.50 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3.50}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.0 \times 10^4$$

$$M_v = (7.0 \times 10^4)^{1/0.70} = (7.0 \times 10^4)^{1.4286}$$

**Step 3: Compute the exponent.**

$$M_v = (7.0 \times 10^4)^{1.4286}$$

Take logarithms:

$$\log M_v = 1.4286 \times \log(7.0 \times 10^4)$$

$$\log(7.0 \times 10^4) = \log(7.0) + 4 = 0.8451 + 4 = 4.8451$$

$$\log M_v = 1.4286 \times 4.8451 = 6.922$$

$$M_v = 10^{6.922} = 8.36 \times 10^6$$

**Step 4: Comment on solvent quality.**

The Mark-Houwink exponent $a$ provides information about the solvent quality and chain stiffness:
- $a = 0.5$: Theta solvent (ideal chain)
- $a = 0.5$–$0.8$: Good solvent (swollen coil)
- $a = 0.8$–$1.0$: Very good solvent / rod-like behavior

Since $a = 0.70$, the solvent is a **good solvent** for this polymer. The chains are **expanded (swollen)** relative to their unperturbed dimensions, and the polymer-solvent interactions are favorable (exothermic mixing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In a good solvent, the intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ is **higher** than in a theta solvent for the same molecular weight, because the coil occupies a larger hydrodynamic volume. This directly impacts the viscosity-concentration relationship by lowering $C^*$.

**Result:** $M_v \approx 8.36 \times 10^6$ g/mol (a high-molecular-weight polymer, consistent with many elastomers).

**[7 Marks: 2 for equation setup, 2 for calculation, 1 for $M_v$ value, 2 for solvent quality comment]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing $M_v$ with $M_w$ or $M_n$**: The Mark-Houwink equation gives the *viscosity-average* molecular weight $M_v$, which lies between $M_n$ and $M_w$ but is closer to $M_w$. Writing "$M_w$" instead of "$M_v$" costs marks.
> 2. **Unit errors in $K$**: $K$ must be in dL/g (or g/dL with appropriate $a$). Mixing CGS and SI units is a frequent error.
> 3. **Forgetting to interpolate/extrapolate**: In the Huggins plot, students often take only the first data point and ignore the regression. Full credit requires demonstrating the linear fit.
> 4. **Misidentifying the regime**: Saying "concentrated regime" when the data is clearly in the dilute regime. Always identify $C^*$ first.
> 5. **Omitting the physical significance of $\phi_m$**: The Mooney equation is incomplete without explaining why the denominator matters (jamming, free volume).
> 6. **Sign errors in logarithms**: $\ln(\eta_r) = \dots$ — students sometimes write $\log_{10}$ instead of natural log in the Kraemer equation context.
> 7. **Forgetting the $1/C$ extrapolation**: Intrinsic viscosity is a *limit* as $C \to 0$. If you state $[\eta] = \eta_{sp}/C$ at a finite concentration, you lose 1 mark immediately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta]$ is the **limit** of reduced viscosity as $C \to 0$; it characterizes the **hydrodynamic volume** of a single chain/particle.
- **Critical overlap concentration** $C^* \approx 1/[\eta]$ marks the **dilute → semi-dilute transition**.
- **Huggins equation** governs the **dilute regime**: $\eta_{sp}/C = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 C$.
- **Huggins constant** $k_H \approx 0.3$–$0.4$ in good solvents; higher values indicate poor solvents or aggregation.
- **Mark-Houwink** equation: $[\eta] = K M_v^a$; $a = 0.5$ (theta), $0.5$–$0.8$ (good), $> 0.8$ (rigid rods).
- **Power-law scaling** in the **concentrated regime**: $\eta \propto C^{3.4}$ (Doi-Edwards reptation).
- **Mooney equation** for **latex dispersions**: $\ln \eta_r = k_E \phi / (1 - \phi/\phi_m)$.
- **Maximum packing fraction** $\phi_m \approx 0.74$ (monodisperse spheres); viscosity **diverges** as $\phi \to \phi_m$.
- **Einstein coefficient** $k_E = 2.5$ for very dilute hard spheres.
- **Doi-Edwards reptation** describes chain motion in the entangled regime; exponent is theoretically **3.4**.
- **Three regimes**: Dilute (isolated), Semi-dilute (overlapping), Concentrated (entangled).
- **Viscosity-concentration is non-linear** — it can rise **exponentially** or as a **power law**.
- **In latex technology**, TSC (Total Solid Content) is the practical concentration variable; $\phi$ is related to TSC via density.
- **Practical importance**: Controls dipping thickness, foam stability, pumpability, and film quality in latex processing.
- **For KTU**: Always identify the regime, state the applicable equation, and explain the physical significance of each parameter.
- **Units to remember**: $[\eta]$ in dL/g, $\eta$ in Pa·s or cP, $C$ in g/dL or wt%, $\phi$ is dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Free Energy and Wetting Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Free Energy and Wetting Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surface Free Energy (γ)

**Surface free energy** (also called **surface tension** when referring to a liquid–vapour interface) is defined as the excess Gibbs free energy per unit area of a surface or interface, arising because molecules at a surface experience an asymmetric force field compared to those in the bulk.

Formally, it is expressed as:

$$\gamma = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial A}\right)_{T,P,n_i}$$

where **γ** is the surface free energy (J/m² or N/m), **G** is the Gibbs free energy, and **A** is the interfacial area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Surface free energy is the *thermodynamic driving force* behind wetting, adhesion, and film formation. In latex technology, it governs how a colloidal latex droplet spreads on a substrate.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a group of friends holding hands in a circle. Friends in the *middle* are held from all sides and feel "complete." A friend on the *edge* has only one or two neighbours, so they have "unused" hand-holding potential — they are at a higher energy state. Surface molecules are like those edge friends: they have unsatisfied intermolecular bonds, giving the surface an **excess energy**.

## 1.2 Wetting Behaviour

**Wetting** is the ability of a liquid (e.g., a latex dispersion) to spread over and maintain intimate contact with a solid substrate. It is quantified macroscopically by the **contact angle (θ)** — the angle formed at the three-phase contact line between the liquid–vapour and liquid–solid interfaces, measured through the liquid.

| Wetting Regime | Contact Angle (θ) | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete wetting** | θ = 0° | Liquid spreads into a thin film |
| **Partial wetting** | 0° < θ < 90° | Liquid wets the surface moderately |
| **Non-wetting** | 90° ≤ θ < 180° | Liquid beads up on the surface |
| **Complete non-wetting** | θ = 180° | Theoretical limit (never attained) |

> [!NOTE]
> In latex coating and adhesive applications, a **low contact angle (θ < 90°)** is desirable for good substrate coverage. Surfactants are routinely added to latex formulations to reduce γ_LV and improve wetting.

## 1.3 Interfacial Tensions in the Three-Phase System

At the three-phase contact line, three interfacial tensions act:
- **γ_SV** — solid–vapour interfacial tension
- **γ_SL** — solid–liquid interfacial tension
- **γ_LV** — liquid–vapour interfacial tension (numerically equal to the liquid's surface tension)

These three tensions are vectorially balanced at equilibrium, which leads directly to **Young's equation** (derived in Section 3).

## 1.4 Relevance to Latex Technology

In latex technology, wetting behaviour controls:
1. **Film formation** — coalescence of latex particles on a substrate.
2. **Adhesion strength** of the dried film to paper, textile, or polymer.
3. **Penetration** of latex into porous substrates (paper, leather, fabric).
4. **Stability of pigment–latex** interactions in coating formulations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of Surface Free Energy

When a molecule is brought from the bulk to the surface, it loses approximately **half** of its cohesive interactions with neighbouring molecules. To create a new surface of area **dA**, work equal to **γ·dA** must be supplied. Hence, surface free energy has a dual character:
- **Thermodynamic** — an excess Gibbs free energy per unit area.
- **Mechanical** — a tensile force per unit length acting parallel to the surface (N/m).

The **thermodynamic condition for spontaneous spreading** of a liquid over a solid is that the total free energy of the system must *decrease*. This leads to the definition of the **spreading coefficient**.

## 2.2 Work of Adhesion (W_a) and Work of Cohesion (W_c)

- **Work of Cohesion (W_c):** The reversible work required to separate a liquid column of unit cross-sectional area into two parts (i.e., to create two new liquid–vapour interfaces).  

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$$

- **Work of Adhesion (W_a):** The reversible work required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface, creating a solid–vapour and a liquid–vapour interface.

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation** in its general thermodynamic form.

## 2.3 Young's Equation and Contact Angle

At thermodynamic equilibrium at the three-phase contact line, a horizontal force balance yields:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

This is **Young's equation** (1805). It is the foundational relationship governing wetting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Combining Young's equation with the Young–Dupré work of adhesion gives the celebrated combined form:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

This single equation allows the work of adhesion to be computed from a simple contact angle measurement and the liquid's known surface tension.

## 2.4 Spreading Coefficient (S)

The **spreading coefficient** quantifies the tendency of a liquid to spread spontaneously on a solid:

$$S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

| Condition | Outcome |
|---|---|
| **S > 0** | Complete spreading (θ = 0°) |
| **S ≤ 0** | Partial wetting (θ > 0°) |

In latex formulations, surfactants are designed to make **S as large as possible (or at least positive)** by reducing γ_LV and γ_SL.

## 2.5 Critical Surface Tension of Wetting (γ_c) — Zisman Plot

Zisman (1964) showed that for a homologous series of liquids on a given solid, **cos θ** plotted against γ_LV is approximately linear. Extrapolating cos θ = 1 (θ = 0°) gives the **critical surface tension of the solid (γ_c)**. A liquid with γ_LV < γ_c will completely wet the solid.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name / Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma = (\partial G / \partial A)_{T,P,n_i}$ | Surface free energy definition |
| 2 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Young's equation (force balance) |
| 3 | $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Young–Dupré equation (work of adhesion) |
| 4 | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Combined Young–Dupré form |
| 5 | $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | Work of cohesion of a pure liquid |
| 6 | $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | Spreading coefficient |
| 7 | $S > 0 \Rightarrow$ complete spreading | Spreading criterion |
| 8 | $\gamma_{LV} < \gamma_c$ | Zisman criterion for complete wetting |

> [!NOTE]
> All **γ** terms are in **J/m²** or **N/m** (dimensionally equivalent). Contact angle θ is in degrees or radians.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Latex and Polymer Engineering

- **Coating industry:** Predicts whether an aqueous latex will wet a polymer film, paper, or metal foil.
- **Adhesive formulation:** Maximises W_a to enhance bond strength.
- **Textile finishing:** Determines penetration of latex binders into fabric weaves.
- **Biomedical latex devices (gloves, catheters):** Surface energy manipulation improves skin contact comfort and barrier performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation (Force Balance at the Three-Phase Line)

**Setup:** A liquid drop rests on a flat, smooth, homogeneous solid surface in a vapour atmosphere. The drop is small enough that gravity is negligible (capillary regime). The drop reaches a mechanical equilibrium shape defined by a constant contact angle **θ**.

**Step 1 — Identify the three interfacial tensions acting at the contact line.**

Three interfaces meet at the contact line:
- Solid–vapour interface with tension **γ_SV** acting tangentially along the solid surface, pulling the contact line *outward* (away from the drop).
- Solid–liquid interface with tension **γ_SL** acting tangentially along the solid surface, pulling the contact line *inward* (under the drop).
- Liquid–vapour interface with tension **γ_LV** acting tangentially along the liquid surface, pulling the contact line along the liquid surface at angle **θ** to the horizontal.

**Step 2 — Resolve γ_LV into horizontal and vertical components.**

The liquid–vapour tension γ_LV acts along the curved liquid surface. At the contact line, this surface makes angle θ with the solid surface (measured through the liquid). Its horizontal component is:

$$\gamma_{LV,\,x} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

This component is directed *outward* (away from the drop), balancing γ_SV. The vertical component γ_LV sin θ is balanced by the normal reaction from the rigid solid and does not enter the horizontal balance.

**Step 3 — Apply mechanical equilibrium in the horizontal direction.**

For a stationary contact line, the net horizontal force per unit length must be zero:

$$\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain Young's equation.**

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\;}$$

This is **Young's equation** (1805). It relates the equilibrium contact angle to the three interfacial tensions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation (Thermodynamic Form)

**Step 1 — Define the work of adhesion W_a.**

Consider separating a unit area of solid–liquid contact in vacuum/vapour. Initially, we have an SL interface (energy γ_SL per unit area). After separation, we have an SV interface (γ_SV) and an LV interface (γ_LV). The work done *by* the system during this reversible separation equals the decrease in interfacial free energy:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

This is the **Dupré equation** (1869).

**Step 2 — Substitute Young's equation into the Dupré equation.**

From Young's equation, $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. Substituting:

$$W_a = (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}) + \gamma_{LV} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta + \gamma_{LV}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify to obtain the combined Young–Dupré equation.**

$$\boxed{\;W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)\;}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**

- If θ = 0° (complete wetting), then $W_a = 2\gamma_{LV} = W_c$. Adhesion equals cohesion.
- If θ = 180° (theoretical non-wetting), then $W_a = 0$. No adhesion.
- For any real system, $0 \le W_a \le W_c$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Spreading Coefficient

**Step 1 — Define the spreading process.**

Consider laying down a thin film of liquid on a solid surface previously in contact with vapour. The change in free energy per unit area when a small amount of liquid spreads to cover an additional area dA is:

$$dG = (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})dA - \gamma_{SV}\,dA$$

**Step 2 — Define the spreading coefficient S as the negative of this free energy change.**

$$S \equiv \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

**Step 3 — Express S in terms of W_a and W_c.**

Using $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ and $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$:

$$\boxed{\;S = W_a - W_c\;}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the spontaneous-spreading criterion.**

For the spreading to be thermodynamically spontaneous, $dG \le 0$, i.e.:

$$S \ge 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad W_a \ge W_c$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Text Row Conversion Logic (Summary):** Equations (2.3), (2.4) and (2.5) above are *not* independent — they form a closed thermodynamic set. Knowing any two of {γ_SV, γ_SL, γ_LV, θ, W_a, S} allows the third to be evaluated.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A latex dispersion has surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 38$ mN/m at 25 °C. When placed on a polyethylene substrate, the equilibrium contact angle is measured to be **θ = 72°**. The solid–liquid interfacial tension is determined independently to be $\gamma_{SL} = 24$ mN/m. Compute (a) the work of adhesion, (b) the work of cohesion, (c) the spreading coefficient, and (d) the solid–vapour interfacial tension.

**Solution:**

(a) **Work of adhesion** (Young–Dupré form):

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = 38 \times (1 + \cos 72°)$$

Since $\cos 72° = 0.3090$:

$$W_a = 38 \times (1 + 0.3090) = 38 \times 1.3090 = 49.74\ \text{mN/m}$$

(b) **Work of cohesion**:

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{LV} = 2 \times 38 = 76\ \text{mN/m}$$

(c) **Spreading coefficient**:

$$S = W_a - W_c = 49.74 - 76 = -26.26\ \text{mN/m}$$

Since $S < 0$, the latex *will not* spontaneously spread; it forms a finite contact angle (consistent with the observed θ = 72°).

(d) **Solid–vapour interfacial tension** (from Young's equation):

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = 24 + 38 \times 0.3090 = 24 + 11.74 = 35.74\ \text{mN/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A negative S and high contact angle confirm that this latex needs a **surfactant additive** to wet the polyethylene substrate properly. Reducing γ_LV from 38 mN/m to ~25 mN/m would push S toward zero and dramatically lower the contact angle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    nodeB --- nodeD

```mermaid

    in2["SURFACTANT ADDITIVE reduces gamma LV"]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** Surfactant addition lowers γ_LV, which in turn lowers θ and raises S, ultimately improving film formation — this is the *central engineering lever* in latex formulation for coating and adhesive applications.
