This is the **expected KTU answer** when asked for the "best" or "most realistic" model. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A] **Define Zeta Potential. Explain its significance in the stability of lyophobic colloids.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 Mark):** Zeta potential ($\zeta$) is the **potential difference existing at the slipping (or shear) plane** of a colloidal particle — the hydrodynamic boundary that separates the ions moving with the particle from those that remain in the bulk dispersion medium. It is *not* the surface potential $\psi_0$ but the potential at the outer edge of the Stern layer / inner edge of the diffuse layer. **Significance (2 Marks):** 1. **Stability Indicator:** A high absolute value of zeta potential ($\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ mV) indicates strong electrostatic repulsion between like-charged particles, which prevents aggregation. This is the **DVLO-type electrostatic stabilization**. 2. **Predicts Coagulation:** When $\zeta$ approaches zero (the **isoelectric point**), the repulsive barrier vanishes and **rapid coagulation** occurs (per Smoluchowski's fast coagulation theory). 3. **Controls Processes:** Zeta potential is used as a quality control parameter in formulations — pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, ceramics, food emulsions. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **zeta potential with surface potential** ($\psi_0$). Marks are deducted for treating them as the same. Remember: $\psi_0$ is at the actual particle surface; $\zeta$ is at the slipping plane — and **$\vert \zeta \vert < \vert \psi_0 \vert$** always. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B] **Differentiate between the Helmholtz and Gouy–Chapman models of the electrical double layer.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Feature | Helmholtz Model (1879) | Gouy–Chapman Model (1910–13) | |---------|------------------------|-------------------------------| | **Ion distribution** | Counter-ions rigidly fixed in a single plane | Counter-ions distributed per Boltzmann statistics | | **Thermal motion** | Ignored | Included | | **Potential profile** | Linear drop with distance | Exponential decay: $\psi(x) = \psi_0 e^{-\kappa x}$ | | **Equivalent circuit** | Parallel-plate capacitor | Diffuse capacitor with thermal spread | | **Limitation** | Unrealistic — ignores thermal agitation | Treats ions as point charges; overestimates concentration at surface | | **Applicability** | Concentrated electrolytes | Dilute electrolytes | **Stern model (1924)** combines both, giving a piecewise profile — linear in the Stern layer + exponential in the diffuse layer. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks) --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the origin of charge on colloidal particles. Discuss the **electrical double layer** with reference to the **Helmholtz, Gouy–Chapman, and Stern models**, using suitable potential-distance diagrams. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation** for electrophoretic mobility. A colloidal sol is subjected to an electric field of **10 V/cm**, and the particles move with a velocity of **$2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm/s**. The viscosity of the medium is **$1.002 \times 10^{-2}$ poise** and the dielectric constant is **80.1**. Calculate the **zeta potential** of the particles (in mV). Use CGS units. #### Model Solution: **(a) Origin of Charge [3 Marks] + Double Layer Models [4 Marks]:** Origin of charge on colloidal particles (discuss all four mechanisms — preferential adsorption, surface ionization, friction, isomorphic substitution) as given in Section 1.3. EDL models — present each with a **labelled potential-distance diagram**: - **Helmholtz (1879):** Linear potential drop, $\psi(x) = \psi_0 (1 - x/d)$. Treats EDL as parallel-plate capacitor. - **Gouy–Chapman (1910–13):** Exponential decay, $\psi(x) = \psi_0 e^{-\kappa x}$. Accounts for thermal motion; ions distributed per Boltzmann. - **Stern (1924):** Two-region model — *rigid* Stern layer (linear) + *diffuse* Gouy layer (exponential). Most realistic. Include the expression for $\kappa$ (Debye–Hückel parameter) and the physical meaning of $\kappa^{-1}$ (double-layer thickness). **(b) Derivation of HS Equation [3 Marks] + Numerical [4 Marks]:** **Derivation:** Provide the full force balance as shown in Section 3.1 above. Conclude with: $$\zeta = \frac{4 \pi \eta \, v}{\varepsilon \, E}$$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given (CGS): - $E = 10 \ \text{V/cm}$ - $v = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{cm/s}$ - $\eta = 1.002 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{poise} = 1.002 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{g/(cm·s)}$ - $\varepsilon = 80.1$ (dielectric constant) — note: in CGS, the absolute permittivity is $\varepsilon_r$ itself when used in the $4\pi\eta$ form, OR we must convert. **Solution using CGS form:** $\zeta = \dfrac{4 \pi \eta v}{\varepsilon_r \, E}$ $$\zeta = \frac{4 \pi \times (1.002 \times 10^{-2}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4})}{80.1 \times 10}$$ Numerator: $$4 \pi \times 1.002 \times 10^{-2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 4 \times 3.1416 \times 2.505 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 12.5664 \times 2.505 \times 10^{-6} = 31.48 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{(statV units in CGS)}$$ Denominator: $$80.1 \times 10 = 801$$ $$\zeta = \frac{31.48 \times 10^{-6}}{801} = 3.93 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{statV}$$ Converting statV → mV: $1 \ \text{statV} = 299.79 \ \text{V} = 299.79 \times 10^3 \ \text{mV}$ $$\zeta = 3.93 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.998 \times 10^{5} \ \text{mV}$$ $$\boxed{\;\zeta \approx 11.78 \ \text{mV}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Note:** If the question provides $\varepsilon$ in CGS (relative value), and uses CGS-form equation, the above method applies. If using SI: > > $$\zeta = \frac{\eta \, v}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, E}$$ > > with $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $E$ in V/m, $v$ in m/s. The numerical answer in mV will be the **same**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** What are **electrokinetic phenomena**? Classify and explain any two of them in detail with neat diagrams. Discuss their inter-relationship. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **DLVO theory** of colloidal stability. How does the **Schulze–Hardy rule** relate to it? A coagulation experiment shows that the critical coagulation concentration (CCC) for an $\text{NaCl}$ electrolyte is $50 \ \text{mmol/L}$. Estimate the CCC for $\text{BaCl}_2$ and $\text{AlCl}_3$ using Schulze–Hardy. #### Model Solution: **(a) Electrokinetic Phenomena [7 Marks]:** **Definition:** Electrokinetic phenomena are the phenomena in which the **relative motion of a charged surface and an electrolyte solution** produces effects — either motion caused by an electric field, or an electric field caused by motion. **Classification (4 phenomena, KTU-mandated):** 1. **Electrophoresis** — movement of *charged colloidal particles* in a stationary liquid under an applied electric field. The velocity $v = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta E}{4 \pi \eta}$ (HS equation). Direction: towards electrode of opposite sign to particle charge. Application: separation of proteins, DNA fingerprinting, EPD coatings. 2. **Electroosmosis** — movement of *liquid* through a fixed porous plug / capillary whose walls carry a fixed surface charge, under an applied electric field. Velocity: $U = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta E}{4 \pi \eta}$. Application: dewatering of soils, drug delivery, capillary electrophoresis. 3. **Streaming Potential** — *potential difference* generated when a liquid is *forced* (by pressure) through a porous plug or capillary. The streaming current builds up a back-EMF until conduction current balances it. Expression: $V_{\text{str}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta P}{4 \pi \eta \kappa_e}$. Application: electrokinetic soil remediation, dam monitoring. 4. **Sedimentation Potential (Dorn Effect)** — *potential difference* generated when colloidal particles *settle* under gravity through a liquid. Ions of one sign go with the particle, leaving a slight excess of opposite sign in the upper region. Expression: $V_{\text{sed}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta (\rho_p - \rho_m) g r^2}{3 \eta}$. Application: monitoring of suspension stability, mineral processing. **Inter-relationship (Reciprocity):** - Electrophoresis ↔ Sedimentation Potential (Onsager reciprocal pair) - Electroosmosis ↔ Streaming Potential (Onsager reciprocal pair) **Diagram:** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 above. **(b) DLVO Theory + Schulze–Hardy [7 Marks]:** **DLVO Theory (Derjaguin–Landau–Verwey–Overbeek, 1941–48):** The total potential energy of interaction $V_T$ between two colloidal particles is the **sum** of two contributions: $$V_T = V_R + V_A$$ where: - $V_R$ = **Repulsive potential** (electrostatic, due to overlap of double layers) — always positive. - $V_A$ = **Attractive potential** (London–van der Waals) — always negative. $$V_R = \dfrac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \, \gamma^2}{\kappa^2} \, e^{-\kappa H}$$ where $R$ = particle radius, $H$ = separation distance, $\gamma = \tanh(z e \psi_0 / 4 k_B T)$. $$V_A = -\dfrac{A_{H} R}{12 H}$$ where $A_H$ = Hamaker constant ($\sim 10^{-20}$ to $10^{-19}$ J). **Plot of $V_T$ vs $H$:** Shows a **primary minimum** (deep, at very small H — irreversible aggregation), a **primary maximum** (energy barrier — prevents aggregation), a **secondary minimum** (shallow, at moderate H — reversible flocculation). **Stability Criterion:** A colloid is **stable** if the primary maximum $V_{\max} > 15$–$25 \, k_B T$ (thermal energy). Adding electrolyte **lowers** the barrier by compressing the double layer. **Schulze–Hardy Rule (1900–1903):** The **critical coagulation concentration (CCC)** of an electrolyte for a lyophobic colloid varies **inversely as the sixth power of the valency** of the **counter-ion** (the ion of charge opposite to that of the colloid): $$\text{CCC} \propto \dfrac{1}{z^6}$$ **Calculation:** For the counter-ion of charge $z$: $$\text{CCC}(z) = \text{CCC}(1) \times \left(\dfrac{1}{z}\right)^6$$ For $\text{NaCl}$: $z = 1$, $\text{CCC} = 50 \ \text{mmol/L}$. For $\text{BaCl}_2$: $z = 2$ (assuming the colloid is negatively charged, so $\text{Ba}^{2+}$ is the counter-ion): $$\text{CCC}(\text{BaCl}_2) = 50 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^6 = \dfrac{50}{64} = 0.781 \ \text{mmol/L}$$ For $\text{AlCl}_3$: $z = 3$: $$\text{CCC}(\text{AlCl}_3) = 50 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^6 = \dfrac{50}{729} = 0.0686 \ \text{mmol/L}$$ | Electrolyte | Counter-ion valence | CCC (mmol/L) | |-------------|--------------------|--------------| | $\text{NaCl}$ | 1 | **50** | | $\text{BaCl}_2$ | 2 | **0.78** | | $\text{AlCl}_3$ | 3 | **0.069** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Schulze–Hardy):** > 1. The "6th power rule" is for the **counter-ion** valency, not the co-ion. If you write $z^6$ for the wrong ion, **0 marks**. > 2. The Schulze–Hardy rule applies to **lyophobic** colloids only. For lyophilic colloids (proteins, polymers), the rule is much weaker or does not apply. > 3. The rule fails for very high ionic strengths (the $z^6$ dependence is approximate, real values are closer to $z^{2}$ to $z^{7}$). > 4. Always state the assumption that the colloid is **negatively charged** (most KTU numericals assume this); if positively charged, swap the role of cation/anion. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision List — Electrical Properties of Colloidal Systems** - ⚡ **Every colloidal particle carries charge** — positive or negative — balanced by counter-ions in the diffuse layer. - ⚡ **Origin of Charge** mechanisms: preferential adsorption (Paneth–Fajans), surface ionization, friction, isomorphic substitution. (Most KTU-favoured: **preferential adsorption**.) - ⚡ **Electrical Double Layer (EDL)** has three models: **Helmholtz (linear)**, **Gouy–Chapman (exponential, $\psi_0 e^{-\kappa x}$)**, **Stern (combined — most accurate)**. - ⚡ **Debye length** $\kappa^{-1} \approx 0.304 / \sqrt{I}$ nm (water, 25 °C) — the **most important length scale** in colloid science. - ⚡ **Zeta potential ($\zeta$)** is at the *slipping plane*, not at the surface. $|\zeta| > 30$ mV = stable; $|\zeta| \approx 0$ = isoelectric point = coagulation. - ⚡ **Four Electrokinetic Phenomena**: Electrophoresis (particles move in E-field), Electroosmosis (liquid moves in E-field), Streaming Potential (V generated by forced flow), Sedimentation/Dorn Potential (V generated by settling). - ⚡ **Reciprocal pairs (Onsager):** Electrophoresis ↔ Sedimentation Potential; Electroosmosis ↔ Streaming Potential. - ⚡ **Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation (CGS):** $\zeta = \dfrac{4 \pi \eta v}{\varepsilon E}$; (SI): $\zeta = \dfrac{\eta v}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E}$. Electrophoretic mobility: $\mu_e = \dfrac{v}{E} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \zeta}{4 \pi \eta}$. - ⚡ **DLVO theory:** $V_T = V_R + V_A$ — Repulsion (EDL overlap) + Attraction (London vdW). Colloid stability determined by height of primary maximum. - ⚡ **Schulze–Hardy Rule:** $\text{CCC} \propto 1/z^6$ where $z$ = valency of **counter-ion**. Adding **multivalent ions** dramatically lowers CCC → easier coagulation. This is why **alum** ($\text{Al}^{3+}$) is used in water treatment. - ⚡ **Common engineering applications:** Water treatment, electrophoretic displays, pharmaceutical formulations, ceramic coatings (EPD), enhanced oil recovery, bioseparations. - ⚡ **Numerical pattern in KTU:** Electrophoresis → calculate $\zeta$ from $v$ and $E$. Schulze–Hardy → calculate CCC ratios. DLVO → sketch $V_T$ vs $H$ curve and identify the energy barrier. - ⚡ **Common confusions to avoid:** $\zeta$ vs $\psi_0$ (different planes), $\kappa$ vs $\kappa^{-1}$ (one is parameter, the other is length), $4\pi$ factor in CGS vs SI versions of HS equation.

topics.title: Thermal Movement of Molecules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Movement of Molecules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Thermal Movement of Molecules** refers to the continuous, random, and energetic motion of atoms, ions, and molecules in any physical state (solid, liquid, or gas) that arises directly from their **internal kinetic energy** — a kinetic energy intrinsically linked to the absolute temperature of the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Thermal motion is the stochastic (random) translational, rotational, and vibrational motion of particles whose average kinetic energy is directly proportional to the absolute temperature, as governed by the **Equipartition Theorem** and the **Maxwell–Boltzmann Statistics**."*

Mathematically expressed through the **equipartition principle**:

$$\langle E_{\text{kin}} \rangle = \tfrac{3}{2} k_B T$$

where:
- $k_B = \mathbf{1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \, J \cdot K^{-1}}$ is the **Boltzmann constant** (exact, per SI 2019 redefinition).
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**.
- The factor $3$ corresponds to the **three independent translational degrees of freedom** in 3-D space.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bumper-Cars at a Fairground"

Imagine a giant indoor bumper-car arena where:
- Each car represents a molecule.
- The **stadium lights** (and the heat they emit) represent the **temperature**.
- The cars never stop; the hotter the stadium gets, the faster and more chaotically the cars dart around, collide, rotate, and vibrate on their suspension springs.
- A small "ghost" car (Brownian particle) thrown in gets kicked about randomly — this is **Brownian motion**.
- After a long time, the perfume sprayed at one corner eventually spreads everywhere — this is **diffusion driven by thermal motion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In polymer technology, the thermal motion of chain segments determines the **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)**, **melting behaviour**, **viscoelasticity**, and **diffusion of penetrants** (oxygen, moisture, solvents) into polymers. Without thermal motion, there is **no processing, no curing, and no diffusion** in any polymeric system.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Modes of Molecular Motion

| Mode | Description | Energy Range (approx.) | Typical Example in Polymers |
|:-----|:------------|:----------------------:|:----------------------------|
| **Translational** | Centre-of-mass migration through space | High ($> k_B T$) | Gas molecules, liquid diffusion |
| **Rotational** | Rotation of the molecule about its centre of mass | Moderate ($\sim k_B T$) | Small molecules in liquids, polymer side-group rotation |
| **Vibrational** | Oscillation of atoms about equilibrium bond length | Quantised ($h\nu$) | Bond stretching in polymer backbone, IR-active modes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Flow — Why and How Molecules Move

- **Why do molecules move?**
  Because every molecule above **absolute zero (0 K)** possesses non-zero internal energy. According to the **kinetic theory of matter**, this energy is realised as motion. Even in a "solid" polymer, atoms vibrate about their mean positions with amplitudes controlled by temperature.

- **How is the motion characterised?**
  Statistically — we never describe a single molecule's velocity, only the **distribution** of velocities and the **mean** values (average, RMS, most probable).

- **What governs the *type* of motion available?**
  The **phase** of the material:
  - **Gas** → all 3 modes fully active, dominant translation.
  - **Liquid** → translation and rotation active, vibrations constrained by neighbours.
  - **Solid (crystalline polymer)** → mainly vibrations; rotations frozen.
  - **Amorphous polymer above $T_g$** → large-amplitude segmental motion (the "rubbery" state).

## 2.2 The Maxwell–Boltzmann Velocity Distribution (Conceptual)

The fraction of molecules with speeds between $v$ and $v+dv$ in an ideal gas at temperature $T$ is:

$$f(v)\, dv = 4\pi \left(\frac{m}{2\pi k_B T}\right)^{3/2} v^{2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{m v^{2}}{2 k_B T}\right) dv$$

where $m$ is the molecular mass. This distribution broadens and shifts to higher speeds as **$T$ increases** — a core KTU concept.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA TABLE — Memorise Before ESE**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbols & Units | Engineering Utility |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Average Translational KE per molecule | $\langle E_{\text{kin}} \rangle = \tfrac{3}{2} k_B T$ | $k_B$: J/K ; $T$: K | Predicts energy of diffusing species in polymer matrices |
| 2 | Root-Mean-Square speed | $v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{3 k_B T}{m}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{3 R T}{M}}$ | $R=8.314$ J/(mol·K); $M$: kg/mol | Escape-velocity problems, effusion in membranes |
| 3 | Most Probable speed | $v_{p} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2 k_B T}{m}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2 R T}{M}}$ | Same as above | Peak of Maxwell distribution |
| 4 | Mean speed | $\bar{v} = \sqrt{\tfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{8 R T}{\pi M}}$ | Same as above | Used in collision-frequency derivations |
| 5 | Mean Free Path | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} n}$ | $d$: molecular diameter (m); $n$: number density (m$^{-3}$) | Diffusion in dense polymer melts (tortuous path) |
| 6 | Fick's First Law (steady-state) | $J = -D\,\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $J$: mol/(m²·s); $D$: m²/s; $C$: mol/m³ | Predicts permeation through polymer films |
| 7 | Fick's Second Law (time-dependent) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Same as above | Predicts sorption/desorption kinetics |
| 8 | Stokes–Einstein Equation | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r}$ | $\eta$: viscosity (Pa·s); $r$: hydrodynamic radius (m) | Links thermal motion to viscosity in polymer solutions |
| 9 | Mean-Square Displacement (Brownian) | $\langle x^{2} \rangle = 2 D t$ (1-D) ; $\langle r^{2} \rangle = 6 D t$ (3-D) | $t$: time (s) | Foundation of **DLS** (Dynamic Light Scattering) in polymer characterisation |
| 10 | Diffusion coefficient vs. $T$ (Arrhenius) | $D = D_{0} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{a}}{R T}\right)$ | $E_{a}$: activation energy (J/mol) | Predicts diffusion above/below $T_g$ |

> **Notation Note:** In the table, all absolute-value or "given by" relationships are written using $\vert$ and $\mid$ delimiters inside LaTeX (e.g. $D \vert_{T>T_g}$) to preserve markdown-table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Thermal Motion Concept |
|:------------------|:--------------------------------------|
| **Polymer Processing (Injection Moulding, Extrusion)** | Viscosity drop with $T$ arises from increased chain-segment mobility. |
| **Membrane Separation** | Fick's first law governs the **permeability** of O₂, CO₂, H₂O through polymer films. |
| **Drug Delivery** | Diffusion of API molecules out of a polymer matrix follows $D \propto T / \eta$. |
| **Adhesion & Coating** | Inter-diffusion of polymer chain-ends across an interface is purely thermal-motion driven. |
| **Curing of Thermosets** | Increased $T$ accelerates the cross-linking reaction via enhanced segmental motion. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Translational Kinetic Energy of a Molecule

### Step 0 — Starting Assumption (Kinetic Theory of Ideal Gas)
Consider an ideal gas enclosed in a cube of side $L$. The gas contains $N$ identical molecules, each of mass $m$, moving randomly. Pressure arises from molecular collisions with the walls.

### Step 1 — Impulse–Momentum Argument for One Wall
A single molecule with velocity component $v_x$ along the $x$-axis hits a wall elastically and rebounds with $-v_x$. The change in momentum is $2 m v_x$. The time between successive collisions with the **same** wall is $\Delta t = 2L / v_x$. By Newton's second law:

$$F_{\text{avg, 1 mol}} = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 m v_x}{(2L / v_x)} = \frac{m v_x^{2}}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Total Force on the Wall
Summing over all $N$ molecules (the $y$ and $z$ components do not contribute to force on the $xz$-wall):

$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{m v_{x,i}^{2}}{L} = \frac{m}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_{x,i}^{2}$$

Pressure is force per unit area. The wall has area $L^{2}$, so:

$$P = \frac{F}{L^{2}} = \frac{m}{L^{3}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_{x,i}^{2} = \frac{N m}{V} \langle v_{x}^{2} \rangle$$

where $V = L^{3}$ is the volume and $\langle v_{x}^{2} \rangle = \frac{1}{N} \sum v_{x,i}^{2}$ is the mean-square $x$-component.

### Step 3 — Isotropy Argument
By symmetry, $\langle v_{x}^{2} \rangle = \langle v_{y}^{2} \rangle = \langle v_{z}^{2} \rangle$, and because $v^{2} = v_{x}^{2} + v_{y}^{2} + v_{z}^{2}$, we have:

$$\langle v^{2} \rangle = 3 \langle v_{x}^{2} \rangle \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \langle v_{x}^{2} \rangle = \frac{1}{3}\langle v^{2} \rangle$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the Ideal Gas Law
For one mole of an ideal gas, $PV = RT$ and $N = N_A$ (Avogadro's number), $N m = M$ (molar mass). Substituting:

$$P = \frac{N_A m}{V} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \langle v^{2} \rangle = \frac{M}{3 V} \langle v^{2} \rangle \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P V = \frac{1}{3} M \langle v^{2} \rangle$$

Equating with the ideal gas law $PV = RT$:

$$\frac{1}{3} M \langle v^{2} \rangle = R T \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{1}{2} M \langle v^{2} \rangle = \frac{3}{2} R T$$

### Step 5 — Per-Molecule Result
Dividing both sides by Avogadro's number $N_A$, noting $R / N_A = k_B$ and $M / N_A = m$:

$$\frac{1}{2} m \langle v^{2} \rangle = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$$

> **Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;\langle E_{\text{kin}} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2} m \langle v^{2} \rangle = \tfrac{3}{2} k_B T\;}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The **average** translational kinetic energy per molecule depends **only** on temperature, not on the mass, size, or chemical nature of the molecule — a profound universality.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Root-Mean-Square Speed ($v_{\text{rms}}$)

Starting directly from the result above:

$$\frac{1}{2} m v_{\text{rms}}^{2} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T \quad\Longrightarrow\quad v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 k_B T}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$$

**Numerical Check (KTU-Style Problem Hook):**
For N₂ at $T = 300$ K, $M = 28 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol:

$$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 8.314 \times 300}{0.028}} \approx 517 \text{ m/s}$$

This is the typical supersonic speed of air molecules at room temperature — thermal motion is **energetic**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Free Path ($\lambda$)

### Step 1 — Trajectory Model
Imagine a "test" molecule of diameter $d$ moving through a sea of stationary identical molecules (also of diameter $d$). Two molecules collide when their centres come within $d$ of each other. Sweeping along its path, the test molecule carves out a **collision cylinder** of cross-sectional area $\sigma = \pi d^{2}$.

### Step 2 — Cylinder Volume
In time $t$, the test molecule travels a length $v t$, sweeping a cylinder of volume:

$$V_{\text{cyl}} = \sigma \cdot v t = \pi d^{2} v t$$

### Step 3 — Number of Target Molecules Inside
If the number density is $n$ (molecules per m³), the expected number of collisions is:

$$N_{\text{coll}} = n \cdot \pi d^{2} v t$$

### Step 4 — Average Distance Between Collisions
The mean free path is the average distance travelled per collision:

$$\lambda = \frac{v t}{N_{\text{coll}}} = \frac{v t}{n \pi d^{2} v t} = \frac{1}{n \pi d^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Correction for Moving Targets
The above assumed stationary targets. With both molecules moving, the **relative speed** replaces $v$. For a Maxwellian distribution, the mean relative speed is $\sqrt{2}\,\bar{v}$. This introduces a $\sqrt{2}$ factor in the denominator:

$$\boxed{\;\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} n}\;}$$

> **Engineering Note:** In polymer melts, $n$ is huge (molecules are tightly packed), so $\lambda$ is on the order of **nanometres** — explaining why bulk polymer diffusion is so slow.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Connection to Diffusion: Fick's First Law from Random Walk

### Step 1 — Random Walk Model
A diffusing molecule performs a 1-D random walk. At each step, it moves a distance $\ell$ (mean free path) in $+x$ or $-x$ direction with equal probability. After $N$ steps:

$$x = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \epsilon_i \ell \qquad \text{where } \epsilon_i = \pm 1$$

### Step 2 — Mean-Square Displacement
Take the expectation value $\langle \cdot \rangle$ of $x^{2}$:

$$\langle x^{2} \rangle = \ell^{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \langle \epsilon_i \epsilon_j \rangle$$

For $i \neq j$, the steps are uncorrelated: $\langle \epsilon_i \epsilon_j \rangle = 0$. For $i = j$, $\langle \epsilon_i^{2} \rangle = 1$. There are exactly $N$ such terms, so:

$$\langle x^{2} \rangle = N \ell^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Relate to Time
If the molecule makes $N = \nu t$ steps per unit time (collision frequency $\nu$), then:

$$\langle x^{2} \rangle = \nu \ell^{2} t$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Diffusion Coefficient
Comparing with the **Einstein diffusion relation** $\langle x^{2} \rangle = 2 D t$ (1-D):

$$\boxed{\;D = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu \ell^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} v \lambda\;}$$

This beautiful result shows: **diffusion is a direct macroscopic consequence of microscopic thermal motion**. Faster molecules and longer mean free paths give higher $D$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparative Summary

| Quantity | Depends on Mass $m$? | Depends on $T$? | Depends on Density $n$? | Polymer-Relevant? |
|:---------|:--------------------:|:---------------:|:-----------------------:|:-----------------:|
| $\langle E_{\text{kin}} \rangle$ | **No** | **Yes (linearly)** | No | High |
| $v_{\text{rms}}$ | **Yes** ($\propto 1/\sqrt{m}$) | **Yes** | No | Medium |
| $\lambda$ | Indirectly (via $d$) | Indirectly (via $n$) | **Yes** ($\propto 1/n$) | **Very High** |
| $D$ | **Yes** | **Yes (Arrhenius)** | **Yes** | **Very High** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA1`, `stepB2`) and contain **no** markdown formatting characters inside double-quoted labels. Reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are avoided as standalone IDs.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Cascade in Thermal Motion

This block diagram maps the *flow of thermal energy* from a macroscopic heat source down to the microscopic kinetic modes that drive polymer behaviour.

```mermaid

    nodeA2["TEMPERATURE T KELVIN"]:::core

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000

```

**How to read this chart:**
- **Yellow block** — external thermal input.
- **Blue block** — the macroscopic state variable ($T$).
- **Green blocks** — the three molecular modes that absorb the energy.
- **Orange blocks** — observable macroscopic consequences (Brownian motion, diffusion, viscosity, permeation).
- **Purple blocks** — engineering applications in polymer technology.

## 4.2 Concept Map — Relationship Between Diffusion, Viscosity, and Thermal Energy

```mermaid
graph LR
    nT["ABSOLUTE T"]:::core
    nE["AVERAGE KINETIC ENERGY 3 OVER 2 KB T"]:::core
    nV["RMS SPEED"]:::core

    nD["DIFFUSION COEFFICIENT"]:::obs
    nVisc["VISCOSITY ETA"]:::obs

    nT --> nE
    nT --> nV
    nT --> nD
    nE --> nV
    nV --> nL
    nL --> nD
    nD --> nSE
    nVisc --> nSE
```
