
### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mass-Transfer Stages During Drying

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Stage 1 25 to 60 deg C Free Water Evaporation Rapid Mass Loss"]
    S2["Stage 2 60 to 70 deg C Volatile FA and NH3 Driven Off"]
    S3["Stage 3 70 deg C Plateau 16 h Diffusive Water Removal from Film"]
    S4["Stage 4 above 100 deg C Bound Water plus Risk of Rubber Oxidation Mass Gain"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S3:::highlight
    classDef highlight fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

The shaded Stage 3 represents the **ISO 124 standardized region** — the sweet spot where drying is complete but rubber oxidation is negligible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define *Total Solids Content* of a latex sample. State the governing international standard and the standard drying conditions used in the test.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Total Solids Content (TSC or TS) of latex is defined as the mass of non-volatile material remaining after a known mass of latex has been completely dried to constant weight, expressed as a percentage of the original latex mass. It includes the rubber hydrocarbon, all non-rubber solids (proteins, lipids, sugars, salts), and any non-volatile compounded additives.

**Standard (1 mark):** The test is governed by **ISO 124:2014** (Rubber, raw natural — Determination of total solids content).

**Drying conditions (0.5 mark):** The standard drying regime is **70 ± 2 °C for 16 hours** in a circulating-air oven, with constant-mass verification (≤ 0.5 mg change between successive weighings after additional 1 h re-heating cycles).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Differentiate between *Total Solids (TS)* and *Dry Rubber Content (DRC)* of a natural rubber latex concentrate. Why are both tests performed?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Total Solids (TS) | Dry Rubber Content (DRC) |
|---|---|---|
| **What is measured** | Rubber + all non-rubber solids | Rubber hydrocarbon only |
| **Method** | Drying a known mass of latex | Coagulate, wash, dry coagulum |
| **Standard** | ISO 124:2014 | ISO 126:2005 |
| **Typical value (centrifuged HA)** | ≥ 61.0 % | ≥ 60.0 % |
| **Includes proteins, salts, etc.?** | Yes | No |

**Why both are performed (1 mark):** TS gives the *total non-volatile mass* in the latex (used for invoicing and process control of the concentration step), while DRC gives the *pure rubber mass* (used by the compounder to calculate the dry-rubber content of a formulation, e.g., for dipped goods). Their difference (**NRS = TS − DRC**) is a quality indicator — abnormal NRS can signal biological degradation, addition of adulterants, or processing errors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module-2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the procedure for the determination of **Total Solids content** of a natural rubber latex concentrate as per **ISO 124:2014**, including apparatus, reagents, and precautions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A latex sample gives the following data:
- $W_1 = 38.2214$ g
- $W_3 = 40.3506$ g
- $W_2 = 39.5440$ g
- DRC of the same sample = 58.4 %

Calculate (i) Total Solids, (ii) Non-Rubber Solids, and (iii) state whether the sample meets the ISO 2004 specification for HA-grade NR latex concentrate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks):**

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
- Analytical balance, readability **0.1 mg** (0.0001 g).
- Flat-bottomed glass petri dish or aluminum moisture dish (~ 75 mm diameter).
- Circulating-air drying oven, thermostatted to **70 ± 2 °C**.
- Desiccator charged with silica gel or fused CaCl₂.
- Pipette or dropper for sample transfer.

**Reagents:** None required (the test is purely physical; no chemical reagents).

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. Dry the empty dish in the oven at 70 °C for 30 min, cool in desiccator for 30 min, and weigh to **0.0001 g** ($W_1$).
2. Bring the latex sample to **23 ± 2 °C** and stir gently for 5 min.
3. Pipette **2.0 ± 0.2 g** of latex into the dish, reweigh immediately ($W_3$).
4. Spread the latex into a thin, uniform film by gentle swirling.
5. Place the dish in the **70 ± 2 °C** circulating-air oven for **16 h**.
6. Transfer to desiccator, cool 30 min, weigh ($W_2$).
7. Re-heat for 1 h, cool, re-weigh. Repeat until **constant mass** (consecutive weighings agree within 0.5 mg).

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Avoid skin formation on the wet film (traps moisture).
- Do not exceed 70 °C (oxidative mass gain at higher temperatures).
- Stir sample gently — no air entrainment (foam causes weighing errors).
- Use covered dish for first 1 h if ammonia-preserved latex (reduces NH₃ flash-off error).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Solids (3 marks):**

$$W_2 - W_1 = 39.5440 - 38.2214 = 1.3226 \text{ g}$$

$$W_3 - W_1 = 40.3506 - 38.2214 = 2.1292 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{TS} = \frac{1.3226}{2.1292} \times 100 = 62.115 \approx 62.12 \text{ \%}$$

**(ii) Non-Rubber Solids (2 marks):**

$$\text{NRS} = \text{TS} - \text{DRC} = 62.12 - 58.40 = 3.72 \text{ \%}$$

**(iii) Specification check (2 marks):**

ISO 2004:2017 requires **TS ≥ 61.0 %** for HA-grade centrifuged NR latex concentrate. The measured TS = 62.12 % **passes** the specification (margin = +1.12 %). The batch can be released for further use. NRS of 3.72 % is within the typical range (2.0–3.5 %, marginally elevated) — could indicate slight serum retention or minor biological activity; recommend a VFA test to cross-check.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, significance, and engineering utility** of the Total Solids test in the natural rubber latex industry. Discuss how TS measurement fits into a typical quality control workflow at a latex concentrate plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two natural rubber latex samples are tested for Total Solids, yielding the following data:

| Sample | $W_1$ (g) | $W_3$ (g) | $W_2$ (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 41.2032 | 43.3850 | 42.5100 |
| B | 41.2032 | 43.4120 | 42.4515 |

Calculate the TS of each sample. If a compounder needs **250 g of dry rubber** for a dipping formulation, calculate the mass of each latex required (assume the compounder uses TS as a proxy for rubber content, i.e., ignores the small NRS correction). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principle, Significance, Utility (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** The test is a **thermogravimetric determination**. A known mass of latex is dried under controlled conditions (70 °C, 16 h) until all volatile matter (water, ammonia, volatile fatty acids) is removed. The mass of the non-volatile residue, expressed as a percentage of the original latex mass, is the Total Solids.

**Significance (2 marks):** TS is the single most important concentration parameter for commercial latex. It is used for (i) batch release against ISO 2004 specifications, (ii) billing and custody transfer, (iii) calculation of dry-rubber mass in compounding, and (iv) process control of the centrifugal concentrator.

**Engineering utility and QC workflow (3 marks):**
- *In-line:* NIR or microwave sensors give continuous TS readings at the concentrate outlet of the centrifuge, enabling real-time adjustment of dilution-water valves.
- *At-line:* Hourly grab samples are auto-diluted and analyzed by a process NIR.
- *Lab verification:* ISO 124 oven method serves as the **primary reference** against which all in-line instruments are calibrated.
- TS results are stored in the **LIMS (Laboratory Information Management System)** and become part of the **Certificate of Analysis (CoA)** shipped with the drum.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Sample A (2.5 marks):**

$$W_2 - W_1 = 42.5100 - 41.2032 = 1.3068 \text{ g}$$

$$W_3 - W_1 = 43.3850 - 41.2032 = 2.1818 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{TS}_A = \frac{1.3068}{2.1818} \times 100 = 59.90 \text{ \%}$$

**Sample B (2.5 marks):**

$$W_2 - W_1 = 42.4515 - 41.2032 = 1.2483 \text{ g}$$

$$W_3 - W_1 = 43.4120 - 41.2032 = 2.2088 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{TS}_B = \frac{1.2483}{2.2088} \times 100 = 56.52 \text{ \%}$$

**Compounding calculations (2 marks):**

Mass of dry rubber needed: 250 g. Using $M_{\text{latex}} = M_{\text{rubber}} \times 100 / \text{TS}$:

**For Sample A:**

$$M_A = \frac{250 \times 100}{59.90} = 417.36 \text{ g}$$

**For Sample B:**

$$M_B = \frac{250 \times 100}{56.52} = 442.32 \text{ g}$$

Sample B requires 24.96 g more latex to deliver the same dry-rubber mass — clearly demonstrating why **buyers prefer higher-TS concentrate**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula inversion trap:** Many students write TS = $(W_3 - W_1)/(W_2 - W_1)$, giving a value less than 1, which the examiner will mark **0** for the formula step.
> 2. **Specifying the standard:** Writing "BS 1672" or "ASTM D1076" without acknowledging **ISO 124:2014** is the current governing standard will cost a half-mark. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically references ISO standards.
> 3. **Confusing TS with DRC:** Examiners regularly award 0 if a student confuses the two tests in a "differentiate" question. Use the table format — it earns full structure marks.
> 4. **Unit omission:** Always state TS in **% (w/w)** or **% (m/m)** — never leave it as a bare number.
> 5. **Constant-mass step forgotten:** Procedural questions (7-mark) lose a mark if "constant mass verification" is omitted from the procedure.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total Solids (TS)** = mass of non-volatile residue / mass of original latex × 100, determined per **ISO 124:2014** at **70 ± 2 °C for 16 h**.
- **TS ≠ DRC** — TS includes all non-rubber solids; DRC is rubber hydrocarbon only.
- **NRS = TS − DRC**, typically **2.0–3.5 %** for NR latex concentrate.
- **Three masses** are recorded: $W_1$ (empty dish), $W_3$ (dish + wet latex), $W_2$ (dish + dried film).
- **Constant-mass criterion:** consecutive weighings within **0.5 mg** after additional 1 h re-heat.
- **Sample mass:** **2.0 ± 0.2 g**; dish: flat-bottomed glass or aluminum.
- **HA-grade specification (ISO 2004):** TS ≥ **61.0 %**, DRC ≥ **60.0 %**.
- **Field latex** has TS ≈ 30–36 %; **centrifuged concentrate** has TS ≈ 60–62 %.
- **High-temperature drying (100–105 °C)** is faster but risks oxidative mass gain in unsaturated rubbers — avoid for NR latex.
- **Use of TS in industry:** process control (centrifuge regulation), invoicing (dry-rubber mass), formulation (compounding), and specification compliance (ISO 2004).
- **Most common student error:** inverting the formula — memorize: **"dried mass over wet mass, times 100"**.
- **Equation to memorize:** $\text{TS} = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_3 - W_1} \times 100$ — no other form is acceptable in KTU exams.
- **Engineering intuition:** TS is the latex analogue of Brix degree in sugar industry and total dissolved solids (TDS) in water treatment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry Rubber Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dry Rubber Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dry Rubber Content (DRC)** is defined as the mass of dry rubber obtained when a known mass of latex is coagulated, the coagulum is thoroughly washed to remove all non-rubber constituents, dried to constant mass, and then weighed. It is expressed as the **percentage (w/w)** of dry rubber per 100 grams of latex sample.

Mathematically, the standard KTU-formatted expression is:

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{dry rubber}}}{M_{\text{latex sample}}} \times 100
$$

where:
- $M_{\text{dry rubber}}$ = Mass of the dried, washed rubber coagulum (in grams)
- $M_{\text{latex sample}}$ = Mass of the original latex sample taken (in grams)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of milk:** When you buy toned milk, the label says "Fat Content: 4.5%". This means that out of every 100 g of milk, only 4.5 g is actual fat — the rest is water, proteins, lactose, and minerals. **Dry Rubber Content (DRC) works exactly the same way** for natural rubber latex (NRL). Latex, as it comes from the *Hevea brasiliensis* tree, is a colloidal dispersion. It is **NOT pure rubber**; it contains water (typically 55–60%), proteins, lipids, sugars, resins, ash, and only about 30–40% actual rubber hydrocarbon.

So if a sample of field latex weighs **100 g**, the DRC tells you precisely **how much of that 100 g is the actual marketable rubber polymer** after removing all the water and non-rubber serum components.

## 1.3 Distinction Between DRC and Total Solid Content (TSC)

This is a **HIGH-YIELD KTU question** because examiners frequently test whether students understand the difference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:**
> - **DRC (Dry Rubber Content):** Considers ONLY the rubber hydrocarbon fraction (coagulable fraction) after coagulation and washing. Non-rubber solids are **deliberately excluded**.
> - **TSC (Total Solid Content):** Considers the **TOTAL mass of all solids** (rubber + proteins + sugars + lipids + ash + resins) left after complete evaporation of water.

Mathematically, this relationship is expressed as:

$$
\text{TSC (\%)} = \text{DRC (\%)} + \text{NRSC (\%)} = \text{DRC (\%)} + \text{Sludge (\%)} + \text{Ash + Proteins (\%)}
$$

where **NRSC** = Non-Rubber Solid Content.

The numerical gap between TSC and DRC is typically **3% to 5%** in normal latex, and this gap is one of the **most important quality indicators** for assessing latex purity.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Framework (KTU-Aligned)

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range in Fresh Field Latex |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------------------|
| Dry Rubber Content | DRC | 30% – 40% |
| Total Solid Content | TSC | 33% – 45% |
| Non-Rubber Solid Content | NRSC | 3% – 5% |
| Volatile Fatty Acid Number | VFA No. | ≤ 0.20 (fresh latex) |
| Alkalinity (NH₃ preserved) | — | 0.15% – 0.25% w/w |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Principles Behind DRC Determination

> [!NOTE]
> The determination of DRC is based on the principle of **selective coagulation**. When dilute acetic acid (a weak organic acid) is added to latex, the negatively charged rubber particles (stabilized by proteins and fatty acid soaps) lose their charge, collide, and fuse into a continuous coagulum. The serum containing proteins, sugars, and salts is decanted, and the coagulum is repeatedly washed — **only the rubber polymer remains**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Laboratory Procedure

1. **Sampling:** A representative latex sample (~10 g) is taken in a clean, pre-weighed porcelain evaporating dish (or aluminum dish). Mass $M_1$ is recorded.
2. **Coagulation:** The sample is diluted with distilled water (1:1 ratio) and 2% **acetic acid** is added dropwise with gentle stirring until the latex coagulates completely (pH ≈ 4.5–4.8, the isoelectric point of natural rubber).
3. **Washing:** The coagulated mass is washed **repeatedly with water** until the wash water is neutral to litmus and free of soluble impurities. This removes all proteins, sugars, and salts.
4. **Rolling / Sheeting:** The washed coagulum is passed through a hand-operated smooth roller to form a thin sheet (~2 mm thickness). Rolling also expels trapped water.
5. **Drying:** The sheet is dried in a **hot air oven at 70 ± 2°C for 16 hours** (or to constant mass).
6. **Cooling & Weighing:** The dried sheet is cooled in a desiccator for 30 minutes and weighed. Mass $M_2$ is recorded.
7. **Repetition:** Drying-cooling-weighing is repeated until two consecutive masses agree within **± 0.001 g** (constant mass achieved).

## 2.2 The Core Mathematical Identity

The fundamental governing equation:

$$
\boxed{\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{(M_2 - M_0)}{(M_1 - M_0)} \times 100}
$$

where:
- $M_0$ = Mass of the empty dish (in grams)
- $M_1$ = Mass of dish + wet latex sample (in grams)
- $M_2$ = Mass of dish + dried rubber sheet (in grams)
- $(M_1 - M_0)$ = Net mass of latex sample
- $(M_2 - M_0)$ = Net mass of dry rubber recovered

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Significance |
|---|---------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | DRC (%) | $\dfrac{M_{\text{dry rubber}}}{M_{\text{latex}}} \times 100$ | Primary purity indicator |
| 2 | TSC (%) | $\dfrac{M_{\text{residues}}}{M_{\text{latex}}} \times 100$ | Total solids after evaporation |
| 3 | NRSC (%) | $\text{TSC} - \text{DRC}$ | Non-rubber impurity load |
| 4 | Serum Volume Fraction | $1 - \text{TSC fraction}$ | Indicates water content |
| 5 | Sludge Content (%) | $\dfrac{M_{\text{sludge}}}{M_{\text{latex}}} \times 100$ | Bottom-fraction impurities |
| 6 | Mass of Dry Rubber | $M_2 - M_0$ | From gravimetric measurement |
| 7 | Mass of Latex Sample | $M_1 - M_0$ | Wet sample taken |
| 8 | Dilution Factor (DF) | $\dfrac{V_{\text{water added}}}{V_{\text{latex}}}$ | Required for proper coagulation |
| 9 | Acid Normality Used | 2% (w/v) acetic acid | Standard precipitating medium |
| 10 | Drying Temperature | $70 \pm 2\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Hot air oven for 16 hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exam problems:** Always express the answer in **% w/w** unless explicitly asked otherwise. Use **2 decimal places** of precision to match board examiner expectations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DRC parameter is the **single most critical commercial metric** in the entire natural rubber industry because:

- **Pricing of latex concentrates:** Latex concentrate (60% DRC latex) is priced based on the exact DRC delivered to the buyer's tank.
- **Yield calculations:** Rubber plantation owners and tappers' wages are often linked to estimated DRC of field latex per tap.
- **Quality control at centrifuging stations:** Before and after centrifugation, DRC is measured to determine the concentration efficiency.
- **Specification compliance:** International standards (ISO 126, IS 5430) require DRC between **58–62%** for high-ammonia (HA) latex concentrate.
- **Process control:** A falling DRC may indicate dilution by rain, micro-organism contamination, or over-tapping stress on trees.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example 1 (Standard Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU University Exam Style):** During a routine DRC test, a technician took 10.50 g of fresh field latex in a porcelain dish of mass 45.00 g. After coagulation, washing, drying, and weighing, the final constant mass of the dish with the dried rubber sheet was 48.60 g. Calculate the DRC of the latex.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**

- Mass of empty dish: $M_0 = 45.00\ \text{g}$
- Mass of dish + wet latex: $M_1 = 45.00 + 10.50 = 55.50\ \text{g}$
- Mass of dish + dry rubber sheet: $M_2 = 48.60\ \text{g}$

**Step 2 — Calculate the mass of the latex sample:**

$$
M_{\text{latex}} = M_1 - M_0 = 55.50 - 45.00 = 10.50\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the mass of the dry rubber:**

$$
M_{\text{dry rubber}} = M_2 - M_0 = 48.60 - 45.00 = 3.60\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the DRC equation:**

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{dry rubber}}}{M_{\text{latex}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{3.60}{10.50} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division:**

$$
\frac{3.60}{10.50} = 0.342857142857\ldots
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to percentage:**

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = 0.342857 \times 100 = 34.29\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{DRC} = 34.29\%\ \text{(w/w)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always show the substitution of numerical values, the intermediate division, and the final result rounded to 2 decimal places. Avoid skipping even a single arithmetic step.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example 2 (DRC with TSC and NRSC)

**Problem:** A latex concentrate sample was tested and the following data were obtained:
- Mass of empty dish: 32.40 g
- Mass of dish + latex sample (for DRC test): 42.45 g
- Mass of dish + dried rubber (DRC test): 39.60 g
- Mass of dish + latex (for TSC test, separate dish): 37.30 g
- Mass of dish + total residues after oven drying (TSC test): 36.00 g

Calculate: (a) DRC, (b) TSC, and (c) NRSC.

### Solution

**Part (a) — DRC calculation:**

$$
M_{\text{latex (DRC)}} = 42.45 - 32.40 = 10.05\ \text{g}
$$

$$
M_{\text{dry rubber}} = 39.60 - 32.40 = 7.05\ \text{g}
$$

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{7.05}{10.05} \times 100 = 70.15\%
$$

**Part (b) — TSC calculation:**

We need the empty dish mass for the TSC dish. Let us denote it $M_0' = 33.20\ \text{g}$ (assumed given in full problem; here for calculation):

$$
M_{\text{latex (TSC)}} = 37.30 - 33.20 = 4.10\ \text{g}
$$

$$
M_{\text{total residues}} = 36.00 - 33.20 = 2.80\ \text{g}
$$

$$
\text{TSC (\%)} = \frac{2.80}{4.10} \times 100 = 68.29\%
$$

**Part (c) — NRSC calculation:**

$$
\text{NRSC (\%)} = \text{TSC (\%)} - \text{DRC (\%)} = 68.29 - 70.15 = -1.86\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A negative NRSC is physically impossible.** This indicates either: (i) experimental error in the TSC measurement, (ii) loss of dry rubber in the TSC dish, or (iii) moisture re-absorption in the TSC dish. In a properly run lab test, TSC must be **≥ DRC** always, because TSC includes DRC plus all other solids. Real-world typical values for centrifuged latex concentrate show TSC ≈ 61.5% and DRC ≈ 60.0%, giving NRSC ≈ 1.5%.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example 3 (Reverse Calculation for Pricing)

**Problem:** A rubber buyer contracted to purchase 5000 kg of latex concentrate at the rate of ₹140 per **kg of dry rubber (DRC basis)**. The laboratory reported the DRC of the delivered lot as 60.50%. Calculate (a) the total mass of dry rubber delivered, and (b) the total price to be paid.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Mass of dry rubber:**

$$
M_{\text{dry rubber}} = M_{\text{latex}} \times \frac{\text{DRC}}{100}
$$

$$
M_{\text{dry rubber}} = 5000 \times \frac{60.50}{100} = 5000 \times 0.6050 = 3025.0\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Total price:**

$$
\text{Price} = M_{\text{dry rubber}} \times \text{Rate} = 3025.0 \times 140 = \text{₹}\ 4{,}23{,}500
$$

**Final Answer:** Mass of dry rubber = 3025.0 kg; Total price = ₹ 4,23,500

## 3.4 Derivation of the Coagulation-Mass Balance

Starting from the latex as a two-phase system (rubber particles dispersed in serum):

$$
M_{\text{latex}} = M_{\text{rubber}} + M_{\text{serum}}
$$

The serum itself contains water and non-rubber solids:

$$
M_{\text{serum}} = M_{\text{water}} + M_{\text{NRSC}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_{\text{latex}} = M_{\text{rubber}} + M_{\text{water}} + M_{\text{NRSC}}
$$

Dividing throughout by $M_{\text{latex}}$:

$$
1 = f_{\text{rubber}} + f_{\text{water}} + f_{\text{NRSC}}
$$

Multiplying by 100 to express as percentages:

$$
100\% = \text{DRC (\%)} + W\% + \text{NRSC (\%)}
$$

where $W\%$ is the percentage of water in the latex (typically 55–65% in field latex, 35–40% in concentrate).

This elegant relationship is the **foundation of the mass balance** used in rubber process engineering calculations, and is a **favourite KTU Part B question**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Workflow for DRC Determination

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow maps the **interactions and decision points** involved in the laboratory determination of DRC, as per IS 5430 and ISO 126 procedural standards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Latex Sampling] --> B[Weigh Empty Porcelain Dish M0]
    B --> C[Add 10g Latex Sample to Dish]

    E --> F[Add 2 Percent Acetic Acid Dropwise]
    F --> G{pH Reaches 4.5 to 4.8?}
    G -->|No| F
    G -->|Yes| H[Coagulation Complete - Wait 5 min]
    H --> I[Decant Serum Carefully]
    I --> J[Wash Coagulum With Water Repeatedly]
    J --> K{Wash Water Neutral to Litmus?}
    K -->|No| J
    K -->|Yes| L[Pass Coagulum Through Hand Roller]

    N --> O[Cool in Desiccator 30 min]

    P --> Q{Constant Mass Achieved?}
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Define Dry Rubber Content (DRC). How is it different from Total Solid Content (TSC)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Dry Rubber Content (DRC)** is the percentage mass of pure dry rubber obtained when a known mass of natural rubber latex is coagulated, the coagulum is washed to remove all non-rubber constituents, dried to constant mass, and weighed.
>
> $$\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{dry rubber}}}{M_{\text{latex}}} \times 100$$
>
> **Difference from TSC:** TSC is the percentage of **all solids** (rubber + proteins + sugars + lipids + ash + resins) remaining after complete evaporation of water from latex. DRC, in contrast, includes **only the rubber hydrocarbon fraction**, with all water-soluble non-rubber solids having been washed out before drying. Therefore, TSC is always **numerically greater than** DRC in good-quality latex, and their difference gives the **Non-Rubber Solid Content (NRSC)**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: List the apparatus and reagents required for the laboratory determination of DRC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The required apparatus and reagents are:

1. **Porcelain evaporating dish** (clean, dry, pre-weighed) — for the test.
2. **Analytical balance** with accuracy of ± 0.001 g.
3. **2% (w/v) acetic acid solution** — to act as the coagulating agent.
4. **Distilled water** — for dilution and washing.
5. **Glass rod, wash bottle, hand roller** — for coagulation, washing, and sheeting.
6. **Hot air oven** (regulated at 70 ± 2 °C) — for drying the rubber sheet.
7. **Desiccator** — for cooling the dried sample to room temperature before final weighing.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the laboratory procedure for the determination of Dry Rubber Content (DRC) of natural rubber latex. Mention the standard formulae, reagents, and conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** During a DRC test, the following data were recorded:
- Mass of empty dish: 38.20 g
- Mass of dish + latex sample: 48.65 g
- Mass of dish + dried rubber sheet: 42.05 g
Calculate the DRC of the latex and the total mass of dry rubber present in 1 metric tonne of this latex. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Sampling (1 mark):**
A representative latex sample of approximately **10 g** is accurately weighed ($M_1$) into a clean, dry, pre-weighed porcelain dish ($M_0$).

**Step 2 — Dilution (1 mark):**
The sample is diluted with **distilled water in a 1:1 ratio** to reduce the viscosity and ensure uniform coagulation.

**Step 3 — Coagulation (2 marks):**
**2% (w/v) acetic acid** is added **dropwise with continuous gentle stirring** using a glass rod. Coagulation is considered complete when the pH reaches the isoelectric point of natural rubber (**pH ≈ 4.5 to 4.8**), and a clear serum separates out. The mixture is left to stand for about 5 minutes.

**Step 4 — Washing (1 mark):**
The serum is decanted, and the coagulum is **washed repeatedly with distilled water** until the wash water becomes **neutral to litmus paper**, indicating removal of all soluble proteins, sugars, and salts.

**Step 5 — Sheeting and Drying (1 mark):**
The washed coagulum is passed through a smooth **hand roller** to form a uniform thin sheet (~2 mm thick). The sheet is then dried in a **hot air oven at 70 ± 2 °C for 16 hours** (or to constant mass).

**Step 6 — Weighing and Calculation (1 mark):**
The dried sheet is **cooled in a desiccator for 30 minutes** and weighed ($M_2$). Drying-cooling-weighing is repeated until **two consecutive masses agree within ± 0.001 g** (constant mass). The DRC is then calculated as:

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{M_2 - M_0}{M_1 - M_0} \times 100
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — List the given data:**

- $M_0 = 38.20\ \text{g}$
- $M_1 = 48.65\ \text{g}$
- $M_2 = 42.05\ \text{g}$

**Step 2 — Mass of latex sample:**

$$
M_{\text{latex}} = M_1 - M_0 = 48.65 - 38.20 = 10.45\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of dry rubber:**

$$
M_{\text{dry rubber}} = M_2 - M_0 = 42.05 - 38.20 = 3.85\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the DRC formula:**

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{3.85}{10.45} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division:**

$$
\frac{3.85}{10.45} = 0.36842
$$

**Step 6 — Final DRC value:**

$$
\text{DRC} = 36.84\%\ \text{(w/w)}
$$

**Step 7 — Total dry rubber in 1 metric tonne (1000 kg = 10,00,000 g):**

$$
\text{Mass of dry rubber} = 10,00,000 \times \frac{36.84}{100}
$$

$$
\text{Mass of dry rubber} = 10,000 \times 36.84 = 3,68,400\ \text{g} = 368.40\ \text{kg}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{DRC} = 36.84\%\ \text{w/w}$; Dry rubber in 1 tonne = **368.40 kg**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the significance of Dry Rubber Content in the natural rubber industry. How does DRC influence the pricing of latex and the wages of tappers? **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A latex concentrate was analysed: Mass of empty dish = 25.00 g, Mass of dish + sample = 35.20 g, Mass of dish + dry rubber = 33.10 g. A separate TSC test on the same lot gave: Mass of empty dish = 26.00 g, Mass of dish + sample = 36.20 g, Mass of dish + oven-dried residue = 33.60 g. Calculate DRC, TSC, and NRSC, and comment on the quality of the latex. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Industrial Significance of DRC (3 marks):**

DRC is the **primary quality parameter** used to assess the commercial value of natural rubber latex. It quantifies the actual amount of marketable rubber hydrocarbon per 100 g of latex, excluding water and non-rubber impurities. International standards (e.g., **ISO 126, IS 5430**) specify that centrifuged latex concentrate must have a DRC between **58% and 62%** for it to qualify as premium-grade. Below 58%, the concentrate is either over-diluted, contaminated, or sub-standard.

**2. Influence on Pricing (2 marks):**

Latex concentrate is **never priced by gross mass**; it is priced on a **DRC basis** (₹/kg of dry rubber). A buyer purchasing 5000 kg of latex at 60% DRC is essentially paying for 3000 kg of dry rubber. If the actual DRC turns out to be only 55%, the supplier must either refund the difference or top up the volume — hence the laboratory DRC certificate is a **legally binding commercial document**.

**3. Influence on Tapper Wages (2 marks):**

In many rubber estates (especially in Kerala and Tamil Nadu), tappers are paid either partly or fully on a **DRC-linked basis**. The estate manager takes a daily composite sample of the latex brought in by each tapper and estimates the DRC. Higher DRC = cleaner, fresher latex = higher wage. This incentivizes tappers to use clean cups, prevent dilution by rain, and tap at optimal early-morning hours when DRC is highest.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**DRC Calculation:**

$$
M_{\text{latex}} = 35.20 - 25.00 = 10.20\ \text{g}
$$

$$
M_{\text{dry rubber}} = 33.10 - 25.00 = 8.10\ \text{g}
$$

$$
\text{DRC (\%)} = \frac{8.10}{10.20} \times 100 = 79.41\%
$$

**TSC Calculation:**

$$
M_{\text{latex (TSC)}} = 36.20 - 26.00 = 10.20\ \text{g}
$$

$$
M_{\text{residues (TSC)}} = 33.60 - 26.00 = 7.60\ \text{g}
$$

$$
\text{TSC (\%)} = \frac{7.60}{10.20} \times 100 = 74.51\%
$$

**NRSC Calculation:**

$$
\text{NRSC (\%)} = \text{TSC} - \text{DRC} = 74.51 - 79.41 = -4.90\%
$$

**Comment on Quality (2 marks):**

> [!WARNING]
> The negative NRSC result is **physically impossible**, because the TSC test should always retain all the rubber plus the non-rubber solids, so TSC must be ≥ DRC. The anomaly indicates that the **TSC dish likely lost some dry rubber during the long oven-drying process** (rubber may have stuck to the dish, or some may have been volatilized due to over-heating). In a properly executed experiment on a high-grade latex concentrate, expected values are **DRC ≈ 60%**, **TSC ≈ 61.5%**, and **NRSC ≈ 1.5%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in DRC Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the empty dish mass** ($M_0$): Some students use $M_2/M_1$ directly instead of $(M_2-M_0)/(M_1-M_0)$ → immediate **zero mark** on the calculation.
> 2. **Confusing TSC with DRC**: TSC includes everything, DRC is only the rubber. Mixing the two costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Not reporting units**: Always state **% (w/w)** explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks if the unit is missing.
> 4. **Wrong significant figures**: Round to **2 decimal places** (e.g., 34.29%, not 34.285714%).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention acetic acid concentration**: In procedure questions, always specify **2% (w/v) acetic acid**, not just "acetic acid".
> 6. **Not mentioning constant mass**: When asked about drying, write "dried to **constant mass**" or "until two consecutive weighings agree within 0.001 g". This single phrase is worth 1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping the desiccator step**: Cooling in a desiccator is mandatory to prevent moisture re-absorption. Missing this costs a mark in procedure questions.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for DRC (DRY RUBBER CONTENT):**
>
> - **Definition:** DRC is the % w/w of pure dry rubber obtained by coagulating, washing, and drying a known mass of latex.
> - **Core Formula:** $\text{DRC (\%)} = \dfrac{M_2 - M_0}{M_1 - M_0} \times 100$
> - **Coagulant:** **2% (w/v) acetic acid**, added dropwise to diluted latex.
> - **pH at coagulation:** 4.5 to 4.8 (isoelectric point of natural rubber).
> - **Drying:** Hot air oven at **70 ± 2 °C** until **constant mass** (± 0.001 g).
> - **Cooling:** **Desiccator** for 30 minutes before final weighing.
> - **DRC vs TSC:** TSC ≥ DRC; difference = NRSC (typically 1.5–5%).
> - **Field latex DRC:** 30%–40%; **Concentrate latex DRC:** 58%–62% (ISO 126).
> - **Industry usage:** Pricing, quality control, tapper wages, process monitoring, yield estimation.
> - **Common error trap:** Never confuse dry rubber content with **dry rubber weight** (which is in grams/kg, not %).
> - **KTU favourite question style:** "Calculate DRC from given dish masses" — always shows up in either Part A (definition) or Part B (full numerical).
> - **Unit:** Always **% w/w** unless otherwise specified.
> - **Two dishes are needed** when DRC and TSC are determined simultaneously on the same lot.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Alkalinity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Total Alkalinity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2: Total Alkalinity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Total Alkalinity (TA)** is defined as the quantitative measure of a water-based system's (such as natural water, industrial process water, or latex serum) capacity to neutralize strong acids to a designated endpoint, conventionally expressed in terms of milligrams per litre (mg/L) or parts per million (ppm) of equivalent calcium carbonate ($CaCO_3$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Total alkalinity is the sum of all titratable bases present in a water sample, primarily contributed by bicarbonate ($HCO_3^-$), carbonate ($CO_3^{2-}$), and hydroxide ($OH^-$) ions."

### Constituents of Alkalinity

The principal species responsible for alkalinity, in order of typical predominance, are:

1. **Bicarbonate Ions** ($HCO_3^-$) – the dominant contributor in natural waters.
2. **Carbonate Ions** ($CO_3^{2-}$) – present at pH > 8.3.
3. **Hydroxide Ions** ($OH^-$) – present at pH > 10.
4. **Minor contributors**: Borates, silicates, phosphates, and dissolved ammonia.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The "Acid Buffer Bank"**
> Imagine alkalinity as a **financial savings account** dedicated to neutralizing acids. When you add acid to water, the bicarbonate and carbonate ions "spend" themselves to neutralize the acid, just like a savings account gets debited. The **higher the alkalinity, the larger the buffer bank** — meaning the water can absorb more acid before its pH drops dramatically.
>
> In latex technology, alkalinity is critical because natural latex is stabilized at **pH 9–11** using ammonia (a base). If the alkalinity is too low, the latex coagulates prematurely; if too high, it can cause skin irritation in dipped products.

### Standard Metrics and Conventions

- **Unit of Expression**: mg/L as $CaCO_3$ (or ppm as $CaCO_3$).
- **Conversion Factor for $CaCO_3$ Equivalence**: 1 meq/L of any species = **50 mg/L as $CaCO_3$**.
- **Standard Titrant**: $0.02 \text{ N } H_2SO_4$ or $0.1 \text{ N } HCl$.
- **Standard Indicators**:
  - **Phenolphthalein** (endpoint pH ≈ **8.3**)
  - **Methyl Orange** (endpoint pH ≈ **4.5**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The measurement of alkalinity follows a **two-stage acid-base titration** methodology, exploiting the differential response of carbonate species to pH changes:

**Stage 1 — Phenolphthalein Alkalinity (P):**
- Titrant ($H^+$) reacts first with $OH^-$ and the **first protonation** of $CO_3^{2-}$ to form $HCO_3^-$.
- Endpoint: pH 8.3 (when all $CO_3^{2-}$ has been converted to $HCO_3^-$).
- Mathematical expression:
$$P = \frac{V_p \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample}} \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3$$

**Stage 2 — Total Alkalinity (T):**
- Titration continues to convert $HCO_3^-$ to $H_2CO_3$ (and then to $CO_2 + H_2O$).
- Endpoint: pH 4.5 (using methyl orange indicator).
- Mathematical expression:
$$T = \frac{V_t \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample}} \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3$$

where $V_p$ and $V_t$ are the volumes of titrant used up to the phenolphthalein and methyl orange endpoints, respectively.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Two Endpoints

| pH Region | Dominant Species | Titration Behavior |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| pH > 12.3 | $OH^-$ and $CO_3^{2-}$ | $OH^-$ neutralized first |
| pH 8.3 – 12.3 | $CO_3^{2-}$ dominant | $CO_3^{2-}$ being converted to $HCO_3^-$ |
| pH 4.5 – 8.3 | $HCO_3^-$ dominant | $HCO_3^-$ being converted to $H_2CO_3$ |
| pH < 4.5 | $H_2CO_3 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O$ | All alkalinity exhausted |

### 2.3 Carbonate System Reactions (Sequential)

The titration is governed by three critical equilibrium reactions:

$$CO_3^{2-} + H^+ \rightarrow HCO_3^-$$

$$HCO_3^- + H^+ \rightarrow H_2CO_3$$

$$H_2CO_3 \rightarrow H_2O + CO_2 \uparrow$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** In latex compounding, controlling alkalinity ensures colloidal stability, prevents premature coagulation, and maintains the **zeta potential** of rubber particles in the aqueous phase.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---------------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Phenolphthalein Alkalinity (P) | $P = \dfrac{V_p \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L as $CaCO_3$ | Detects $OH^-$ and half of $CO_3^{2-}$ |
| Total Alkalinity (T) | $T = \dfrac{V_t \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L as $CaCO_3$ | Measures all titratable bases |
| Bicarbonate Alkalinity | $B = T - 2P$ (when $P < T/2$) | mg/L as $CaCO_3$ | Common case in natural water |
| Carbonate Alkalinity | $C = 2P$ (when $P < T/2$) | mg/L as $CaCO_3$ | Contribution from $CO_3^{2-}$ |
| Hydroxide Alkalinity | $H = 2P - T$ (when $P > T/2$) | mg/L as $CaCO_3$ | Rare in natural water, common in treated latex |
| Equivalent Weight of $CaCO_3$ | $\dfrac{100}{2} = 50$ | g/eq | Standard reference |
| $CaCO_3$ Equivalence | $1 \text{ meq/L} = 50 \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3$ | — | Universal conversion factor |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Treatment Plants:** Determines the dosage of lime ($Ca(OH)_2$) or soda ash ($Na_2CO_3$) required for water softening.
- **Latex Industry:** Critical parameter in **centrifuged latex concentrate** production, where ammonia is the standard alkali for preservation (typically 0.6–0.8% on total weight).
- **Boiler Feed Water:** High alkalinity causes scaling and foaming; low alkalinity causes corrosion.
- **Wastewater Analysis:** Used to assess the buffering capacity of effluents.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why 50,000 appears in the Formula

We derive the universal alkalinity formula from first principles. The objective is to express alkalinity in **mg/L as $CaCO_3$**.

**Given:**
- Normality of titrant acid: $N$ (eq/L)
- Volume of titrant: $V_t$ (mL)
- Sample volume: $V_{sample}$ (mL)
- Equivalent weight of $CaCO_3$: **50 g/eq**

**Step 1: Calculate equivalents of acid consumed.**

$$\text{Equivalents of acid} = \frac{V_t \text{ (mL)} \times N \text{ (eq/L)}}{1000 \text{ (mL/L)}}$$

**Step 2: Equate to equivalents of alkalinity in the sample.**

$$\text{Equivalents of alkalinity} = \frac{V_t \times N}{1000} \text{ eq (in } V_{sample} \text{ mL)}$$

**Step 3: Convert equivalents to grams of $CaCO_3$ using equivalent weight.**

$$\text{Mass of } CaCO_3 = \frac{V_t \times N}{1000} \times 50 \text{ grams}$$

**Step 4: Express per litre of sample.**

$$\text{Alkalinity per litre} = \frac{V_t \times N \times 50}{1000} \times \frac{1000}{V_{sample}} \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 5: Convert grams to milligrams (×1000).**

$$\text{Alkalinity (mg/L)} = \frac{V_t \times N \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{sample}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{V_t \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample}} \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Style)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Problem]**
> A 100 mL water sample requires 15.0 mL of $0.02 \text{ N } H_2SO_4$ to reach the phenolphthalein endpoint, and a total of 28.5 mL to reach the methyl orange endpoint. Calculate: (i) P, (ii) T, and (iii) the concentrations of $OH^-$, $CO_3^{2-}$, and $HCO_3^-$ in mg/L as $CaCO_3$.

**Given Data:**
- $V_{sample} = 100$ mL
- $N = 0.02$ eq/L
- $V_p = 15.0$ mL
- $V_t = 28.5$ mL

---

**Part (i): Phenolphthalein Alkalinity (P)**

$$P = \frac{V_p \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample}}$$

$$P = \frac{15.0 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{100}$$

$$P = \frac{15{,}000}{100} = 150 \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3$$

---

**Part (ii): Total Alkalinity (T)**

$$T = \frac{V_t \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{sample}}$$

$$T = \frac{28.5 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000}{100}$$

$$T = \frac{28{,}500}{100} = 285 \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3$$

---

**Part (iii): Identification of Species using the $P$ vs $T$ Rule**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:**
> - If $P = 0$: Only $HCO_3^-$ present.
> - If $P < T/2$: $OH^-$ = 0; $CO_3^{2-} = 2P$; $HCO_3^- = T - 2P$.
> - If $P = T/2$: Only $CO_3^{2-}$ present; $HCO_3^-$ = 0.
> - If $P > T/2$: $HCO_3^-$ = 0; $OH^- = 2P - T$; $CO_3^{2-} = 2(T - P)$.

**Apply the Rule:**

$$\frac{T}{2} = \frac{285}{2} = 142.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$P = 150 > 142.5 = T/2$$

Since $P > T/2$, we are in the **mixed hydroxide-carbonate regime**.

---

**Calculate $CO_3^{2-}$ Alkalinity:**

$$CO_3^{2-} = 2 \times (T - P)$$

$$CO_3^{2-} = 2 \times (285 - 150) = 2 \times 135 = 270 \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3$$

---

**Calculate $OH^-$ Alkalinity:**

$$OH^- = 2P - T$$

$$OH^- = 2 \times 150 - 285 = 300 - 285 = 15 \text{ mg/L as } CaCO_3$$

---

**Calculate $HCO_3^-$ Alkalinity:**

$$HCO_3^- = 0 \text{ (since } P > T/2\text{)}$$

---

**Verification (Mass Balance):**

$$OH^- + CO_3^{2-} + HCO_3^- = 15 + 270 + 0 = 285 \text{ mg/L} = T \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Conversion of mg/L as $CaCO_3$ to Actual Species Concentration

To express $CO_3^{2-}$ in mg/L as the actual ion (not as $CaCO_3$):

$$\text{Equivalent weight of } CO_3^{2-} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ g/eq}$$

$$[CO_3^{2-}] \text{ in mg/L} = 270 \times \frac{30}{50} = 270 \times 0.6 = 162 \text{ mg/L}$$

Similarly for $OH^-$ (eq. wt. = 17 g/eq):

$$[OH^-] \text{ in mg/L} = 15 \times \frac{17}{50} = 15 \times 0.34 = 5.1 \text{ mg/L}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Two-Stage Titration Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Water Sample 100 mL] --> B[Add 2-3 drops of Phenolphthalein Indicator]
    B --> C{Solution turns Pink?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Titrate with 0.02 N H2SO4 until Pink color disappears]
    C -- No --> E[Note Vp = 0; P Alkalinity = 0]
    D --> F[Record Volume Vp in mL]
    F --> G[Add 2-3 drops of Methyl Orange Indicator]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Continue Titration until Yellow turns to Faint Red Orange]
    H --> I[Record Total Volume Vt in mL]
    I --> J[Calculate P and T using formulas]
    J --> K{P greater than T by 2?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Use Mixed OH and CO3 formulas]
    K -- No --> M{P equals T by 2?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Only CO3 present]
    M -- No --> O{P less than T by 2?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Use Bicarbonate formulas]
    O -- No --> Q[Recheck titration data]
    L --> R[Report Final Alkalinity Species]
    N --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> S[Repeat Experiment]
    S --> A
```

```mermaid
    D -->|Hydroxide Region pH greater than 12| E[OH minus Hydroxide]

```

```mermaid

        RM2 --> RM3[Quality Control Lab]
    end
```
