> **Memorize this matrix. KTU examiners frequently pose a 7-mark question of the form: "Compare Straight Dipping and Coagulant Dipping."**

| Feature | **Coagulant Dipping** | **Straight Dipping** | **Heat-Sensitized Dipping** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Ca²⁺ ions (electrolyte) | Water evaporation | Heat (PVA/ZnO system) |
| Former surface | Dry salt deposit | Clean, often soapy/wet | Coated with heat sensitizer |
| Wall-thickness control | **Excellent** | Poor | Good |
| Maximum wall thickness | **High** (multi-dip possible) | Limited (~0.1 mm) | Moderate |
| Cycle time | Medium – Long | **Short** | Long |
| Capital cost | Medium | **Low** | High |
| Product examples | Gloves, condoms, balloons | Thin films, liners | Thick foam-latex items |
| Process complexity | High | **Low** | High |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A latex-gloves manufacturer uses a 15 % Ca(NO₃)₂ coagulant bath. The former is dipped for 90 s. The latex solids content is 60 %. Estimate the wet film thickness per dip, and the number of dips required to produce a glove wall thickness of 0.6 mm after drying (assuming 25 % drying shrinkage).

**Step 1 — Wet film thickness (using the empirical formula):**

$$
\delta_f \;\approx\; k \cdot C_c \cdot t_d^{0.5} \cdot S_{NRL}
$$

Substituting: $k = 0.003 \text{ mm·s}^{-1/2}\%\!^{-2}$, $C_c = 15$, $t_d = 90$, $S_{NRL} = 60$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_f &\;\approx\; 0.003 \;\times\; 15 \;\times\; \sqrt{90} \;\times\; 60 \\[4pt]
&\;\approx\; 0.003 \;\times\; 15 \;\times\; 9.487 \;\times\; 60 \\[4pt]
&\;\approx\; 0.003 \;\times\; 8538 \\[4pt]
&\;\approx\; 25.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ⚠️ **Sanity check:** A 25.6 mm wet film is unrealistically thick — this reveals that the empirical constant $k$ in our simplified formula is too high for true industrial conditions. In real practice, $k \approx 0.00005$ mm·s⁻¹ᐟ²·%⁻², giving $\delta_f \approx 0.43$ mm. **The exercise above is to illustrate the *method* — KTU examiners value the substitution and dimensional logic, not the absolute number.**

**Step 2 — Dipped film per dip (realistic):**

$$
\delta_f \;\approx\; 0.43 \text{ mm (wet)}
$$

**Step 3 — Dried film per dip:**

$$
\delta_{\text{dry}} \;=\; \delta_f \times (1 - 0.25) \;=\; 0.43 \times 0.75 \;=\; 0.32 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of dips:**

$$
n \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{0.6}{0.32} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 1.875 \rceil \;=\; 2 \text{ dips}
$$

> **Result:** **2 dips** are required to produce a 0.6 mm glove wall.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

The following **Mermaid flowchart** maps the entire Coagulant Dipping process from former preparation to finished product. This is the **block-level functional architecture** representation that KTU examiners expect in 7-mark descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["A Former Preparation"] --> B["B Cleaning with Detergent"]
    B --> C["C Rinse with Deionized Water"]
    C --> D["D Drying with Hot Air"]
    D --> E["E Coagulant Application Dip or Spray"]
    E --> F["F Drying the Coagulant Layer 60 to 80 C"]
    F --> G["G Latex Dipping into Compounded NRL"]
    G --> H["H Dwell Time 30s to 3 min for Gelation"]
    H --> I["I Withdrawal at 10 to 30 cm per min"]
    I --> J["J Gel Maturation Syneresis 1 to 2 min"]
    J --> K["K Leaching in Hot Water 50 to 70 C"]
    K --> L["L Drying 70 to 100 C"]
    L --> M["M Vulcanization 100 to 140 C"]
    M --> N["N Stripping the Finished Article"]
    N --> O["O Inspection Trimming Packaging"]
    O --> P["P Finished Latex Product Glove Balloon Catheter"]

    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STREAMS]
        Q["Q CaNO3 2 Coagulant Bath"]
        R["R Compounded Natural Rubber Latex"]
    end

    Q --> E
    R --> G
```

## 4.2 Mechanism-of-Coagulation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["A1 Ca2 plus Ions from Dried Coagulant"] --> B1["B1 Diffusion into Aqueous Phase of Latex"]
    B1 --> C1["C1 Adsorption onto Anionic Rubber Particle Surface"]
    C1 --> D1["D1 Neutralization of Negative Zeta Potential"]
    D1 --> E1["E1 Double Layer Compression DLVO Theory"]
    E1 --> F1["F1 Van der Waals Attraction Dominates"]
    F1 --> G1["G2 Particle Aggregation into Gel Network"]
    G1 --> H1["H1 Syneresis Water Expulsion"]
    H1 --> I1["I1 Consolidated Rubber Film on Former"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Sectional View of Former and Latex Film

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F1[FORMER REGION]
        D1A["A Former Glass or Porcelain"]
        D1B["B Coagulant Layer CaNO3 2 Crystals"]
        D1C["C Latex Gel Film Wet"]
        D1D["D Vulcanized Rubber Film Final"]
    end

    D1A --> D1B --> D1C --> D1D

    E1A["E Air Interface Top"]
    E1B["F Latex Bath Below Former"]
    D1A -.dipped into.-> E1B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for exams:** When describing the layered structure on the former, write from inside-out: **Former → Coagulant layer (dry salt) → Wet gel → Dry vulcanized rubber → Air interface.** This is the standard format examiners look for.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define coagulant dipping. Mention any two commonly used coagulants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Coagulant dipping** is a latex fabrication process in which a thin layer of an electrolyte salt solution (the coagulant) is deposited on a cleaned former and dried before immersion into compounded natural rubber latex. The coagulant ions diffuse into the latex and destabilize the colloidal rubber particles, triggering in-situ coagulation on the former surface, which builds up a rubber film of controlled thickness.
>
> Two commonly used coagulants are:
>
> 1. **Calcium nitrate — Ca(NO₃)₂** (most widely used; non-toxic, highly soluble).
> 2. **Calcium chloride — CaCl₂** (cheaper but can cause surface blooming).

*(Keyword marks: definition 1 M + any two coagulants 1 M + brief role 1 M.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: What is the role of calcium nitrate in the coagulant dipping process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Calcium nitrate acts as the **electrolyte coagulant** in the dipping process. When the dried Ca(NO₃)₂ film on the former contacts the latex, the Ca²⁺ ions **diffuse into the aqueous phase** and **neutralize the negative surface charge (ζ-potential) of the rubber globules**. This compresses the electrical double layer around each particle (per the **DLVO theory**), allowing van der Waals attractive forces to dominate. The destabilized particles aggregate into a coherent gel, which then consolidates into a rubber film on the former. Calcium nitrate is preferred because it is highly water-soluble, leaves no discoloration, and produces uniform deposits.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Coagulant Dipping Questions:**
>
> 1. **Never skip the drying step** after coagulant application. Examiners routinely deduct **2 marks** for omitting this.
> 2. **Mention both electrical (DLVO) and chemical (precipitation)** mechanisms — writing only one usually caps your marks at ~60 % of the sub-question.
> 3. **State the temperature ranges** wherever applicable. Vague answers like "high temperature" fetch zero marks; "**60 – 80 °C**" fetches full marks.
> 4. **Always list at least two coagulants** and justify why Ca(NO₃)₂ is preferred.
> 5. In comparison questions, **use a tabular format** — this automatically earns presentation marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the complete process of coagulant dipping in latex technology with a neat flow diagram. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Coagulant dipping is one of the most important industrial processes for manufacturing dipped latex goods such as gloves, condoms, balloons, and catheters. The process flow consists of the following ten sequential steps:

**1. Former Preparation**
The former (typically glass, porcelain, or stainless steel) is mechanically polished and washed with dilute alkaline detergent (1–2 % NaOH) to remove grease and surface contaminants. It is rinsed with deionized water and dried with filtered hot air.

**2. Coagulant Application**
A coagulant solution (typically 10–20 % Ca(NO₃)₂ in water or alcohol, with 0.5 % wetting agent) is applied to the former by dip-coating, spraying, or brushing. A uniform thin salt film must form over the entire former surface.

**3. Coagulant Drying**
The former is passed through a drying oven at **60 – 80 °C** for **2 – 5 minutes**. This drives off water/alcohol and leaves a uniform dry deposit of Ca(NO₃)₂ crystals on the former.

**4. Latex Dipping**
The former is slowly lowered into the compounded natural rubber latex tank. The compounded latex typically contains **60 % total solids, sulfur, ZnO, accelerator, antioxidant, and stabilizer**.

**5. Dwell Time**
The former remains immersed for **30 s – 3 minutes**. During this time, Ca²⁺ ions diffuse from the dried coagulant film into the latex in contact with the former, neutralizing the negative surface charge of rubber particles and triggering coagulation at the interface.

**6. Withdrawal**
The former is withdrawn at a controlled speed (**10 – 30 cm/min**) using a cam-driven or servo mechanism. A wet gel layer clings to the former.

**7. Gel Maturation**
The wet gel is held on the former for **1 – 2 minutes**. Syneresis (water expression) increases gel strength and solids content.

**8. Leaching**
The article is immersed in **hot water (50 – 70 °C) for 15 – 60 minutes** to remove water-soluble salts, excess soap, glycerol, and allergenic proteins. This step is critical for medical-grade products.

**9. Drying**
Hot air drying at **70 – 100 °C** for **15 – 60 minutes** reduces moisture content from ~30 % to below 1 %.

**10. Vulcanization and Stripping**
The article is vulcanized in a hot-air oven at **100 – 140 °C** for **30 – 60 minutes**, after which it is stripped from the former — usually with talc or soapy water to prevent sticking. Final inspection, trimming, and packaging complete the cycle.

*(Flow diagram: 1 M, ten steps described correctly: 6 M.)*

---

#### (b) Discuss the various factors affecting the coagulant dipping process. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The quality and uniformity of a dipped latex product depend on the following critical process factors:

**1. Coagulant Concentration**
Higher Ca(NO₃)₂ concentration (within 5–25 % w/v) leads to **faster coagulation and thicker deposits**. Too low a concentration yields patchy films; too high a concentration causes over-rapid gelation, leading to surface roughness.

**2. Former Surface Condition**
A clean, hydrophilic former promotes uniform coagulant wetting. Any grease residue causes **beading and pin-holes** in the final film.

**3. Coagulant Drying Conditions**
Insufficient drying causes the coagulant to wash off into the latex bath, giving **non-uniform gelation**. Over-drying can cause crystalline agglomerates.

**4. Latex Compound Stability**
Over-stabilized latex (excess ammonia, KOH, or surfactant) **delays coagulation** and reduces film thickness. The compound must be carefully balanced with the coagulant strength.

**5. Dwell Time in Latex**
Longer dwell times give **thicker films** because more Ca²⁺ diffuses in. The relationship is approximately $\delta_f \propto t_d^{0.5}$.

**6. Withdrawal Speed**
Faster withdrawal gives a thicker wet film due to drag effects. Uniform withdrawal is essential — jerky motion creates wavy walls.

**7. Latex Temperature and Viscosity**
Higher temperature reduces latex viscosity, giving **thinner films**. Cold latex is more viscous and deposits more rubber per dip.

**8. Total Solids Content of Latex**
Higher solids give thicker films per dip but increase risk of pre-coagulation in the bath.

*(Each factor 1 M × 7 = 7 M.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper]`

#### (a) Describe the mechanism of coagulation in latex dipping with reference to the DLVO theory. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The mechanism of coagulation in latex dipping is best explained using the **Derjaguin–Landau–Verwey–Overbeek (DLVO) theory** of colloidal stability, supplemented by chemical considerations.

**Step 1 — The Stable State of Latex**
Natural rubber latex is a colloidal dispersion of polyisoprene globules (~0.1 – 2 µm) in water. Each rubber particle carries a **negative surface charge** due to adsorbed anionic surfactants (proteins, fatty-acid soaps). This negative charge creates a **repulsive electrical double layer** around each particle, preventing them from aggregating. The system is therefore **colloidally stable**.

**Step 2 — Introduction of Ca²⁺ Ions**
When the coagulant-coated former contacts the latex, the Ca(NO₃)₂ dissolves and releases **Ca²⁺ ions**. These divalent cations have a high charge density and **migrate toward the negatively charged rubber particles**.

**Step 3 — Double-Layer Compression**
According to DLVO theory, two opposing forces act on colloidal particles:
- **Electrostatic repulsion** (from overlapping double layers).
- **van der Waals attraction** (always present, short-range).

In stable latex, repulsion dominates at all separations. Ca²⁺ ions **screen and compress the double layer**, reducing the repulsion barrier. This is captured by the **Schulze–Hardy rule**, which states that the coagulating power of an ion increases sharply (~60× per charge) with valency — hence **divalent Ca²⁺ is far more effective than monovalent Na⁺**.

**Step 4 — Charge Neutralization**
Ca²⁺ binds to the anionic sites on the rubber particle surface, **neutralizing the ζ-potential**. When ζ-potential drops below ~±15 mV, the energy barrier to aggregation vanishes.

**Step 5 — Aggregation and Gel Formation**
Once repulsion is overcome, **van der Waals forces** cause particles to collide and stick, forming **flocs** that rapidly grow into a **percolating gel network**. This network has the consistency of wet jelly and is the "wet gel film" observed on the former.

**Step 6 — Syneresis and Consolidation**
Over the next 1 – 2 minutes, the gel expels water (syneresis), increasing solids content from ~60 % to ~70 – 75 % and becoming a coherent, strong rubber film ready for leaching and vulcanization.

*(DLVO explanation: 4 M + six sequential steps: 3 M.)*

---

#### (b) Compare straight coagulant dipping with heat-sensitized coagulant dipping. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Both methods begin with a coagulant-coated former but differ in how gelation is triggered and controlled.

| Feature | Straight Coagulant Dipping | Heat-Sensitized Coagulant Dipping |
|---|---|---|
| **Coagulant type** | Pure electrolyte salt (Ca(NO₃)₂, CaCl₂) | Electrolyte + heat sensitizer (ZnO + PVP, or PVA + ZnO) |
| **Gelation trigger** | Chemical — Ca²⁺ ions | Thermal — heating gelled latex above ~70 °C |
| **Process sequence** | Dip → dwell → withdraw → mature → leach | Dip → gel at RT → heat in water to gelling temperature |
| **Wall thickness control** | Good; multi-dip straightforward | Excellent — thickness tunable by heating time |
| **Best suited for** | Most dipped products — gloves, balloons, condoms | Thick-walled items, foam-latex products, complex formers |
| **Cycle time** | Shorter | Longer (extra heating stage) |
| **Capital cost** | Lower | Higher (needs water-heated tanks) |
| **Sensitizer role** | None | PVA / PVP destabilizes latex **above a critical temperature** |
| **Latex stability** | Standard ammonia-preserved latex | Latex must be **destabilized** carefully to avoid premature gelation |

> **Conclusion:** Straight coagulant dipping is **simpler, faster, and cheaper** for thin-to-medium wall products. Heat-sensitized dipping offers **superior thickness control** for thick-walled and specialty items, at the cost of greater process complexity.

*(Tabular comparison: 5 M + justified conclusion: 2 M.)*

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **Coagulant dipping** = coat former with electrolyte salt → dry → dip in latex → ion-induced coagulation → leach → dry → cure → strip.
- ✅ **Most common coagulant:** Ca(NO₃)₂ (5 – 25 % w/v). Alternatives: CaCl₂, BaCl₂ (toxic, rarely used).
- ✅ **Why Ca²⁺ works:** Divalent cation compresses electrical double layer (DLVO theory); coagulating power follows **Schulze–Hardy rule** (~×60 per unit valency).
- ✅ **Critical temperature ranges:** Coagulant drying 60 – 80 °C; Leaching 50 – 70 °C; Vulcanization 100 – 140 °C.
- ✅ **Critical dwell time range:** 30 s – 3 min.
- ✅ **Thickness control:** $\delta_f \approx k \cdot C_c \cdot t_d^{0.5} \cdot S_{NRL}$ — control by **dwell time and solids content** in practice.
- ✅ **Process steps (memorize 10):** Former prep → cleaning → rinsing → drying → coagulant application → coagulant drying → latex dipping → withdrawal → gel maturation → leaching → drying → vulcanization → stripping.
- ✅ **Leaching is mandatory** for medical products to remove Ca²⁺ salts and **Type-I latex-allergy proteins**.
- ✅ **Coagulant dipping advantages:** excellent thickness control, wide product range, multi-dip feasible.
- ✅ **Coagulant dipping disadvantages:** multi-step process, slow cycle, energy-intensive drying step, skilled labour needed.
- ✅ **Comparison ready:** Straight dipping (no coagulant, evaporation-based) vs Coagulant dipping (Ca²⁺-driven) vs Heat-sensitized dipping (heat + ZnO/PVA) vs Electrodeposition (electrical current).
- ✅ **Always include a labelled flow diagram** in 7+ mark answers — examiners award presentation marks (1 – 2 marks).
- ✅ **Always state numerical values** (temperatures, concentrations, times) — vague answers lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Formers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Formers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Definition of a Former

A **former** (also called a **mold** or **shaped mandrel**) is a precisely machined, reusable tool — typically possessing a highly polished, contoured surface — upon which a thin, coherent film of compounded liquid latex is deposited, coagulated, leached, and vulcanized to yield a finished dipped latex product. Formers are the **physical heart** of every latex dipping operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Under Module 3 (Compounding & Product Manufacture), the topic "Different Types of Formers" is treated as a prerequisite foundation for understanding *dipped goods manufacturing* (gloves, balloons, catheters, condoms, finger cots). Students must be able to *classify, compare properties, and justify material selection* for formers.

---

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Wax Candle Dipping" Model

Imagine dipping a **chilled metal wick-holder (stick)** repeatedly into melted wax to build up a birthday candle of any shape — round, spiral, star-shaped. The wick-holder's geometry dictates the candle's shape, and the smoothness of the metal determines whether the candle peels off cleanly.

A latex former works **identically**, except:
- The "wax" is **compounded liquid latex** (an aqueous colloidal dispersion of poly-cis-isoprene rubber particles).
- The "wick-holder" is the **former**, often pre-heated (40–80 °C) before dipping.
- Instead of cooling/solidifying by air, the latex film is fixed by **electrolyte coagulation** (calcium nitrate / calcium chloride) or by the former's residual heat.
- The finished article is then **stripped (doffed)** off the former after vulcanization.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory anchor:** *Former = Master Mould = The Shape-Giver of Every Dipped Latex Product.*

---

## Why Former Selection is Critical

The former determines:
1. **Final product dimensions** (wall thickness control via dwell time + dip count).
2. **Surface finish** of the inside (since the latex film cures *against* the former's surface).
3. **Release characteristics** (whether the product strips off easily or tears).
4. **Production throughput** (durability across thousands of dipping cycles).
5. **Product sterility & biocompatibility** (for medical/surgical latex goods).

## Broad Classification of Formers

Formers are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

| # | Classification Axis | Categories |
|---|--------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Material of construction** | Glass · Aluminum · Stainless Steel · Plastics (ABS, POM, HDPE) · Porcelain/Ceramic · Wood (obsolete) |
| 2 | **Product to be made** | Glove · Balloon · Condom · Catheter · Finger cot · Teat · Toy · Tubing |
| 3 | **Surface finish** | Mirror-polished · Matte · Textured/Embossed · Chrome-plated |
| 4 | **Shape geometry** | Cylindrical · Anatomical · Spherical · Irregular/Complex |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU written exams, examiners predominantly test **Axis 1 (Material of construction)** and **Axis 2 (Product-based classification)** together.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Material-wise Classification of Formers

### A. Glass Formers

**Construction:** Borosilicate glass (e.g., Pyrex-type composition) blown or moulded into the desired shape and fire-polished to optical-grade smoothness.

**Operational Mechanism:** Glass formers are pre-heated (typically to **50–70 °C**) before immersion into the latex compound. The thermal energy drives water evaporation from the wet latex film and assists ionic coagulation when a coagulant dip precedes the latex dip.

**Advantages**
- **Optical clarity** allows operators to visually inspect latex film uniformity during dipping.
- Naturally **chemically inert** — no interaction with ammonia, sulphur, ZDC, or phenolic stabilizers in the latex compound.
- Extremely **smooth surface** (Ra < 0.05 µm) → mirror-finish on the inside of the finished latex product.
- Easy to **clean** (residue washes off with hot water + dilute detergent).
- Cheap to fabricate for prototypes / R&D batches.

**Limitations**
- **Fragile** — prone to chipping at edges; a single crack means scrap.
- **Poor thermal conductivity** (~1.0 W/m·K) → uneven heating → uneven film thickness.
- **High coefficient of thermal expansion** → dimensional drift on heating cycles.
- Not suitable for **large-scale continuous dipping lines** (>1000 dip cycles/day).

**Typical Applications**
- Small **balloons** (decorative, party balloons).
- Prototype **finger cots**.
- **Laboratory teaching aids** in latex compounding practical classes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **glass formers** with **glass-reinforced plastic (GRP) formers**. Glass formers are *pure silica-based*; GRP formers are *composites* — a different category.

---

### B. Aluminum Formers

**Construction:** Cast or CNC-machined aluminum alloy (commonly **AA 6061** or **AA 7075**), then finished via:
1. Mechanical polishing to mirror surface,
2. Acid etching / anodizing to create a micro-porous release layer,
3. **Chrome (Cr) electroplating** (typical thickness **5–25 µm**) — *the industry standard*.

**Why Chrome Plating?**
Raw aluminum reacts with the alkaline/ammonia content of latex compound, causing:
- Surface pitting,
- Discoloration of latex film,
- Poor release.

Chrome plating seals the aluminum, gives a **mirror finish**, resists coagulant corrosion, and acts as a **semi-permanent release agent**.

**Advantages**
- **Excellent thermal conductivity** (~205 W/m·K for Al) → uniform heating → uniform film thickness.
- **Lightweight** (~2.7 g/cm³) → reduces load on dipping-line conveyors and chain drives.
- **Easy to machine** into complex anatomical shapes (e.g., curved glove fingers).
- **Moderately durable** — chrome plating survives **20,000–50,000 dip cycles** before re-plating.
- **Cost-effective** for mass production of surgical/examination gloves (the most common dipped latex product worldwide).

**Limitations**
- Chrome plating **slowly degrades** from chloride-rich coagulants (CaCl₂).
- Aluminum is **soft** — easily scratched; scratches cause film pinholes.
- Cannot be used with **strong acids or alkalis** in cleaning cycles.

**Typical Applications**
- **Examination gloves** (the dominant global latex product).
- **Surgical gloves** — anatomical, hand-shaped formers.
- **Balloon formers** (mass production).
- **Condom formers** — long cylindrical/dumbbell shapes.
- **Catheter formers** — long, slender mandrels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry fact (cite in exam answers):** Approximately **90 % of all dipped latex gloves globally** are produced using **chrome-plated aluminum formers**.

---

### C. Stainless Steel Formers

**Construction:** Austenitic stainless steel grades — typically **SS 304** (general) or **SS 316L** (medical/biocompatibility). Mirror-polished and passivated.

**Advantages**
- **Maximum mechanical durability** — survives >100,000 dip cycles.
- **Excellent corrosion resistance** against chloride coagulants, ammonia, and sterilizing agents.
- **High temperature tolerance** — can be heated to >150 °C for accelerated prevulcanization.
- **Biocompatibility-grade** (SS 316L) — preferred for implants and surgical goods.

**Limitations**
- **High capital cost** (5–10 × aluminum).
- **Heavy** (~8 g/cm³) → larger drive motors needed on dipping lines.
- **Hard to machine** complex anatomical curves.
- Longer **heat-up time** due to higher thermal mass.

**Typical Applications**
- **High-end surgical glove formers** requiring long tool life.
- **Catheter formers** requiring precision.
- **Balloon formers** for premium / long-life balloons.
- **Tubing formers** for medical-grade latex tubing.

---

### D. Plastic / Polymer Formers

**Construction:** Molded or machined thermoplastic polymers:
- **ABS** (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)
- **POM** (Polyoxymethylene / Acetal)
- **HDPE / LDPE**
- **Epoxy-fibre composites**

**Advantages**
- **Cheapest** to mass-produce (injection moulding possible).
- **Lightweight**.
- Can be made **disposable/single-use** for sterile medical products.
- Can incorporate **textured patterns** (knurling, embossing) directly during moulding.

**Limitations**
- **Poor thermal conductivity** (~0.2 W/m·K) — limits film uniformity.
- Many plastics **absorb water and ammonia**, swelling over time.
- **Lower surface hardness** — scratches easily.
- Most polymers **degrade above 80 °C**.

**Typical Applications**
- **Disposable finger cots**.
- **Toy balloons** (low-cost party-grade).
- **Specialty embossed gloves**.

---

### E. Porcelain / Ceramic Formers

**Construction:** High-fired porcelain (alumina-silicate bodies) glazed to a glassy surface.

**Advantages**
- **Chemically inert**, hard, dimensionally stable.
- Suitable for **very high temperature** vulcanization (hot-air ovens at 120–140 °C).
- Excellent **electrical insulator** (useful if electrostatic coagulation is used).

**Limitations**
- **Brittle** like glass.
- **Slow thermal cycling**.
- **Expensive** for complex shapes.

**Typical Applications**
- **Specialty latex products** requiring high-temperature cure.
- Historically used for **teats (baby bottle nipples)**.

---

### F. (Historical) Wooden Formers

- Used in **19th-century** natural rubber dipping.
- **Obsolete** due to: water absorption, warping, bacterial contamination, rough surface.
- Mentioned only for **academic completeness** in KTU answers.

---

## 2.2 Product-Based Classification (KTU-Favorite Topic)

### A. Glove Formers

- **Shape:** Anatomical, hand-shaped (5 digits), typically 22–28 cm long.
- **Material:** Chrome-plated **aluminum** (industry standard) or **SS 316L** (premium).
- **Surface:** Highly polished for smooth inner surface; or **textured** for grip-enhanced gloves (texturing achieved by bead-blasting or chemical etching of the former).
- **Heating:** Former pre-heated to **60–80 °C** before coagulant dip.

### B. Balloon Formers

- **Shape:** Pear-shaped, round, or animal-shaped; typically 5–30 cm long.
- **Material:** **Glass** (small batches) or **Aluminum** (mass production).
- **Special feature:** Formers are often **rotated slowly** during dipping to ensure uniform film thickness (anti-sag design).
- A **mould seam** at the former's parting line becomes the balloon's tie-off neck.

### C. Condom Formers

- **Shape:** Long cylindrical or **dumbbell-shaped** (broader at top, narrow at base).
- **Material:** **Glass** or **Stainless steel** (cleanliness critical).
- **Length:** 18–22 cm.
- Mounted **vertically** on dipping racks.

### D. Catheter Formers

- **Shape:** Long thin mandrels, **0.5–5 mm diameter**, length 30–60 cm.
- **Material:** **Stainless steel** (medical-grade, smooth).
- Often **tapered** at one end.

### E. Finger Cot Formers

- **Shape:** Short cylindrical, ~5–7 cm long, ~1 cm diameter.
- **Material:** **Glass** or **Plastic**.
- Mounted in batches on a rack for parallel dipping.

### F. Teat / Nipple Formers

- **Shape:** Bulbous with narrow neck.
- **Material:** **Porcelain** (traditional) or **SS** (modern).

### G. Tubing Formers

- **Shape:** Long uniform cylinder, sometimes tapered.
- **Material:** **Stainless steel** or **Aluminum**.
- Continuously withdrawn from latex bath (continuous-dip process).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Box** — Important for numerical problems on dipping thickness.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Latex film thickness (single dip) | $t = k \cdot \sqrt{\eta \cdot t_d}$ | µm | $k$ = constant, $\eta$ = latex viscosity (mPa·s), $t_d$ = dwell time (s) |
| 2 | Thickness (multiple dips, n dips) | $t_n \approx n \cdot t_1$ | µm | Linear approximation for moderate $n$ |
| 3 | Coagulant film thickness | $C_f = V_c / A_f$ | µm | $V_c$ = coagulant volume pickup, $A_f$ = former surface area |
| 4 | Former heat balance | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J | $m$ = former mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) |
| 5 | Cycle time per former | $T_c = t_{\text{dip}} + t_{\text{dwell}} + t_{\text{leach}} + t_{\text{dry}} + t_{\text{vulc}} + t_{\text{strip}}$ | s | Sum of all station times on a dipping line |
| 6 | Throughput (formers/hr) | $\text{TH} = 3600 / T_c$ | formers/hr | Determines line productivity |
| 7 | Release force (qualitative) | $F_r \propto \gamma_{LA} \cdot \cos\theta$ | N | $\gamma_{LA}$ = latex-air surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle on former |
| 8 | Thermal expansion (former) | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | m | $\alpha$ = linear expansion coefficient (1/K) — **Al: 23×10⁻⁶**, **Glass: 3.3×10⁻⁶**, **SS: 17×10⁻⁶** |
| 9 | Aluminium mass per former | $m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot (\pi r^2 L)$ | kg | $\rho_{Al}$ = **2700 kg/m³** |
| 10 | Chrome plating thickness | $\delta_{Cr} = I \cdot t \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot M_{Cr} / (n \cdot F \cdot \rho_{Cr})$ | µm | Faraday's law of electrolysis — relevant for plating time calculation |

> **Constant Reference Values (Memorize):**
> - $\rho_{Al}$ = **2700 kg/m³**
> - $\rho_{SS}$ = **8000 kg/m³**
> - $\rho_{glass}$ ≈ **2500 kg/m³**
> - $\rho_{Cr}$ = **7190 kg/m³**
> - $c_{p,Al}$ = **900 J/kg·K**
> - $c_{p,SS}$ = **500 J/kg·K**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Former Choice | Why |
|-----------------|---------------|-----|
| **Medical gloves (surgical, exam)** | Chrome-plated Aluminum | Cost-effective + good thermal profile for high-volume production |
| **Premium surgical gloves** | SS 316L | Biocompatibility + extreme durability |
| **Party balloons** | Glass or ABS plastic | Cheap, no large-volume stress |
| **Medical catheters** | SS 316L | Smoothness + sterility |
| **Condoms** | Glass or SS | Hygiene-critical, smooth surface |
| **Toy balloons (low cost)** | Plastic | Single-use economics |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU answer enrichment:** Whenever asked to *justify* a former material in an exam, always link to **(a) thermal conductivity, (b) surface smoothness, (c) corrosion resistance, and (d) cost-per-cycle**. This 4-point logic fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Single-Dip Latex Film Thickness

The classical **Stevenson's model** for latex film deposition (validated by KTU reference texts) considers that when a smooth vertical former is withdrawn from a liquid latex bath, a thin film of liquid adheres to the former. The film thickness is governed by viscous drag, gravity, and the balance of surface tensions.

### Setting Up the Problem

Consider a vertical flat former of width $w$ being withdrawn at constant velocity $v$ from a Newtonian liquid latex of viscosity $\eta$ and surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$.

### Force Balance

At the dynamic wetting line, three forces act on the meniscus:
1. **Viscous drag force** pulling liquid up: $F_{\text{visc}} \propto \eta \cdot v / t$
2. **Gravitational force** pulling liquid down: $F_{\text{grav}} \propto \rho \cdot g \cdot t$
3. **Surface tension force** at the contact line: $F_{\text{ST}} \propto \gamma_{LV}$

### Dimensional Analysis

Setting $F_{\text{visc}} \sim F_{\text{grav}}$ gives the **Landau-Levich equation**:

$$
t = k \cdot \left( \frac{\eta \cdot v}{\rho \cdot g} \right)^{1/2}
$$

where $k$ is a dimensionless constant (~0.93 for Newtonian liquids).

### Adaptation to Latex Compounds

For latex (a colloidal dispersion, not a simple Newtonian liquid), the rheology is more complex (shear-thinning). The simplified empirical form used industrially is:

$$
\boxed{t \;=\; k \cdot \sqrt{\eta \cdot t_d}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{where: } \quad t      &= \text{wet film thickness (µm)} \\
k                              &= \text{system constant (~50–80 µm·(mPa·s)⁻⁰·⁵·s⁻⁰·⁵)} \\
\eta                           &= \text{apparent latex viscosity at shear rate (mPa·s)} \\
t_d                            &= \text{dwell time in latex bath (s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A glove former is dipped into a latex compound of viscosity **85 mPa·s** for a dwell time of **12 seconds**. Using $k = 60$ µm·(mPa·s)⁻⁰·⁵·s⁻⁰·⁵, compute the wet film thickness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= k \cdot \sqrt{\eta \cdot t_d} \\
  &= 60 \cdot \sqrt{85 \cdot 12} \\
  &= 60 \cdot \sqrt{1020} \\
  &= 60 \cdot 31.937 \\
  &= 1916.2 \;\; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 1.92 \;\; \text{mm (wet)}}
$$

After drying and vulcanization, the **dry film thickness** is roughly **10 % of the wet thickness**:

$$
t_{\text{dry}} = 0.10 \times t_{\text{wet}} = 0.192 \;\; \text{mm} = 192 \;\; \mu m
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Heat Required to Pre-Heat Aluminum Former

> **Problem:** A chrome-plated aluminum glove former has a mass of **0.35 kg**. It is to be heated from ambient **30 °C** to dipping temperature **70 °C**. How much heat energy must be supplied per former per cycle?

### Governing Equation

$$
Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{where: } \quad m      &= 0.35 \;\; \text{kg} \\
c_p                            &= 900 \;\; \text{J/kg·K} \quad \text{(aluminum)} \\
\Delta T                       &= 70 - 30 = 40 \;\; \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Substitution

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0.35 \times 900 \times 40 \\
  &= 0.35 \times 36000 \\
  &= 12600 \;\; \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = 12.6 \;\; \text{kJ per former per cycle}}
$$

### Production-Scale Extrapolation

If a dipping line runs at **TH = 3600 formers/hour**, the heater-bank power required is:

$$
P = Q \times \text{TH} = 12600 \times \frac{3600}{3600} = 12600 \;\; \text{W} \approx 12.6 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

(assuming no heat losses).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Thermal Expansion of a Glass Former

> **Problem:** A glass balloon former has length $L_0 = 25$ cm at 25 °C. It is heated to 65 °C. Compute the elongation. $\alpha_{\text{glass}} = 3.3 \times 10^{-6}$ /K.

$$
\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= 0.25 \times (3.3 \times 10^{-6}) \times (65 - 25) \\
         &= 0.25 \times (3.3 \times 10^{-6}) \times 40 \\
         &= 33 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L = 33 \;\; \mu m}
$$

Compare with **aluminum** at same conditions: $\Delta L_{Al} = 0.25 \times 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 230$ µm — **7× larger**. This is why glass formers hold dimensional tolerances better during repeated heating cycles.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Property Table

| Property | Glass | Aluminum (Al 6061) | Stainless Steel (SS 316L) | Plastic (ABS) | Porcelain |
|----------|:-----:|:------------------:|:-------------------------:|:-------------:|:---------:|
| Density (kg/m³) | 2500 | **2700** | 8000 | 1050 | 2400 |
| Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | 1.0 | **205** | 16 | 0.2 | 1.5 |
| $c_p$ (J/kg·K) | 750 | **900** | 500 | 1400 | 800 |
| $\alpha$ (×10⁻⁶ /K) | 3.3 | 23 | 17 | 70 | 4 |
| Surface Hardness (Mohs) | 5–6 | 2.5–3 | 5–6 | 2 | 7 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Poor (needs Cr plating) | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Cost Index (relative) | 3 | **1** | 8 | 1 | 5 |
| Dip Cycle Life | 1000s | 20k–50k | >100k | 100s–1k | 5k |
| Max Working Temp (°C) | 300 | 200 | 600 | 80 | 400 |
| Biocompatibility | High | High (Cr plated) | **Highest** | Variable | High |

---

## 3.5 Decision-Tree for Former Selection

Use this KTU-friendly decision tree in exam answers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product:} \quad & \text{Surgical / Medical grade?} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{SS 316L or Cr-plated Al} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{NO} \\
\text{Volume:} \quad & > 10^6 \text{ units/yr?} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Cr-plated Aluminum} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{NO} \\
\text{Shape:} \quad & \text{Complex anatomical?} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Aluminum (easy to CNC)} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{NO} \\
\text{Temp:} \quad & > 100 \text{ °C cure?} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Porcelain or SS} \\
                      & \;\;\downarrow \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{Glass or Plastic (low cost)}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Dipping Line with Former

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coagulant Tank] -->|1st dip - CaNO3| B[Coagulant Dip Station]
    B --> C[Former Pre-Heater 60-80 C]
    C --> D[Latex Compound Tank]
    D -->|2nd dip - dwell 8-15 s| E[Wet Latex Film on Former]
    E --> F[Leaching Tank - hot water 70 C]
    F --> G[Drying Oven 80-100 C]
    G --> H[Vulcanization Oven 120-140 C]
    H --> I[Stripping Station - doffing]
    I --> J[Finished Latex Product]
    I -.->|recycle| A

    subgraph FORMER_LIFECYCLE
      direction LR
      K[Former Mounted on Rack] --> L[Heating]
      L --> M[Dipping]
      M --> N[Leaching]
      N --> O[Curing]
      O --> P[Stripping]
      P --> Q[Re-mounted - Next Cycle]
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Former Material Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Product Requirement]) --> Q1{Medical Grade?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Tool Life > 100k cycles?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Annual Volume > 1M units?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| SS[SS 316L Former]
    Q2 -->|No| CrAl[Chrome-plated Aluminum]
    Q3 -->|Yes| CrAl
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Complex Anatomical Shape?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Al[Aluminum - CNC Machined]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{High Temp Cure > 100C?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Porc[Porcelain or SS]
    Q5 -->|No| Glass[Glass Former]
    Q5 -.->|Low cost priority| Plastic[ABS Plastic Former]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Cross-Section of a Chrome-Plated Aluminum Former

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compounded Latex Film - 0.1-0.3 mm] --> B[Chrome Plating Layer - 5-25 um]
    B --> C[Aluminum Substrate - 3-8 mm]
    C --> D[Mounting Shank - SS - for chain conveyor]
    D --> E[Conveyor Mount]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Coating Build-up on Former During Dipping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Bare Former]) --> S1[Step 1 Coagulant Pickup]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Latex Wet Film Deposition]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Coagulation Diffusion - Ca2+ ions]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Gel Film Formation]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Leaching - water wash]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Drying - water evaporation]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Prevulcanization - 80-100 C]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Final Vulcanization - 120-140 C]
    S8 --> S9([Vulcanized Latex Film Ready for Stripping])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block above corresponds to a *time station* on an industrial continuous dipping line. The former physically visits each station in sequence, driven by an overhead chain conveyor. A typical line has 30–80 stations, and one full cycle takes **8–20 minutes** depending on product thickness.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a former in latex technology. List any three materials used for former fabrication.**

**Model Answer:**

A former is a precisely shaped mould, typically with a highly polished surface, used in the latex dipping process to give shape to a dipped latex product by deposition, coagulation, and vulcanization of a thin liquid latex film on its surface.

Three common former materials are:
1. **Glass** — used for balloons and laboratory products.
2. **Aluminum** (chrome-plated) — the industry standard for gloves.
3. **Stainless Steel (SS 316L)** — used for premium medical/surgical products.

*(Any 3 from: Glass, Aluminum, Stainless Steel, Plastics, Porcelain — all valid.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why are aluminum formers for latex dipping usually chrome-plated? Mention any two advantages of chrome plating.**

**Model Answer:**

Raw aluminum is chemically reactive with the **alkaline ammonia** and **chloride-rich coagulants** used in latex dipping, leading to pitting, discoloration, and poor release of the cured latex film. Chrome plating seals the surface.

Two advantages of chrome plating:
1. Provides a **mirror-smooth surface** (Ra < 0.05 µm), ensuring the inner surface of the latex product is defect-free.
2. Acts as a **semi-permanent release layer**, allowing easy stripping (doffing) of the finished product without tearing.

*(Bonus point for: corrosion resistance, longer tool life of 20,000–50,000 dip cycles.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B]**

---

### Question A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Classify the different types of formers used in latex dipping industries based on **(i) material of construction** and **(ii) product to be manufactured**. For each category, give one example product.   **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare the properties of **glass formers, aluminum formers, and stainless-steel formers** under the heads of: thermal conductivity, surface smoothness, corrosion resistance, cost, and typical dip-cycle life. Justify which former is most suitable for **mass production of surgical gloves**.   **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification:**

**(i) Material of Construction:**

| # | Former Type | Typical Product |
|---|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Glass | Balloons, finger cots |
| 2 | Chrome-plated Aluminum | Surgical gloves, examination gloves |
| 3 | Stainless Steel (SS 304 / 316L) | Catheters, condoms, premium surgical gloves |
| 4 | Plastics (ABS, POM) | Disposable finger cots, low-cost balloons |
| 5 | Porcelain / Ceramic | Teats (baby bottle nipples) |
| 6 | Wooden (obsolete) | — |

**(ii) Product-based Classification:**

| # | Former Type | Geometry | Material Choice |
|---|-------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Glove formers | Anatomical, 5-digit | Cr-plated Al / SS 316L |
| 2 | Balloon formers | Pear / round | Glass / Al |
| 3 | Condom formers | Long dumbbell | Glass / SS |
| 4 | Catheter formers | Long thin mandrel | SS 316L |
| 5 | Finger-cot formers | Short cylinder | Glass / Plastic |
| 6 | Tubing formers | Long cylinder | SS / Al |
| 7 | Teat formers | Bulbous with neck | Porcelain / SS |

**(b) Comparative Table:**

| Property | Glass | Aluminum (Cr-plated) | Stainless Steel (316L) |
|----------|:-----:|:-------------------:|:----------------------:|
| Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | 1.0 (poor) | **205 (excellent)** | 16 (moderate) |
| Surface Smoothness | Excellent | Excellent (after Cr plating) | Excellent (mirror polish) |
| Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Good (Cr layer protects) | **Outstanding** |
| Cost Index (Al = 1) | ~3 | **1** | ~8 |
| Dip-Cycle Life | ~1,000 | 20,000–50,000 | **>100,000** |
| Weight | Moderate | Light | Heavy |

**Justification for Surgical Gloves:**

**Chrome-plated aluminum formers** are most suitable for mass production of surgical gloves because:

1. **High thermal conductivity (205 W/m·K)** ensures rapid and uniform pre-heating of the former → uniform latex film thickness → low rejection rate.
2. **Lightweight (2700 kg/m³)** allows higher packing density on dipping-line conveyors → higher throughput.
3. **Excellent machinability** allows fabrication of complex anatomical hand shapes with five digits.
4. **Adequate dip-cycle life** (20,000–50,000 cycles) at **low cost**, making it economically viable.
5. Chrome plating provides the required **biocompatibility, smoothness, and corrosion resistance** for medical-grade products.

*(Stainless steel is reserved for premium applications where very high cycle life is needed; glass is too fragile for mass production.)*

---

### Question B. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **role of a former** in the latex dipping process. Describe the **functional sequence** of stations visited by a former on a typical continuous dipping line.   **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An aluminum glove former of mass **0.40 kg** is heated from **25 °C to 75 °C**. Compute the heat energy required per former. If the dipping line processes **3000 formers per hour**, estimate the **electrical heater power** required (assuming no heat loss). Take $c_p$ for aluminum = **900 J/kg·K**.   **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of a Former — Block Flow Explanation:**

A former is the **shape-defining tool** in latex dipping. It performs the following functions:

1. **Defines geometry** of the final dipped product (glove, balloon, condom, catheter, etc.).
2. **Supports the wet latex film** during coagulation, leaching, drying, and vulcanization.
3. **Provides thermal energy** to assist drying and prevulcanization (the former is pre-heated).
4. **Determines inner surface finish** of the finished product (because the latex cures *against* the former).
5. **Acts as a heat reservoir** that can be reused over thousands of dipping cycles.

**Functional Station Sequence on a Dipping Line:**

| Station # | Operation | Typical Conditions |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | Former mounting on rack | Ambient |
| 2 | Pre-heat oven | 60–80 °C |
| 3 | Coagulant dip | Ca(NO₃)₂ or CaCl₂ solution, 8–15 % |
| 4 | Drying after coagulant | 50–70 °C |
| 5 | Latex compound dip | Dwell 8–15 s, viscosity 50–150 mPa·s |
| 6 | Wet film setting | 30–60 s dwell |
| 7 | Leaching tank (hot water wash) | 60–80 °C, removes excess surfactant |
| 8 | Beading station (for gloves — rolls cuff bead) | Mechanical |
| 9 | Drying oven | 80–100 °C, 5–10 min |
| 10 | Prevulcanization | 100–110 °C, 10–20 min |
| 11 | Final vulcanization | 120–140 °C, 15–30 min |
| 12 | Stripping / Doffing | Mechanical or manual |
| 13 | Former cleaning + re-mount | Continuous loop |

A typical line has **30–80 stations**, and one complete cycle takes **8–20 minutes**.

**(b) Heat Energy and Heater Power Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Heat per former:**

$$
Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m      &= 0.40 \;\; \text{kg} \\
c_p    &= 900 \;\; \text{J/kg·K} \\
\Delta T &= 75 - 25 = 50 \;\; \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0.40 \times 900 \times 50 \\
  &= 18000 \;\; \text{J} \\
  &= 18 \;\; \text{kJ per former}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Electrical heater power:**

Line throughput: $TH = 3000$ formers/hour $= 3000 / 3600 = 0.8333$ formers/s

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= Q \times \text{TH (per second)} \\
  &= 18000 \times 0.8333 \\
  &= 15000 \;\; \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 15 \;\; \text{kW}}
$$

(In practice, the heater must be oversized by 20–30 % to account for convective and radiative losses, giving a design rating of **18–20 kW**.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "former" with "mould"** in compression moulding — a former is specifically for **dipping**, not press-curing.
> 2. **Writing "aluminum" without specifying "chrome-plated"** — examiners penalize this; raw aluminum is unsuitable.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention thermal conductivity** when justifying aluminum formers — this is the single most important property.
> 4. **Mixing up glass formers and porcelain formers** — they are chemically similar but differ in fabrication and applications.
> 5. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 6. **Not drawing a labelled sketch** in 14-mark answers — minimum **one block diagram** is expected.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Different Types of Formers"**

- [x] **Former** = the *shape-defining reusable mould* in latex dipping; it is the **physical heart** of the dipping process.
- [x] Formers are classified by **(a) material**, **(b) product made**, **(c) surface finish**, and **(d) geometry**.
- [x] **Glass formers** → smooth, brittle, cheap, used for balloons and lab-scale work.
- [x] **Aluminum formers** → most common; **must be chrome-plated**; thermal conductivity = **205 W/m·K**; cycle life 20k–50k.
- [x] **Stainless steel formers** → premium, durable >100k cycles, biocompatible (SS 316L for medical).
- [x] **Plastic formers** (ABS, POM, HDPE) → cheap, single-use, low-temperature, decorative products.
- [x] **Porcelain formers** → high-temperature resistant, used for teats.
- [x] **Wooden formers** → obsolete; mentioned only historically.
- [x] **Glove formers** = anatomical, hand-shaped, Cr-plated Al or SS 316L.
- [x] **Balloon formers** = pear-shaped, often rotated during dipping to prevent sagging.
- [x] **Condom formers** = dumbbell-shaped, glass or SS, mounted vertically.
- [x] **Catheter formers** = long thin mandrels, SS, 0.5–5 mm diameter.
- [x] **Chromium plating thickness** = 5–25 µm; prevents Al corrosion + provides release.
- [x] **Surface roughness** of polished former < **0.05 µm** (mirror finish) is essential to avoid film pinholes.
- [x] **Film thickness formula:** $t = k \sqrt{\eta \cdot t_d}$ — the workhorse KTU equation.
- [x] **Pre-heating temperature** of former: **60–80 °C** typically.
- [x] **Chrome-plated Al formers** dominate ~90 % of global dipped latex glove production.
- [x] **Thermal conductivity** order: Glass (1) < SS (16) < **Al (205)**.
- [x] **Cycle time** = sum of station times; typical 8–20 min per former.
- [x] **Former stripping (doffing)** is the final step; release aided by Cr plating + release agents.
- [x] **Biocompatibility** is critical for medical goods → SS 316L or properly Cr-plated Al only.

> **Mnemonic — "GASP"** to remember former materials: **G**lass · **A**luminum · **S**tainless Steel · **P**lastic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dipping Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dipping Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dipping Process (KTU Official Definition):**
> The dipping process is a **latex forming technique** in which a shaped former (mold) made of glass, porcelain, aluminum, or stainless steel is immersed into a tank containing compounded natural rubber latex (NRL) or synthetic latex, allowing a thin, uniform film of rubber to deposit on the former's surface through either **wet gelation (coagulant dipping)**, **thermal gelation (heat-sensitive dipping)**, or **electrolyte-induced destabilization (anode/electrodeposition process)**. The deposited wet gel film is subsequently leached, dried, vulcanized, and stripped off the former to yield seamless hollow rubber products.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCPOT303 syllabus (Module 3)**, the dipping process is classified as one of the four major latex conversion technologies alongside casting, foaming, and thread production.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Candle-Making Principle":**
> Imagine dipping a wick repeatedly into melted wax. Each dip deposits a thin layer; letting it solidify between dips builds up the final thickness. Latex dipping follows the **same principle**, except the "wax" is liquid latex (a colloidal dispersion of rubber particles in water), the "wick" is a clean polished former, and the "solidification" happens because either a chemical (coagulant) or heat destabilizes the rubber particles, forcing them to fuse into a continuous wet gel film on the former.

**Plain-English Translation:** Take a hand-shaped porcelain mold, dip it into a calcium nitrate (coagulant) solution, dry it, then dip it into liquid latex. The coagulant ions diffuse into the latex and **clump together** the rubber particles exactly where the latex touches the mold. After drying and curing, peel off the mold → you get a perfect rubber glove.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industry Value |
|---|---|
| **Total Solids Content (TSC)** | **50–62 %** |
| **Viscosity (Brookfield, spindle 2 @ 60 rpm)** | **50–500 mPa·s** |
| **Coagulant Concentration (Ca(NO₃)₂)** | **10–25 % w/v** in alcohol/water |
| **Withdrawal Speed** | **0.5–5 cm/s** |
| **Drying Temperature** | **60–120 °C** |
| **Dwell Time in Latex** | **5–30 seconds** |
| **Typical Final Film Thickness** | **0.1–0.6 mm** |

> [!TIP]
> These are **industry-standard reference values** for examination purposes; examiners expect answers within these numerical windows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Principal Dipping Technologies (Logic Steps)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **three industrial variants** of latex dipping, each defined by the mechanism that triggers coagulation on the former surface:

#### 🔹 Variant 1 — Coagulant Dipping (Porcelain / "Belfast" Process)
- **Step 1:** Former is cleaned, polished, and pre-heated to ~40–60 °C.
- **Step 2:** Former is dipped into a **coagulant bath** (typically Ca(NO₃)₂ or CaCl₂ in alcohol or water).
- **Step 3:** Coagulant-coated former is **dried** to remove solvent → uniform salt layer.
- **Step 4:** Former is dipped into compounded latex; **diffusion of Ca²⁺ ions destabilizes** the rubber particles within microns of the surface, forming a wet gel.
- **Step 5:** Withdrawal, dwell, leaching (to extract proteins, excess salts), and oven vulcanization.
- **Step 6:** Stripping — peeling the dry cured film off the former.

> Used for **~85 %** of dipped products globally (gloves, balloons, condoms).

#### 🔹 Variant 2 — Straight Dipping (Anode / Electrodeposition Process)
- **Step 1:** Former is the **anode** (+) of an electrolytic cell; latex contains the **cathode** (–) material (e.g., carbon rod).
- **Step 2:** DC current (~1–10 V) is passed; **H⁺ ions** generated near the anode lower pH locally, causing destabilization.
- **Step 3:** Rubber cations deposit on the anode (former).
- **Step 4:** Coagulation depth is controlled by **current density, voltage, and time**.

#### 🔹 Variant 3 — Heat-Sensitized (Thermal) Dipping
- **Step 1:** Latex is compounded with **heat sensitizers** (ZnO + NH₄NO₃, or polyethers, or PVP-H₂O₂ systems).
- **Step 2:** Hot former (~70–90 °C) is dipped into cold latex; **heat triggers gelation** at the interface.
- **Step 3:** Film thickness depends on former temperature and dwell time.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta_{wet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6\,\eta\, V_w}{\rho\, g}}$ | **Landau–Levich wet film thickness** for straight dipping. η = latex viscosity, V_w = withdrawal speed, ρ = latex density, g = gravity. |
| 2 | $\delta_{wet} \;\propto\; \sqrt{t_{dwell}}$ | **Coagulant dipping** — wet thickness scales with square root of dwell time (Fickian diffusion). |
| 3 | $\delta_{wet} \;\propto\; C_{coag}^{1/2}$ | Film thickness proportional to √(coagulant concentration). |
| 4 | $M_{film} = V_{film} \times \rho_{dry} \times \dfrac{TSC}{100}$ | Mass of deposited dry rubber from film volume. |
| 5 | $TSC = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | Total Solids Content of latex. |
| 6 | $t_{leach} \propto \dfrac{x^{2}}{D}$ | Leaching time scales with square of film thickness; D = diffusivity of ions in gel. |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Shear stress–strain-rate–viscosity relationship (Newtonian assumption for thin films). |
| 8 | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{\rho_{latex} \times \delta_{wet} \times A_{former}}{m_{final}}$ | Material utilization efficiency check. |
| 9 | $\delta_{dry} = \delta_{wet} \times \dfrac{TSC}{100} \times (1 - \text{shrinkage})$ | Conversion of wet film to dry cured film thickness. |
| 10 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density (A/m²) governing anode dipping rate. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** In all dipping thickness problems, **withdrawal speed (V_w) and viscosity (η) appear under a square root**. Increasing either gives a sub-linear (√) gain — a frequent KTU examination trap. Students mistakenly assume linear proportionality.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Product Made by Dipping |
|---|---|
| **Medical / Healthcare** | Surgical gloves, examination gloves, catheters, balloon-tipped tubing |
| **Consumer Goods** | Balloons, swim caps, toy balloons, finger cots |
| **Industrial PPE** | Chemical-resistant gloves, dielectric gloves (after talc/dip coating) |
| **Dental / Baby Care** | Teats, soothers, dental dams |
| **Automotive** | Flexible bellows, valve diaphragms |

> The dipping process is the **only industrial technique** capable of producing seamless thin-walled rubber goods of complex geometry — a capability unmatched by extrusion or molding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Wet Film Thickness in Coagulant Dipping

**Problem (KTU-style):** A glass former is dipped into compounded natural rubber latex (viscosity η = 80 mPa·s, density ρ = 980 kg/m³) and withdrawn at speed V_w = 2.0 cm/s. Compute the theoretical wet film thickness.

We begin from the **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin (LLD) balance** for a thin Newtonian film dragged up a flat plate under gravity. For a thin film of thickness δ, the gravity-driven drainage flow is opposed by viscous shear. At equilibrium, the volumetric flow rate entrained equals the drainage flow rate.

**Step 1 — Set up the entrained film flow rate:**

The volumetric flow rate (per unit width) carried up by the moving plate is:

$$
q_{entrained} = V_w \cdot \delta
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the gravity-driven drainage flow rate:**

For a viscous film of thickness δ on a vertical plate under gravity, the downward parabolic (Poiseuille-type) drainage has a flow rate (per unit width):

$$
q_{drain} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, \delta^{3}}{3 \, \eta}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate entrained and drained flow rates (steady-state balance):**

$$
V_w \cdot \delta \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, g \, \delta^{3}}{3 \, \eta}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for δ:**

Cancel one δ on both sides:

$$
V_w \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, g \, \delta^{2}}{3 \, \eta}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\delta^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{3 \, \eta \, V_w}{\rho \, g}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \, \eta \, V_w}{\rho \, g}}
$$

> The classical Landau–Levich result uses a numerical coefficient of 0.94 × (the square-root term), derived from matching the inner viscous boundary layer with the outer static meniscus. For KTU exam purposes we use the **approximate engineering form**:

$$
\delta_{wet} \;\approx\; 0.94\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\eta \, V_w}{\rho \, g}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Always quote the **0.94 coefficient** when the question specifies "Landau–Levich" by name; otherwise use the **√(3ηV/ρg)** form.

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

Given: η = 80 mPa·s = 0.080 Pa·s; V_w = 2.0 cm/s = 0.020 m/s; ρ = 980 kg/m³; g = 9.81 m/s².

$$
\delta_{wet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \times 0.080 \times 0.020}{980 \times 9.81}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
3 \times 0.080 \times 0.020 \;=\; 4.80 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
980 \times 9.81 \;=\; 9613.8
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta_{wet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.80 \times 10^{-3}}{9613.8}} \;=\; \sqrt{4.99 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 7.06 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{wet} \;\approx\; 0.71\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to dry film thickness:**

Assuming TSC = 60 % and negligible shrinkage:

$$
\delta_{dry} = \delta_{wet} \times 0.60 \;\approx\; 0.43\;\text{mm}
$$

This thickness range (**0.4–0.7 mm**) is typical of industrial surgical glove walls — confirming dimensional realism.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Diffusion-Controlled Thickness in Coagulant Dipping

**Step 1 — Fick's Second Law for Ca²⁺ diffusion into latex gel:**

$$
\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} \;=\; D\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions (semi-infinite medium approximation):**

C(x, 0) = 0 (initially no coagulant in latex).  
C(0, t) = C₀ (constant surface concentration maintained by coagulant layer).

**Step 3 — Standard solution (error function complement):**

$$
C(x, t) \;=\; C_{0}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define the gelation front** as the locus where C = C_critical (the minimum ion concentration that destabilizes latex). At this front, x = x_g:

$$
C_{crit} \;=\; C_{0}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x_g}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Invert the error function** (using series or tables):

$$
\dfrac{x_g}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \;=\; \text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{crit}}{C_{0}}\right) \;=\; k \;(\text{a constant})
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the gel thickness:**

$$
\boxed{\,x_g \;=\; 2k\,\sqrt{Dt}\,}
$$

**This confirms the $\delta \propto \sqrt{t_{dwell}}$ rule** cited in the Formula Sheet. The film grows by diffusion, NOT linearly — a critical KTU concept.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Comparison Standard)

| Parameter | Coagulant Dipping | Anode (Electrochemical) | Heat-Sensitized |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger for gelation** | Ca²⁺ / Mg²⁺ ions | Electrolytic pH drop | Heat + ZnO/NH₄NO₃ |
| **Former material** | Glass, porcelain, Al, SS | Conductive (Ni-plated brass) | Heat-conductive metal |
| **Former temp** | 40–60 °C | Room | 70–90 °C |
| **Latex shelf-life concern** | Low | Moderate (pH drift) | High (sensitizer decomp.) |
| **Film uniformity** | Excellent | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Thickness control knobs** | Dwell time, C_coag, TSC | Voltage, current, time | Temp, dwell |
| **Capital cost** | Medium | High | Low–Medium |
| **Typical product** | Gloves, balloons | Latex thread precursors | Thick-walled goods |
| **Production speed** | 8–12 sec/cycle | 15–30 sec/cycle | 10–20 sec/cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask **"Compare and contrast coagulant dipping with anode dipping"** — this matrix is the definitive answer skeleton.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coagulant Dipping Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Raw NR Latex 60 percent TSC"] --> A1["Compounding Tank"]
    A1 --> A2["Maturation Tank 24 hours"]
    A2 --> A3["Latex Dipping Tank 28 deg C"]
    F0["Former Cleaning Station"] --> F1["Polishing and Drying"]
    F1 --> F2["Pre heating 50 deg C"]
    F2 --> F3["Coagulant Dip Ca NO3 2 in alcohol"]
    F3 --> F4["Coagulant Drying Oven 80 deg C"]
    F4 --> F5["Dip into Latex Tank dwell 12 s"]
    F5 --> F6["Wet Gel Former"]
    A3 --> F5
    F6 --> F7["Leaching Tank 60 deg C water"]
    F7 --> F8["Beading and Rolling Cuff"]
    F8 --> F9["Vulcanization Oven 120 deg C 30 min"]
    F9 --> F10["Stripping Station"]
    F10 --> F11["Finished Dipped Product"]
    F11 --> F12["Quality Inspection"]
    F12 --> F13["Packaging and Sterilization"]
    B1["Overflow Latex Recycle"] -.-> A3
    F6 -.-> B1
```

```mermaid
    style V4 fill:#C1E1FF

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Single Dipping Cycle (Time-Axis View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Former
    participant CoagTank as Coagulant Tank
    participant DryOven1 as Drying Oven 1
    participant LatexTank as Latex Tank

    participant VulcOven as Vulcanization Oven
    participant Stripper as Stripping Station
    Former->>CoagTank: Immerse 5 s
    Former->>DryOven1: Air dry coagulant 60 s at 80 C

    Former->>LatexTank: Withdraw at 2 cm per s

```


> [!NOTE]
