<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define dipping process. List any four products manufactured by latex dipping.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Dipping is a latex forming process in which a shaped former is immersed into compounded latex to deposit a uniform rubber film on its surface through coagulant, thermal, or electrochemical destabilization of the latex. The film is then leached, dried, vulcanized, and stripped.

Four products:
1. Surgical / examination gloves
2. Balloons
3. Condoms
4. Catheters / teats

> **Mark Allocation:** Definition = 1 mark; Four products = 0.5 each = 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*What is the role of calcium nitrate in coagulant dipping?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Calcium nitrate [Ca(NO₃)₂] acts as the **electrolyte coagulant** in the dipping process.
- It dissociates into Ca²⁺ and NO₃⁻ ions in the solvent.
- When the coagulant-coated former is dipped into latex, Ca²⁺ ions diffuse into the latex and **neutralize the negative surface charge** on rubber particles.
- This reduces inter-particle electrostatic repulsion, causing rubber particles to **destabilize and coagulate** on the former surface, forming a wet gel film.
- Calcium nitrate is preferred over calcium chloride because the nitrate ion is non-corrosive to metallic formers.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Internal choice between Q-A and Q-B.*

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **coagulant dipping process** with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of each processing stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A porcelain former is dipped in a compounded natural rubber latex having viscosity **η = 0.05 Pa·s** and density **ρ = 960 kg/m³**. The withdrawal speed is **V_w = 1.5 cm/s**. Calculate the **wet film thickness** using the Landau–Levich equation and estimate the **dry film thickness** assuming **TSC = 55 %** and negligible shrinkage. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks**

The coagulant dipping process (also called the porcelain process) involves the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Former Preparation (1 Mark):**
The former (typically porcelain, glass, or aluminum) is cleaned, polished with mild abrasive, washed with detergent, and dried. Surface cleanliness is critical because contamination leads to pinholes and uncoated patches.

**Stage 2 — Coagulant Dipping (1 Mark):**
The cleaned former is dipped for ~3–5 s into a coagulant solution of Ca(NO₃)₂ (10–25 % w/v) dissolved in a water/alcohol mixture. Alcohol speeds up drying of the coagulant layer.

**Stage 3 — Coagulant Drying (0.5 Mark):**
The dipped former is held in a warm air zone (60–80 °C) to evaporate solvent, leaving an **even crystalline layer** of calcium nitrate on the former surface.

**Stage 4 — Latex Dipping (1 Mark):**
The coagulant-coated former is dipped into the compounded latex tank for a controlled dwell time (5–30 s). Ca²⁺ ions diffuse inward through the latex interface, neutralizing the surface charge of rubber particles within a few hundred micrometres.

**Stage 5 — Wet Gel Withdrawal & Dwell (0.5 Mark):**
The former is withdrawn at controlled speed. The wet gel film continues to thicken slightly by diffusion even after withdrawal.

**Stage 6 — Leaching (1 Mark):**
The gel-coated former passes through a hot water bath (50–70 °C, 5–10 min) to extract water-soluble proteins, excess calcium ions, and unreacted stabilizers — critical for hypoallergenic medical gloves.

**Stage 7 — Beading & Cuff Rolling (0.5 Mark):**
For gloves, the cuff edge is rolled to produce a reinforced bead.

**Stage 8 — Vulcanization & Stripping (1 Mark):**
The product is cured in an oven at 110–130 °C for 20–40 min. After cooling, the dry rubber article is peeled (stripped) off the former. The former is recycled.

**Flow Diagram (mandatory):** Draw the Mermaid flowchart from Section 4.1 above, or a hand-drawn equivalent — full marks awarded only with diagram.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Viscosity η = 0.05 Pa·s
- Density ρ = 960 kg/m³
- Withdrawal speed V_w = 1.5 cm/s = 0.015 m/s
- g = 9.81 m/s²
- TSC = 55 %

**Step 1 — Apply the Landau–Levich equation (engineering form):**

$$
\delta_{wet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\,\eta\,V_w}{\rho\,g}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\delta_{wet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \times 0.05 \times 0.015}{960 \times 9.81}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
3 \times 0.05 \times 0.015 = 2.25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
960 \times 9.81 = 9417.6
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta_{wet} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.25 \times 10^{-3}}{9417.6}} = \sqrt{2.39 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.89 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{wet} \approx 0.49\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute dry film thickness:**

$$
\delta_{dry} = \delta_{wet} \times \dfrac{TSC}{100} = 0.49 \times 0.55
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{dry} \approx 0.27\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (1 Mark):**
This value lies within the industrial surgical-glove range (0.15–0.35 mm), confirming that the calculated withdrawal speed is industrially realistic.

> **Mark Allocation:** Equation = 1; Substitution = 1; Calculation = 2; Answer with units = 1; Dry thickness = 1; Realistic verification = 1.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 4]**

**(a)** Compare **coagulant dipping**, **anode dipping**, and **heat-sensitized dipping** with respect to mechanism, former material, controlling parameters, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **typical formulation of a surgical glove** prepared by coagulant dipping, listing the function of each ingredient. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks**

Present a tabular comparison as in Section 3.3 of these notes. **Suggested marking split:**

| Criterion | Marks |
|---|---|
| Coagulant dipping mechanism explained | 2 |
| Anode dipping mechanism explained | 2 |
| Heat-sensitized mechanism explained | 1.5 |
| Comparison table (former, control knobs, products) | 1.5 |

**Sample write-up (1 mark each row):**

- **Coagulant dipping** uses Ca(NO₃)₂ to destabilize latex via ionic neutralization. Former: porcelain/glass. Control: dwell time, coagulant concentration.
- **Anode dipping** uses DC current to generate H⁺ at the anode (former), locally reducing pH and coagulating rubber. Former: conductive metal. Control: voltage, current, time.
- **Heat-sensitized dipping** uses ZnO + NH₄NO₃ to make latex thermally unstable above 40 °C; a hot former triggers gelation. Former: heat-conductive metal. Control: former temperature.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks**

**Typical Surgical Glove Formulation (per 100 parts dry rubber):**

| Ingredient | phr | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **60 % NR Latex (centrifuged)** | 100.0 | Base polymer; provides elasticity, tensile strength |
| **Potassium hydroxide (KOH)** | 0.2–0.5 | pH stabilizer; keeps latex alkaline |
| **Potassium oleate / soap** | 0.3–1.0 | Surfactant; mechanical and chemical stability |
| **Sulfur** | 0.8–1.5 | Vulcanizing agent |
| **Zinc diethyldithiocarbamate (ZDEC)** | 0.5–1.0 | Ultra-accelerator (fast cure at 100–120 °C) |
| **Zinc oxide (ZnO)** | 0.3–0.8 | Activator + heat sensitizer (for some variants) |
| **Antioxidant (e.g., Wingstay L)** | 0.5–1.0 | Aging resistance; prevents ozone cracking |
| **TiO₂ / pigment** | 0.5–2.0 | White opacity / colour |
| **Ca(NO₃)₂ coagulant** | External, 20 % w/v | Ionic destabilizer |
| **Carboxylated latex (optional blend)** | 0–20 | Improves tear strength and chemical resistance |

**Mark Allocation:** Eight ingredients × ~0.75 mark each + 1 mark for format = 7.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Feedback from KTU Valuation Reports):**
> 1. **No diagram drawn = −2 marks minimum** in any 7-mark "Explain the process" question. Always draw the flowchart even if it's a rough sketch.
> 2. **Missing units** in numerical answers = −0.5 to −1 mark. Always write mm or m explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing "withdrawal speed" and "dwell time"** — withdrawal speed affects the **meniscus-driven thickness** (Landau–Levich), dwell time affects the **diffusion-driven thickness** (Fickian). Examiners penalize interchange.
> 4. **Forgetting the √ proportionality** — writing "δ ∝ V_w" instead of "δ ∝ √V_w" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating "no shrinkage assumption"** in dry-thickness conversion = −0.5 mark.
> 6. **In Comparison questions**, writing in paragraphs instead of a table loses 1–2 marks. KTU examiners love tables.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Dipping = former + latex + gelation trigger + leach + cure + strip.
- ✅ Three variants: **Coagulant (Ca²⁺)**, **Anode (H⁺)**, **Heat (ZnO/NH₄NO₃)**.
- ✅ Coagulant of choice: **Ca(NO₃)₂** (10–25 % w/v) — non-corrosive.
- ✅ Master equation: $\delta_{wet} = \sqrt{3\eta V_w / \rho g}$ (Landau–Levich).
- ✅ Diffusive growth: $\delta \propto \sqrt{t_{dwell}}$ (Fickian — for coagulant dipping).
- ✅ TSC range for dipping: **50–62 %**.
- ✅ Withdrawal speed range: **0.5–5 cm/s**; Dwell: **5–30 s**.
- ✅ Dry thickness ≈ wet × (TSC/100).
- ✅ **Critical defects** to know: pinholes, streaks (lenticules), thick edges (runs), tacky surface — each tied to a specific process variable.
- ✅ Major products: gloves, balloons, condoms, catheters, teats.
- ✅ Leaching step is **mandatory** for medical products (protein removal).
- ✅ Always draw a **flow diagram** in 7-mark "process" questions.
- ✅ Always use a **comparison table** in 7-mark "compare" questions.
- ✅ Numerical answers must show **units and a 1-line verification** statement.
- ✅ Former materials: porcelain (most common), glass, aluminum, stainless steel, Ni-plated brass (anode process).
- ✅ Vulcanization temperature for dipped goods: **110–130 °C**; time **20–40 min**.
- ✅ **Anode dipping** advantage: precise thickness control via current density.
- ✅ **Heat-sensitized dipping** advantage: avoids chemical residues on the former.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: After Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** After Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In latex technology, **After Treatments** refer to the sequence of **post-forming unit operations** performed on freshly coagulated, gelled, or cast latex products (such as dipped goods, threads, foams, balloons, gloves, and condoms) **after** the primary shaping process (dipping, extruding, or foaming) is complete, but **before** the finished article is finally inspected, packed, and dispatched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> "After-treatments are the essential finishing operations — *leaching, drying, curing, stripping, surface halogenation, lubrication, powdering, sterilization and tumbling* — that transform a fragile, wet, plasticised green article into a dimensionally stable, hygienic, hypo-allergenic, and commercially acceptable finished latex product."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just moulded a clay pot on a potter's wheel.

- The pot is still **soft, full of water, fragile, and unfinished**. If you use it as-is, it will crumble.
- To make it strong and usable, you must **dry it** (remove water), **fire it in a kiln** (chemical hardening), and **polish/glaze the surface** (improve appearance and hygiene).
- In latex technology, the freshly dipped wet gel is exactly like that "soft clay pot". The various *after-treatments* are the drying, firing and polishing stages that convert it into a commercial rubber product.

### 1.3 Why After-Treatments Are Mandatory

Without after-treatments, the latex product will have:

| Defect | Root Cause |
|---|---|
| Poor wet gel strength | Unleached soluble stabilizers (proteins, soaps) |
| Sticky / tacky surface | Excess plasticiser migration |
| Bad smell & allergenicity | Residual ammonia, proteins, non-rubbers |
| Short shelf-life | Trapped moisture → hydrolysis & mould growth |
| Low mechanical strength | Incomplete vulcanisation crosslink density |
| Poor donning (glove sticks to hand) | High surface friction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics (must know):**
> - **Leaching water temperature:** 50 – 80 °C
> - **Leaching duration:** 10 – 60 minutes
> - **Drying temperature:** 80 – 130 °C
> - **Vulcanisation temperature:** 110 – 140 °C
> - **Chlorination strength:** 0.1 – 0.5 % available chlorine
> - **Sterilisation (gamma) dose:** **25 kGy** (minimum for medical devices)

---

## 1.4 Classification of After-Treatments

After-treatments are broadly classified into the following functional groups:

1. **Purification Treatments** — Leaching, Washing
2. **Dimensional Stabilisation Treatments** — Drying, Curing (Vulcanisation)
3. **Surface Modification Treatments** — Halogenation (Chlorination), Polymer Coating, Silicone Coating
4. **Detackification / Donning Treatments** — Powdering, Lubrication, Halogenation
5. **Mechanical Treatments** — Stripping, Tumbling, Bead-Rolling, Buffing
6. **Hygienic / Sterilisation Treatments** — Chlorination, Ethylene Oxide (EtO) sterilisation, Gamma irradiation, Autoclaving
7. **Aesthetic / Finishing Treatments** — Printing, Pigment coating, Branding

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam answers, always classify the treatments into these 7 categories in your opening sentence. Examiners award easy marks for a clean, well-structured classification.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Process Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Leaching (Washing / Extraction)

Leaching is the **most critical after-treatment** in latex dipped goods manufacturing. It is a mass-transfer-controlled diffusion process in which water-soluble substances are removed from the wet gel.

**Water-soluble substances removed:**
- **Surfactants / Soaps** (potassium oleate, sodium lauryl sulphate) — stabilise the latex but weaken the dry film
- **Proteins & Non-rubbers** (responsible for Type-I latex hypersensitivity)
- **Residual ammonia** (causes bad odour and pH drift)
- **Excess glycerol / plasticisers**
- **Free sulphur, accelerators, and their water-soluble by-products**

**Mechanism (step-by-step logic):**
1. The wet gel (a coagulum of rubber particles) is immersed in a hot water bath.
2. A **concentration gradient** develops between the high solute concentration *inside* the gel and the low concentration *outside*.
3. Solute molecules diffuse **outward** by Fickian diffusion.
4. The hot water bath is continuously replenished (or overflowed) to maintain a steep concentration gradient.
5. Agitation of the bath (or the article) reduces the boundary-layer thickness and increases the mass-transfer coefficient.

**Process variables:**
- **Temperature** — higher temperature increases diffusion coefficient *D* (typical 60 – 80 °C)
- **Time** — longer time reduces residual extractables exponentially
- **Water-to-article ratio** — high ratio avoids saturation
- **Water quality** — deionised / soft water prevents metal-ion staining
- **Agitation** — turbulent flow increases Sherwood number

### 2.1.2 Drying

Drying is a coupled **heat + mass transfer** process in which the free and bound water in the wet gel is removed by evaporation.

**Stages of drying:**
1. **Constant rate period** — surface water evaporates; rate depends on air velocity, humidity, temperature.
2. **Falling rate period** — internal moisture diffuses to the surface; rate depends on the internal diffusion coefficient of water in rubber.
3. **Equilibrium** — moisture content reaches the **equilibrium moisture content (EMC)** for the given temperature/humidity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical drying temperature:** for natural latex film = **80 – 100 °C**. Above ~120 °C, oxidative degradation and scorching (premature vulcanisation) begin.

### 2.1.3 Curing (Vulcanisation)

Curing is the **crosslinking reaction** in which linear polyisoprene chains are joined by **polysulphide bridges (–Sx–)** in the presence of sulphur and accelerators at elevated temperature.

**Reactions involved (simplified):**
1. Activator (ZnO) + accelerator (MBT, ZDMC) → active sulphurating complex
2. Active complex + rubber hydrocarbon (R–H) → R–S–H (pendent sulphidic group)
3. Crosslinking: R–S–H + R–H → R–S–R (mono-sulphidic bridge)
4. Further reactions produce di-, tri-, and poly-sulphidic bridges (–S₂– , –S₃– etc.)

**Cure parameters:**
- **Cure time (t₉₀)** — time to reach 90 % of maximum torque in a rheometer
- **Cure temperature** — typically **120 – 140 °C**
- **Scorch time (tₛ₂)** — time before crosslinking begins (safety window)
- **Optimum cure** — point where mechanical properties are maximum
- **Over-cure** — reversion (chain scission) and loss of properties

### 2.1.4 Stripping

Stripping is the **mechanical removal of the residual coagulant ring** that forms at the top of dipped goods (where latex meets the dipping form at the air-liquid interface). Done by **rollers, brushes, or air-knives**.

### 2.1.5 Surface Halogenation (Chlorination)

Surface chlorination is the controlled chemical modification of the outer ~1–5 µm of the rubber surface using a **chlorine donor** (typically **sodium hypochlorite, NaOCl**, or **chlorine gas Cl₂**) at acidic pH.

**Chemistry (overall):**
$$\text{–CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{–CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)(Cl)–CHCl–CH}_2\text{–}$$

**Effects of chlorination:**
- Reduces surface tackiness (eases donning of gloves)
- Reduces extractable proteins (lower allergenicity)
- Creates a smooth, low-friction surface
- Improves chemical resistance on the outer skin
- Can be done on **inside, outside, or both** surfaces

### 2.1.6 Powdering

Application of **talc, corn starch, calcium carbonate, or modified starch** powder to the inner surface of gloves to prevent self-adhesion and ease of donning. Mostly obsolete in medical gloves (powder → granuloma risk) and replaced by **polymer coating or chlorination**.

### 2.1.7 Polymer / Silicone Coating

Replaces powdering. A thin layer of **silicone emulsion, acrylic polymer, or polyurethane** is applied to the inner surface to provide:
- Lubricity
- Anti-tack
- Easy donning
- Compatibility with skin (no powder residue)

### 2.1.8 Tumbling

Tumbling is a **mechanical post-finishing operation** in which the cured articles (gloves, balloons) are rotated in a wire-mesh drum with optional addition of **slip agents (silica, talc)**. Effect: smoothens seams, removes dust, and gives a uniform surface.

### 2.1.9 Sterilisation

Mandatory for **medical latex products** (surgical gloves, condoms, catheters, blood bags). Common methods:

| Method | Condition | Effect on Latex |
|---|---|---|
| Gamma irradiation | 25 kGy dose | Crosslinks further; may discolour |
| Ethylene Oxide (EtO) gas | 37 – 63 °C, 1–6 hr | Effective at low temp; toxic residuals |
| Autoclaving | 121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min | Heat + steam; can degrade rubber |
| Electron-beam (E-beam) | 25 kGy | Similar to gamma; no radioactive source |

> [!WARNING]
> Do **not** confuse **sterilisation** with **disinfection**. Sterilisation = absolute destruction of *all* viable microorganisms (SAL ≤ 10⁻⁶). Disinfection only reduces microbial load.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's 1st Law of Leaching | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | J = flux; D = diffusivity; ∂C/∂x = concentration gradient | mol m⁻² s⁻¹; m² s⁻¹; mol m⁻⁴ | Drives solute out of gel |
| 2 | Sherwood Number | $\text{Sh} = \dfrac{k_c \, L}{D}$ | k_c = mass transfer coefficient; L = characteristic length | dimensionless | High Sh ⇒ faster leaching |
| 3 | Drying rate (constant period) | $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = h_c \, A \, (T_\infty - T_s) / \lambda$ | h_c = heat transfer coefficient; λ = latent heat of vaporisation | kg s⁻¹; W m⁻² K⁻¹; J kg⁻¹ | Surface-controlled |
| 4 | Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) | $\text{EMC} = f(T, \text{RH})$ | T = temperature; RH = relative humidity | % | Empirical (BET / GAB isotherms) |
| 5 | Crosslink Density (Flory-Rehner) | $\nu_e = - \dfrac{\ln(1 - v_2) + v_2 + \chi v_2^2}{V_1 (v_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_2}{2})}$ | v₂ = polymer volume fraction; V₁ = molar volume of solvent; χ = interaction parameter | mol cm⁻³ | Determines modulus, swelling |
| 6 | Swelling Ratio | $Q = \dfrac{V_\text{swollen}}{V_\text{dry}}$ | volumes before/after swelling | dimensionless | Higher crosslink ⇒ lower Q |
| 7 | Tensile Strength (empirical) | $\sigma \propto \nu_e^{2/3}$ | ν_e = crosslink density | MPa | Holds for lightly crosslinked rubber |
| 8 | Chlorine Demand | $m_{Cl_2} = V_\text{bath} \times C_\text{avail.}$ | V = bath volume; C = available Cl₂ concentration | g | Used in chlorination tank design |
| 9 | Sterilisation SAL | $\text{SAL} = 10^{-6}$ | absolute sterility assurance level | unitless | For medical devices |
| 10 | Gamma dose | $D = \dot{D} \times t$ | Ḋ = dose rate; t = exposure time | kGy | For irradiation sterilisation |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for 7 After-Treatments:** "**L**ord **D**on't **C**ompromise **S**trict **T**esting **P**rocedures" → **L**eaching, **D**rying, **C**uring, **S**tripping, **T**umbling, **P**owdering, Sterilisation (S). Use it in viva to recall the sequence.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical gloves (surgical/examination):** Leaching → Chlorination (or polymer coating) → Tumbling → Sterilisation (gamma/EtO) → Packaging.
- **Condoms:** Leaching → Drying → Vulcanisation → Electronic testing (leak/dimensional) → Silicone lubrication → Foil packing.
- **Latex threads (used in garment elastic):** Coagulation → Leaching → Drying → Curing (continuous oven) → Winding.
- **Latex foam (mattress/cushion):** Foaming → Gelation → Stripping → Washing → Hot-air drying → Vulcanisation (Dunlop process).
- **Balloon industry:** Dipping → Leaching → Drying → Curing → Halogenation → Tumbling → Printing.

> [!NOTE]
> The "cleaner, safer, donning-friendly" attributes of modern medical gloves are **entirely the result of after-treatments**, not the latex compound itself. This is a frequently asked KTU viva question.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Solute Removal during Leaching (Fickian Model)

We model the wet latex gel as a **flat slab of thickness 2L** (a glove wall) immersed in an infinite, well-stirred bath. Initially, the solute (e.g., soap) is uniformly distributed at concentration $C_0$. At time $t = 0$, the surface concentration drops to zero (well-mixed bath) and we track the average concentration inside the slab.

### 3.1.1 Governing Equation

Fick's second law in one dimension:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \;=\; D \, \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### 3.1.2 Boundary & Initial Conditions

$$C(x,\, 0) \;=\; C_0 \quad \text{(uniform initial loading)}$$

$$C(\pm L,\, t) \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(perfect sink — well-stirred bath)}$$

### 3.1.3 Solution by Separation of Variables

Assume $C(x,t) = X(x)\,T(t)$. Substituting:

$$X(x) \, T'(t) \;=\; D \, X''(x) \, T(t)$$

Dividing both sides by $D\, X(x)\, T(t)$:

$$\frac{T'(t)}{D \, T(t)} \;=\; \frac{X''(x)}{X(x)} \;=\; -\lambda^{2}$$

(The negative sign ensures boundedness, so we set the separation constant $-\lambda^{2}$.)

**Time part:** $T'(t) + D \lambda^{2} T(t) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; T(t) = A \, e^{-D \lambda^{2} t}$

**Space part:** $X''(x) + \lambda^{2} X(x) = 0$

The general solution of the spatial part is $X(x) = B \cos(\lambda x) + E \sin(\lambda x)$.

**Apply boundary conditions** $X(\pm L) = 0$:

$$B \cos(\lambda L) + E \sin(\lambda L) = 0$$
$$B \cos(\lambda L) - E \sin(\lambda L) = 0$$

Subtracting: $2 E \sin(\lambda L) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; E = 0$ (for the non-trivial solution).
Then $B \cos(\lambda L) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda L = \dfrac{(2n-1)\pi}{2},\;\; n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

So $\lambda_n = \dfrac{(2n-1)\pi}{2L}$.

### 3.1.4 Series Solution

$$C(x, t) \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \, \cos\!\left(\lambda_n x\right) \, e^{-D \lambda_n^{2} t}$$

Apply the initial condition $C(x, 0) = C_0$:

$$C_0 \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \cos(\lambda_n x)$$

This is a half-range cosine expansion of a constant. The coefficients are:

$$A_n \;=\; \frac{2}{L} \int_{0}^{L} C_0 \cos(\lambda_n x)\, dx \;=\; \frac{2C_0}{L} \left[\frac{\sin(\lambda_n L)}{\lambda_n}\right]$$

Since $\sin(\lambda_n L) = \sin\!\left(\frac{(2n-1)\pi}{2}\right) = (-1)^{n+1}$:

$$A_n \;=\; \frac{2 C_0}{L} \cdot \frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{\lambda_n} \;=\; \frac{2 C_0}{L} \cdot \frac{(-1)^{n+1} \, 2L}{(2n-1)\pi} \;=\; \frac{4 C_0 \, (-1)^{n+1}}{(2n-1)\pi}$$

### 3.1.5 Final Concentration Distribution

$$\boxed{\,C(x, t) \;=\; \frac{4 C_0}{\pi} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{2n-1} \, \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n-1)\pi x}{2L}\right) \, e^{-D \left(\frac{(2n-1)\pi}{2L}\right)^{2} t}\,}$$

### 3.1.6 Average Concentration (Mass-Averaged)

$$\bar{C}(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} C(x, t)\, dx$$

Using $\int_{0}^{L} \cos(\lambda_n x)\, dx = \dfrac{\sin(\lambda_n L)}{\lambda_n} = \dfrac{(-1)^{n+1}}{\lambda_n}$:

$$\bar{C}(t) \;=\; \frac{4 C_0}{\pi} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n-1)^{2}} \, e^{-D \left(\frac{(2n-1)\pi}{2L}\right)^{2} t}$$

### 3.1.7 Fractional Removal (Engineering Useful Form)

$$\frac{\bar{C}(t)}{C_0} \;=\; \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n-1)^{2}} \, e^{-D \left(\frac{(2n-1)\pi}{2L}\right)^{2} t}$$

**Key Engineering Insight:**

The dominant time constant of the leaching process is the **first term ($n=1$)**. Hence the leaching kinetics follow an approximately **exponential decay**:

$$\boxed{\,\bar{C}(t) \;\approx\; \frac{4 C_0}{\pi^{2}} \, e^{-t / \tau}\,} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau \;=\; \frac{4 L^{2}}{\pi^{2} D}$$

**Interpretation:**
- **Doubling the slab thickness (L)** *quadruples* the leaching time $\tau$.
- **Doubling the diffusivity (D)** *halves* $\tau$.
- This is why **thin, hot, well-agitated baths** are the industrial standard.

### 3.1.8 Numerical Illustration

Take a surgical-glove film of half-thickness $L = 0.15$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, and soap diffusivity in latex $D = 1 \times 10^{-11}$ m² s⁻¹ at 70 °C.

$$\tau = \frac{4 \, (1.5 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 10^{-11}} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8}}{9.87 \times 10^{-11}} \;\approx\; 912 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 15.2 \text{ min}$$

So a 15-minute leach at 70 °C removes ~63 % of the soap (one time-constant). To reach 95 % removal, we need $t \approx 3\tau \approx 45$ min — consistent with industry practice (30 – 60 min leach).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Crosslink Density from Swelling (Flory-Rehner)

For a crosslinked rubber network immersed in a solvent, the Flory-Rehner equation gives the crosslink density $\nu_e$ in terms of the equilibrium swelling ratio $v_2$ (volume fraction of polymer in the swollen gel):

$$\nu_e \;=\; - \frac{\ln(1 - v_2) + v_2 + \chi v_2^{2}}{V_1 \!\left( v_2^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_2}{2} \right)}$$

### 3.2.1 Derivation Sketch

The total Gibbs free energy change on mixing + elastic deformation is set to zero at swelling equilibrium:

$$\Delta G_{\text{mix}} + \Delta G_{\text{elastic}} \;=\; 0$$

**Mixing term (Flory-Huggins):**

$$\frac{\Delta G_{\text{mix}}}{k_B T} \;=\; n_1 \ln(1 - v_2) + n_1 v_2 \, \chi$$

**Elastic term (Flory):**

$$\frac{\Delta G_{\text{elastic}}}{k_B T} \;=\; \frac{3}{2} \nu_e \!\left( v_2^{1/3} - \frac{v_2}{2} \right)$$

Differentiating w.r.t. the number of solvent molecules and setting the chemical potential of the solvent inside the network equal to that in the pure bath gives the boxed result above. (The full derivation is a 3-page standard textbook treatment; for KTU, the boxed formula and the qualitative derivation are sufficient.)

### 3.2.2 Application — Quality Check of Curing

A latex film is cured for $t_1$ and $t_2$ minutes and the equilibrium swelling in toluene is measured:

| Cure time (min) | Mass swollen (g) | Mass dry (g) | Density polymer | Density toluene | $v_2$ | $\nu_e$ (mol m⁻³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 0.42 | 0.30 | 0.92 | 0.87 | 0.61 | 110 |
| 20 | 0.36 | 0.30 | 0.92 | 0.87 | 0.69 | 175 |
| 30 | 0.33 | 0.30 | 0.92 | 0.87 | 0.74 | 225 |

> [!NOTE]
> As cure time increases, $v_2$ rises and $\nu_e$ rises — the network is tighter, the film is stronger and less swellable. **This is the standard experimental verification of cure state used in industry.**

---

## 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix — After-Treatment Effect on Final Properties

| After-Treatment | Tensile Strength | Elongation | Protein Allergy | Surface Tack | Dimensional Stability | Biocompatibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Leaching | ↑ (small) | ↑ | ↓↓ (major effect) | neutral | ↑ | ↑↑ |
| Drying | ↑↑ | ↓ | neutral | ↓ | ↑↑ | neutral |
| Curing | ↑↑↑ | ↓ (peak at optimum) | neutral | ↓↓ | ↑↑↑ | neutral |
| Chlorination | ↑ (slight) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓↓↓ (low friction) | ↑ | ↑ |
| Tumbling | neutral | neutral | neutral | ↓ (smoothens) | neutral | neutral |
| Polymer coating | neutral | neutral | neutral | ↓↓↓ (very low) | neutral | ↑ |
| Sterilisation (gamma) | ↑↑ (further crosslink) | ↓ | ↓ (further denatures) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ (germ-free) |
| Powdering | neutral | neutral | neutral | ↓↓ | neutral | ↓ (granuloma) |

Legend: ↑↑↑ strong increase, ↑ increase, neutral no major effect, ↓ decrease, ↓↓ strong decrease, ↓↓↓ very strong decrease.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall After-Treatment Flow (Dipped Latex Goods)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Form Removal: Wet Gel Article] --> B[Stripping: Remove Bead Ring]
    B --> C[Pre-Leach Rinse: 30-40 C Water]
    C --> D[Hot Leaching: 60-80 C, 15-60 min]
    D --> E[Surface Treatment: Chlorination or Polymer Coat]
    E --> F[Drying: 80-100 C, 20-40 min]
    F --> G[Vulcanisation / Curing: 120-140 C, 20-30 min]
    G --> H[Cooling Zone: 40-50 C]
    H --> I[Tumbling: Wire-mesh Drum]
    I --> J[Powdering or Lubrication: Talc or Silicone]
    J --> K[Inspection: Visual + Leak Test]
    K --> L{Medical Grade?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Sterilisation: Gamma 25 kGy or EtO]
    L -- No --> N[Direct Packing]
    M --> O[Final Packing: Pouch or Box]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Warehouse and Dispatch]
```

## 4.2 Process Architecture of a Continuous Latex-Dipping Production Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DippingTower[Forming Zone]
        DF1[Former Cleaning] --> DF2[Coagulant Dip]
        DF2 --> DF3[Latex Dip Tank]
        DF3 --> DF4[Gelation Oven]
    end
    DippingTower --> LT[Leaching Tunnels]
    LT --> ST[Surface Treatment Station]
    ST --> DT[Drying Oven]
    DT --> CT[Curing Oven]
    CT --> COOL[Cooling Conveyor]
    COOL --> TUM[Tumbling Drums]
    TUM --> LUB[Lubrication or Powdering Booth]
    LUB --> INSP[Inspection Conveyor]

```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Surface Treatment (Chlorination vs Polymer Coating vs Powdering)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[End-Use of Glove?] --> Q1{Medical Surgical?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> CHK1{Protein Allergen Required Less Than 50 ug per g?}
    CHK1 -- Yes --> CHL[Chlorination + Thorough Leaching]
    CHK1 -- No --> PC[Polymer Coating]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Industrial / Household?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> POWD[Online Powdering with Corn Starch]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Speciality: Anti-Static?}

    Q3 -- No --> STD[Standard Halogenation]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GEL[Wet Latex Gel: High Solute C0] -->|Diffusion via Ficks Law| WAT[Hot Water Bath: Low Solute 0]
    WAT -->|Overflow or Recirculation| TANK[Make-up Tank: Constant Low C]
    GEL -->|After Time t| LEACH[Leached Film: Low Solute C0 minus delta]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What are after-treatments in latex technology? List any four after-treatments with one-line purpose for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

After-treatments are the **post-forming finishing operations** carried out on a freshly shaped wet latex article to convert it into a stable, hygienic, and commercially usable product.

| After-Treatment | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Leaching** | Removes water-soluble proteins, soaps, ammonia — reduces allergenicity |
| **Drying** | Removes water, gives dimensional stability, prepares for curing |
| **Curing (Vulcanisation)** | Crosslinks rubber chains, gives strength, elasticity and elasticity |
| **Chlorination** | Halogenates surface, reduces tack, eases donning of gloves |

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for 4 treatments = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between powdering and chlorination as detackification methods for latex gloves.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Powdering | Chlorination |
|---|---|---|
| Agent used | Corn starch / talc / calcium carbonate | Sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) or Cl₂ gas |
| Mechanism | Physical lubrication by powder film | Chemical — replaces C=C with C–Cl, hardens surface |
| Effect on proteins | None | Significant reduction in extractable proteins |
| Donning ease | Moderate; powder residue | Excellent; smooth low-friction surface |
| Health risk | Granuloma, powder-induced allergy | Low; some loss of tensile if overdone |
| Modern relevance | Phased out for medical gloves | Industry standard for surgical gloves |

*(1.5 marks mechanism + 1.5 marks comparison table = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With neat flow diagrams, explain the **after-treatments given to latex dipped goods** in the manufacture of surgical gloves. Discuss the role of **leaching** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **chlorination process** for latex gloves. Discuss its chemistry, process variables, advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) After-treatments for surgical gloves (7 marks):**

The flow sequence is:

$$\text{Dipping} \rightarrow \text{Stripping} \rightarrow \text{Pre-Leach} \rightarrow \text{Hot Leaching} \rightarrow \text{Surface Treatment} \rightarrow \text{Drying} \rightarrow \text{Curing} \rightarrow \text{Tumbling} \rightarrow \text{Sterilisation} \rightarrow \text{Packing}$$

*(1.5 marks for flow diagram / sequence)*

**Role of Leaching — Detailed (5.5 marks):**

1. **Definition:** Leaching is the diffusion-controlled washing of the wet latex gel in hot water (50 – 80 °C) for 15 – 60 minutes to remove water-soluble impurities.
2. **Impurities removed:** residual soaps (potassium oleate, KOH-neutralised fatty acid), proteins, ammonia, free sulphur by-products, excess accelerators, and unbound glycerol.
3. **Mechanism:** Fickian diffusion — solutes move from high concentration inside the gel to the low concentration in the bath, driven by the concentration gradient.
4. **Process variables:**
   - Temperature: 60 – 80 °C (higher T → higher D → faster leaching)
   - Time: at least 3 time-constants (3τ) for >95 % removal
   - Water-to-article ratio: high (avoid bath saturation)
   - Agitation: increases Sherwood number, reduces boundary layer
   - Water quality: deionised / soft (avoid metal staining)
5. **Mathematical relation:** $\tau = \dfrac{4L^2}{\pi^2 D}$. Halving the wall thickness reduces leaching time by 75 %.
6. **Effects on properties:** drastic reduction in **Type-I latex allergy** (protein content drops from ~1000 µg/g to <50 µg/g), improved wet-gel strength, better transparency, and reduced odour.
7. **Industrial setup:** Multi-tank cascade with counter-current water flow for water economy.

---

**(b) Chlorination of Latex Gloves (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Surface chlorination is the controlled chemical treatment of the outer 1–5 µm of a cured latex film using a chlorine donor (usually 0.1 – 0.5 % available chlorine from NaOCl or Cl₂) at acidic pH 2 – 4.

**Chemistry (2 marks):**
The C=C double bond of polyisoprene reacts with chlorine to form chlorinated segments:

$$\text{–CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{Cl}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CH}_2\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)Cl–CHCl–CH}_2\text{–}$$

In acidic aqueous NaOCl:
$$\text{NaOCl} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + \text{NaOH}$$
$$\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl}$$
The active chlorinating species is **HOCl / Cl₂**.

**Process Variables (1.5 marks):**
- Concentration of available Cl₂: 0.1 – 0.5 % w/w
- pH: 2 – 4 (low pH keeps HOCl/Cl₂ form)
- Temperature: 25 – 40 °C
- Immersion time: 30 s – 5 min
- Agitation: gentle to avoid entrapping bubbles

**Process (1 mark):**
Pre-leached + dried + cured glove is immersed in chlorination bath → rinsed with neutralising NaOH/sodium thiosulphate → washed with deionised water → dried.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Excellent donning (low surface friction)
- Drastically reduced extractable proteins → hypo-allergenic
- Smooth, non-tacky surface
- Better chemical resistance
- Eliminates need for powder

**Limitations (0.5 mark):**
- Slight yellowing / discolouration
- Marginal loss of tensile if over-chlorinated
- Disposal of spent chlorination bath (chlorine handling hazard)
- One-time treatment (no regeneration on glove surface)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **drying and curing** of latex dipped goods. Compare **hot-air drying vs microwave drying** for latex films. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **sterilisation methods** used for latex medical products. Compare gamma irradiation with ethylene oxide (EtO) sterilisation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Drying and Curing of Latex Dipped Goods (7 marks):**

**Drying (3.5 marks):**

Drying is the removal of free and bound water from the wet gel. It has three stages:

1. **Constant-rate period:** Surface water evaporates; rate depends on air velocity, temperature, and humidity. The film temperature remains close to the wet-bulb temperature.
2. **Falling-rate period:** Internal moisture diffuses to the surface; rate limited by internal diffusion coefficient of water in rubber.
3. **Equilibrium:** Moisture content approaches the EMC for the operating temperature/humidity.

Drying conditions for latex: **80 – 100 °C** for 20 – 40 minutes in a tunnel oven with **counter-flow hot air**.

**Curing (Vulcanisation) (2.5 marks):**

Curing crosslinks the linear polyisoprene chains using sulphur and accelerators at **120 – 140 °C** for 20 – 30 minutes. The ZnO/accelerator system activates sulphur to form **polysulphide bridges (–Sx–)** between chains. Optimum cure gives maximum tensile, modulus, and ageing resistance; under-cure gives tackiness, over-cure gives reversion (chain scission). The cure state is monitored by **rheometer (t₉₀, scorch time tₛ₂)** and by **swelling index (Flory-Rehner crosslink density)**.

**Hot-Air vs Microwave Drying (1 mark):**

| Feature | Hot-Air | Microwave |
|---|---|---|
| Heating mode | Surface to interior (conduction + convection) | Volumetric (interior heats simultaneously) |
| Cycle time | 20 – 40 min | 2 – 5 min |
| Energy efficiency | Low (mostly exhaust heat) | High (in-material conversion) |
| Uniformity | Risk of case-hardening | More uniform |
| Equipment cost | Low | High (vacuum or tunnel microwave) |
| Suitability for latex | Standard, well-established | Modern, niche (e.g. medical gloves) |

---

**(b) Sterilisation of Latex Medical Products (7 marks):**

**Methods (3 marks):**

1. **Gamma Irradiation** — cobalt-60 source, dose **25 kGy**, kills microbes by DNA scission. No heat, no chemicals.
2. **Ethylene Oxide (EtO) Gas** — 400 – 1200 mg/L EtO at 37 – 63 °C, 1 – 6 hr, 40 – 80 % RH. Effective for heat-sensitive articles.
3. **Electron-Beam (E-beam)** — 25 kGy, similar to gamma but no radioactive source.
4. **Autoclaving** — 121 °C, 15 psi saturated steam for 15 – 30 min. Heat-based; not preferred for latex (degradation).
5. **Plasma Sterilisation** — low-temperature hydrogen peroxide plasma, gentle, newer technology.

**Gamma vs EtO Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | Gamma Irradiation | EtO Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Ionising radiation, DNA damage | Alkylation of proteins and DNA |
| Temperature | Ambient | 37 – 63 °C |
| Cycle time | Hours (continuous in conveyor) | 1 – 6 hr + aeration time |
| Penetrability | Excellent (bulk sterilisation) | Good with humidity preconditioning |
| Effect on latex | Further crosslinking; possible discolouration | Slight swelling, no chemical residue if aerated |
| Toxic residue | None | EtO and ethylene glycol residual — must be aerated |
| Cost | High (source replacement, shielding) | Moderate (gas, aeration chamber) |
| Regulatory status | ISO 11137 (sterilisation standard) | ISO 11135 (sterilisation standard) |
| Environmental | Source decommissioning challenge | EtO is a carcinogen; emissions controlled |

**Why gamma is preferred for latex:** ambient temperature, no toxic residue, full penetration through packaged goods. **Why EtO is still used:** it is gentler on some polymers and pre-existing packaging materials.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks (from KTU 2018–2024 past scripts):**
>
> 1. **Writing "curing = heating"** — WRONG. Curing is the *chemical crosslinking* reaction, not just heating. Heat is the means; the crosslink is the result. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing "drying" with "curing"** — they are TWO separate operations, often in TWO separate ovens. Drying removes water; curing forms crosslinks. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting to mention pH in chlorination** — examiners specifically test whether the student knows that pH 2 – 4 is required to keep chlorine in active form (HOCl / Cl₂). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not writing the Fick's law equation in leaching answers** — for 7-mark questions, examiners expect at least a one-line mathematical relation. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing "sterilisation" with "disinfection"** — sterilisation = complete kill (SAL 10⁻⁶); disinfection = reduction. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Missing the "Sterilisation Assurance Level (SAL) = 10⁻⁶"** when discussing medical-grade sterilisation. (–1 mark)
> 7. **Forgetting to mention protein reduction** as the key benefit of leaching — examiners from 2022 onwards have explicitly looked for this point. (–1 mark)
> 8. **No classification of after-treatments** at the start of long answers — KTU 2024 scheme rewards structured presentation. (–0.5 to –1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — After-Treatments (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** After-treatments = post-forming finishing operations on wet latex gel.
- **Sequence (Mnemonic — "Lord Don't Compromise Strict Testing Procedures"):** Leaching → Drying → Curing → Stripping → Tumbling → (Powdering | Chlorination | Polymer Coating) → Sterilisation → Packing.
- **Leaching** is **diffusion-controlled**; obeys **Fick's 2nd Law**; key time constant $\tau = \dfrac{4L^2}{\pi^2 D}$. Higher T, higher D, thinner wall → faster leaching.
- **Leaching removes:** soaps, proteins, ammonia, accelerator residues — drastically reduces **Type-I latex allergy**.
- **Drying** has 3 stages: constant rate → falling rate → equilibrium (EMC). Typical 80 – 100 °C, 20 – 40 min.
- **Curing (Vulcanisation)** = sulphur crosslinking via ZnO + accelerator; typical 120 – 140 °C, 20 – 30 min. Rheometer: t₉₀ = cure time, tₛ₂ = scorch time.
- **Crosslink density (νₑ)** is measured by **Flory-Rehner swelling equation**. Higher νₑ → tighter network, lower swelling, higher modulus.
- **Stripping** = mechanical removal of bead ring.
- **Tumbling** = polishing in wire-mesh drum with optional slip agent.
- **Powdering** is being **phased out** for medical gloves (granuloma risk).
- **Chlorination** uses **NaOCl or Cl₂ at pH 2–4**; active species is **HOCl / Cl₂**; forms C–Cl bonds; gives low tack and low protein.
- **Polymer coating** (silicone / PU / acrylic) is the **modern alternative** to powder.
- **Sterilisation methods for medical latex:** **Gamma (25 kGy)**, **EtO (ISO 11135)**, **E-beam**, **autoclave (rare for latex)**, **H₂O₂ plasma**.
- **SAL (Sterility Assurance Level)** for medical devices = **10⁻⁶** (absolute sterility).
- **Distinguish:** sterilisation (absolute) ≠ disinfection (reduction).
- **Engineering applications:** surgical gloves, condoms, latex foam, threads, balloons, catheters, blood bags.
- **Crosslink density vs Tensile:** $\sigma \propto \nu_e^{2/3}$ for lightly crosslinked rubber — but over-cure causes reversion and falls off.

> **Key Constants / Numbers to Memorise:**
> - Leaching T: 50 – 80 °C
> - Drying T: 80 – 100 °C
> - Curing T: 120 – 140 °C
> - Chlorination pH: 2 – 4
> - Chlorination Cl₂: 0.1 – 0.5 %
> - Sterilisation dose: 25 kGy
> - SAL: 10⁻⁶
> - Protein content target for low-allergen gloves: < 50 µg/g

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects in Dipped Goods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Defects in Dipped Goods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Defects in Dipped Goods — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dipped Goods:** In latex technology, **dipped goods** are thin-walled rubber articles manufactured by repeatedly immersing (dipping) shaped porcelain or stainless-steel formers into compounded natural rubber latex. The deposited latex film is coagulated, leached, dried, and vulcanized on the former before stripping. Common examples include **surgical gloves, examination gloves, balloons, condoms, catheters, finger cots, and rubber teats**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defect in Dipped Goods:** A **defect** is any deviation from the specified quality parameters — physical, chemical, dimensional, or aesthetic — that renders the finished dipped article non-conforming to the relevant **BIS / ISO / ASTM** specification (e.g., IS 13417 for gloves, ASTM D3578 for rubber gloves).

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames "Defects in Dipped Goods" under the broader module on **latex dipping technology**. Students are expected to identify, classify, and propose remedies for the entire spectrum of in-process and finished-product defects arising from raw-latex quality, compounding, dipping kinetics, coagulation chemistry, drying, vulcanization, and post-cure operations.

## 1.2 Classification of Dipped Goods (Context for Defects)

The defect profile of a dipped article is strongly tied to its end use:

| Category | Examples | Wall Thickness (typical) | Critical Quality Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Examination goods** | Gloves (latex), finger cots | 0.08 – 0.15 mm | Tensile strength, elongation, barrier integrity |
| **Surgical goods** | Surgical gloves, balloons | 0.15 – 0.30 mm | Tensile, elongation, stress at 500% elongation, protein content |
| **Prophylactics** | Condoms | 0.030 – 0.075 mm | Burst volume, burst pressure, freedom from holes |
| **Tubing / Catheters** | Foley catheters, drainage tubes | Variable (1–5 mm) | Kink resistance, dimensional accuracy, biocompatibility |
| **Specialty items** | Teats, swimming caps, toy balloons | 0.20 – 0.50 mm | Elasticity, color uniformity, tear strength |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — the candle-dipping analogy:**
> Imagine dipping a wick repeatedly into melted wax to build up a candle. Each dip deposits a thin film; the thickness grows with the number of dips and the time the wick stays in the wax. Dipped latex goods are made exactly the same way — except the "wax" is **liquid latex compounded with curatives, stabilizers, and colorants**, and the "wick" is a precisely shaped **porcelain former**. Now imagine what could go wrong with a candle: an uneven layer, air bubbles trapped inside, wax that doesn't solidify uniformly, a wick that comes out too thin or too thick, or a colour that streaks. Every one of those problems has an *exact parallel* in dipped latex goods — that is the universe of **defects in dipped goods** we are about to study.

## 1.4 Why Defect Analysis Matters in KTU Context

From an engineering-economics standpoint, dipped-goods manufacturing is **waste-prone**: even a 2 % reject rate translates to significant revenue loss at production volumes of millions of gloves per day. KTU examiners therefore place weight on:
1. **Cause identification** — connecting the defect to its root process step.
2. **Remedial action** — corrective measures in compounding, dipping, or curing.
3. **Quality control** — in-line and finished-product testing as per standards.

The next sections dissect these defects, their root causes, and their remedies in KTU-board style.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Defects

The KTU syllabus recognizes a four-tier classification:

### A. Raw-Latex / Compounding-Stage Defects
Originating from the latex compound *before* it touches the former.

### B. Dipping / Coagulation-Stage Defects
Originating from the dipping tank, coagulant bath, dwell time, or former condition.

### C. Drying / Vulcanization-Stage Defects
Originating from the oven, leaching tank, or curing cycle.

### D. Post-Vulcanization / Stripping Defects
Originating from beading, stripping, surface treatment, or storage.

## 2.2 Exhaustive Defect Catalog with Cause–Effect Analysis

### 2.2.1 Foam / Bubbles in the Film

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Surface craters, pinholes, frothy surface |
| **Root cause** | Excessive mechanical agitation, air entrainment during compounding, low viscosity, surfactant imbalance |
| **Mechanism** | Air bubbles get trapped against the former; when latex gels around them, they rupture during drying, leaving craters |
| **Remedy** | Add **anti-foam agent** (silicone emulsion, 0.05–0.1 phr), allow 24 h maturation, degas under vacuum, control stirrer speed |
| **KTU reference** | ASTM D1076 — total solid content and mechanical stability |

### 2.2.2 Cratering

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Small bowl-shaped depressions on the surface |
| **Root cause** | High surface tension, contaminated former (oil/grease), too-rapid drying |
| **Remedy** | Clean former with alkaline detergent, slow ramp in drying oven, add wetting agent |

### 2.2.3 Pinholes (Holiday Defects)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Microscopic holes visible only under backlight/electrical continuity test |
| **Root cause** | Dust on former, gel particles in latex, insufficient leaching, poor coagulant coverage |
| **Detection** | Water leak test, electrical holiday detector (e.g., 10 kV for gloves) |
| **Remedy** | Filter latex through 80-mesh, ISO 14644-1 Class 7 clean room, optimize coagulant dip |

### 2.2.4 Streaking / Striations

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Longitudinal lines parallel to withdrawal direction |
| **Root cause** | Improper former withdrawal speed (too fast ⇒ laminar flow instability), uneven coagulant film, vibration in conveyor |
| **Remedy** | Maintain withdrawal speed at **2–5 cm/s** for thin gloves, use vibrating-free dipping line |

### 2.2.5 Orange-Peel Texture

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Rough, dimpled surface resembling an orange peel |
| **Root cause** | Too-rapid coagulation, high calcium nitrate concentration, insufficient maturation |
| **Remedy** | Reduce coagulant concentration (typical **5–20 % calcium nitrate**), increase dwell time |

### 2.2.6 Tackiness / Sticking

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Surface remains sticky after vulcanization |
| **Root cause** | Under-cure, missing zinc oxide, missing sulphur, plasticizer migration |
| **Remedy** | Optimize cure time, verify ZnO (0.5–1.0 phr) and S (0.5–1.5 phr), check accelerator balance |

### 2.2.7 Blisters (Macro-Voids)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Raised hemispherical bubbles 1–10 mm |
| **Root cause** | Trapped air between former and latex film, leaching water temperature too high causing steam formation |
| **Remedy** | Hot-leach at 60–70 °C gradually, smooth former profile, angle former on entry |

### 2.2.8 Beading Defects (for Gloves)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Irregular, weak, or absent rolled bead at cuff |
| **Root cause** | Bead-roller mis-alignment, incorrect former design at cuff, low-viscosity latex |
| **Remedy** | Re-align roller, use serrated former edge, control total solids (TSC) at **30–35 %** |

### 2.2.9 Thickness Variation

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Non-uniform wall thickness; critical for gloves (spec: 0.08 ± 0.02 mm at fingertip for exam gloves) |
| **Root cause** | Former wear, dipping speed fluctuation, uneven coagulant |
| **Remedy** | Periodic former calibration with gauge, use **automatic dipping lines** with servo-controlled withdrawal |

### 2.2.10 Discoloration / Yellowing

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Yellow, brown, or grey patches |
| **Root cause** | Antioxidant migration, copper/manganese contamination, over-cure, UV exposure |
| **Remedy** | Use phenolic antioxidants (BHT), avoid metal contamination, control cure temperature |

### 2.2.11 Low Tensile / Elongation

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Fails tensile test (ASTM D412) |
| **Root cause** | Under-vulcanization, excessive prevulcanization, plasticizer overdose, low NR content |
| **Remedy** | Re-optimize cure (typical **120 °C × 20–30 min**), verify prevulcanization degree by chloroform number (target **2–3**) |

### 2.2.12 Protein / Allergen Residue

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Not visible; detected by chemical assay |
| **Root cause** | Inadequate leaching, residual NRL proteins |
| **Remedy** | Double leaching (hot water + chlorination), use **enzymatic deproteinization**, target < 50 µg/g (ASTM D5712) |

### 2.2.13 Over-Cure / Burnt Film

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Brittle, dark-coloured, cracked film |
| **Root cause** | Oven temperature too high, residence time too long |
| **Remedy** | Adhere to cure schedule, install oven thermocouples |

### 2.2.14 Tearing at Stripping

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Tears at the cuff, fingertip, or thin sections |
| **Root cause** | Insufficient powder, wrong stripping angle, over-cured brittle film |
| **Remedy** | Use talc/cornstarch slurry or chlorination for easy stripping |

### 2.2.15 Odour

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Strong rubber/chemical smell |
| **Root cause** | Residual ammonia, accelerator residues, vulcanization fumes |
| **Remedy** | Post-cure leaching, activated-carbon filtration, low-odour accelerators (ZDEC instead of ZDBC) |

## 2.3 Process Parameters Causing Defects — Cause-Effect Map

| Process Step | Critical Parameter | Typical Range | Defect if Out of Spec |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latex maturation** | Time, temperature | 24–72 h at 25 °C | Foam, orange-peel |
| **Total Solid Content (TSC)** | Mass fraction | 30–55 % | Cratering, thickness variation |
| **Viscosity (Brookfield)** | Spindle / rpm dependent | 30–150 mPa·s | Dripping, sagging |
| **Coagulant concentration** | Ca(NO₃)₂ in alcohol | 5–25 % | Streaking, pinholes |
| **Dipping speed (entry)** | cm/s | 1–3 cm/s | Bubbles, splash marks |
| **Withdrawal speed** | cm/s | 2–5 cm/s | Thickness variation, orange peel |
| **Dwell in latex** | seconds | 6–60 s | Wall thickness |
| **Drying temperature** | °C | 80–120 °C | Blisters, over-cure |
| **Cure temperature** | °C | 110–140 °C | Under-cure / over-cure |
| **Cure time** | min | 15–45 min | Tensile / elongation |
| **Leaching** | water, 60–70 °C, 5–15 min | – | Protein, chemicals |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the high-yield formulae for Module 3 (Defects in Dipped Goods). They are examinable in both Part A (definition/recall) and Part B (numerical) form.

| Equation | Variables / Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $$\text{TSC} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{dry rubber}}}{m_{\text{latex}}} \times 100 \,\%$$ | $m_{\text{dry rubber}}$ = mass of dried film (g); $m_{\text{latex}}$ = mass of wet latex (g); result in % | **Total Solid Content** — controls film thickness per dip. A low TSC gives thin, defect-prone films; high TSC gives sagging. |
| $$t_{\text{film}} \;=\; k \cdot \sqrt{\eta \, t_{\text{dwell}}}$$ | $t_{\text{film}}$ = wet film thickness (m); $\eta$ = latex viscosity (Pa·s); $t_{\text{dwell}}$ = dwell time (s); $k$ = process constant | **Wet-film build-up law** — explains why longer dwell gives thicker film and why viscosity must be controlled. |
| $$\text{Wall thickness (dry)} \;=\; t_{\text{film}} \times \text{TSC} \times (1 - \text{shrinkage})$$ | All ratios dimensionless | Used to calculate number of dips required for target gauge. |
| $$\text{Chloroform number} \;=\; \frac{\text{Volume of swollen gel}}{\text{Original volume}}$$ | Dimensionless; typical 2–3 | **Degree of prevulcanization** — too high ⇒ brittle film; too low ⇒ green strength poor. |
| $$\sigma_{\text{tensile}} \;=\; \frac{F_{\max}}{A_0}$$ | $\sigma_{\text{tensile}}$ in MPa; $F_{\max}$ in N; $A_0$ in m² | ASTM D412 tensile test — must meet $\geq$ 14 MPa (exam gloves). |
| $$\varepsilon_{\text{break}} \;=\; \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 \,\%$$ | $L_f$ final length, $L_0$ original; result in % | Elongation at break — must meet $\geq$ 500 % (exam gloves). |
| $$P_{\text{burst}} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{\text{hoop}}}{D}$$ | $P_{\text{burst}}$ in kPa; $t$ = wall thickness (mm); $D$ = diameter (mm); $\sigma_{\text{hoop}}$ in MPa | **Thin-walled pressure vessel analogy** for condom/catheter burst pressure. |
| $$\text{Water leak test pass criterion} \;:\; \text{pinhole count} \;=\; 0$$ | Pieces inspected per AQL 1.5 or 2.5 | Binomial sampling criterion for gloves per ISO 2859-1. |
| $$\eta_{\text{apparent}} \;=\; \eta_0 \, \big(1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2\big)^{\frac{n-1}{2}}$$ | Carreau model: $\eta_0$ zero-shear viscosity, $\lambda$ relaxation time, $n$ power-law index | Latex is **shear-thinning**; viscosity drops under agitation. |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Beyond the Exam

> [!TIP]
> - **Medical industry**: defect analysis underpins the qualification of surgical gloves per **ISO 10282** and examination gloves per **ASTM D3578 / EN 455**.
> - **Prophylactics**: defect-driven burst-volume failures relate directly to the **pressure-vessel formula** above — a key design parameter.
> - **Process control**: SPC charts on TSC, viscosity, pH, and tensile strength reduce reject rate from ~5 % to under 0.5 % in modern plants.
> - **Regulatory**: FDA 21 CFR 880.6250 and EU MDR 2017/745 require defect-rate disclosure for medical gloves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wall Thickness from a Single Dip (Wet-Film Build-Up)

**Statement.** Show that the wet-film thickness deposited on a vertical former being withdrawn from a latex bath follows the relation $t_{\text{film}} = k \sqrt{\eta \, t_{\text{dwell}}}$ in the diffusion-limited regime.

**Given.**
- Newtonian film drainage model on a flat vertical plate.
- Viscosity $\eta$ of latex (Pa·s).
- Dwell time $t_{\text{dwell}}$ (s) the former spends in the latex.
- Process constant $k$ incorporating surface tension, density, and former geometry.

**Step 1.** The Navier-Stokes equation for thin-film drainage on a vertical plate under gravity reduces to:

$$\mu \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2} \;=\; \rho g$$

**Step 2.** Integrating twice with no-slip at the plate and zero shear at the film surface:

$$v_x(z) \;=\; \frac{\rho g}{2 \mu} \, z \, (2 h - z)$$

where $h$ is the film thickness and $z$ is the coordinate normal to the plate.

**Step 3.** The volumetric flow rate per unit width:

$$Q \;=\; \int_0^h v_x \, dz \;=\; \frac{\rho g \, h^3}{3 \mu}$$

**Step 4.** Conservation of mass for a draining film on a stationary former:

$$\frac{dh}{dt_{\text{dwell}}} \;=\; -\frac{Q}{h} \;=\; -\frac{\rho g \, h^2}{3 \mu}$$

**Step 5.** Separate variables:

$$h^{-2} \, dh \;=\; -\frac{\rho g}{3 \mu} \, dt_{\text{dwell}}$$

**Step 6.** Integrate from initial film thickness $h_0$ at $t=0$ to $h_f$ at $t = t_{\text{dwell}}$:

$$\int_{h_0}^{h_f} h^{-2} \, dh \;=\; -\frac{\rho g}{3 \mu} \int_0^{t_{\text{dwell}}} dt$$

$$\frac{1}{h_0} - \frac{1}{h_f} \;=\; \frac{\rho g \, t_{\text{dwell}}}{3 \mu}$$

**Step 7.** In the limit $h_0 \gg h_f$ (long drainage, i.e., long dwell), the term $1/h_0$ becomes negligible:

$$\frac{1}{h_f} \;\approx\; \frac{\rho g \, t_{\text{dwell}}}{3 \mu} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad h_f \;\approx\; \frac{3 \mu}{\rho g \, t_{\text{dwell}}}$$

**Step 8.** Re-arranging in terms of film thickness vs. viscosity and dwell:

$$t_{\text{film}} \;\equiv\; h_f \;\propto\; \frac{\eta}{t_{\text{dwell}}} \quad\text{(drainage-dominated)}$$

Combining with the diffusion-limited gel-formation law $t_{\text{film}} \propto \sqrt{\eta \, t_{\text{dwell}}}$ (Landau-Levich-Derjaguin), the empirical constant $k$ is calibrated per plant:

$$t_{\text{film}} \;=\; k \, \sqrt{\eta \, t_{\text{dwell}}}$$

**Engineering interpretation.** A defect (e.g., **orange-peel** or **streaking**) appears when $\eta$ is non-uniform across the dip (e.g., due to temperature gradient or partial gelation in the bath), giving spatially varying $t_{\text{film}}$ and hence uneven wall thickness.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Burst Pressure of a Dipped Condom / Thin-Wall Tube

**Statement.** Derive the hoop-stress-based burst pressure of a thin-walled dipped cylindrical article.

**Given.**
- Wall thickness $t$ (mm).
- Internal diameter $D$ (mm).
- Hoop tensile strength $\sigma_{\text{hoop}}$ (MPa).

**Step 1.** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of radius $r = D/2$ and wall $t$ under internal pressure $P_{\text{burst}}$.

**Step 2.** Force balance on a longitudinal half-section of unit length:

$$P_{\text{burst}} \cdot D \cdot 1 \;=\; 2 \cdot t \cdot 1 \cdot \sigma_{\text{hoop}}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $P_{\text{burst}}$:

$$P_{\text{burst}} \;=\; \frac{2 \, t \, \sigma_{\text{hoop}}}{D}$$

**Step 4.** For a condom with typical $D = 32$ mm, $t = 0.060$ mm, $\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = 25$ MPa:

$$P_{\text{burst}} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.060 \times 25}{32} \;\approx\; 0.094 \text{ kPa (per unit stress)}$$

Multiplying by the actual latex failure stress in MPa gives the burst pressure.

**Engineering interpretation.** A defect such as a **pinhole** or **thin spot** reduces $t$ locally, dramatically lowering $P_{\text{burst}}$ — this is why AQL sampling for condoms is among the strictest in any dipped-goods category (AQL 0.25).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Protein-Leaching Removal Efficiency

**Statement.** Show that a two-stage counter-current leaching reduces residual protein more effectively than a single-stage wash.

**Given.**
- Initial protein concentration $C_0$ (µg/g).
- Single-stage removal fraction $f$ per wash.
- $n$ wash stages.

**Step 1.** Single-stage residual:

$$C_1 \;=\; C_0 \, (1 - f)$$

**Step 2.** Two-stage counter-current residual:

$$C_2 \;=\; C_0 \, (1 - f)^2$$

**Step 3.** For $n$ stages:

$$C_n \;=\; C_0 \, (1 - f)^n$$

**Step 4.** Example: $C_0 = 1000$ µg/g, $f = 0.5$ (50 % per wash).

Single wash:

$$C_1 \;=\; 1000 \times 0.5 \;=\; 500 \, \mu\text{g/g}$$

Two-stage counter-current:

$$C_2 \;=\; 1000 \times 0.5^2 \;=\; 250 \, \mu\text{g/g}$$

Three-stage:

$$C_3 \;=\; 1000 \times 0.5^3 \;=\; 125 \, \mu\text{g/g}$$

**Engineering interpretation.** To reach the ASTM D5712 / EN 455-1 limit of < 50 µg/g extractable protein, multi-stage leaching is essential — this is a **defect-prevention strategy** rather than a corrective one.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SPC Control Limits for TSC

**Statement.** Compute the upper and lower control limits (UCL / LCL) for Total Solid Content at 3σ.

**Given.** Mean TSC $\bar{x} = 33 \,\%$; standard deviation $\sigma = 0.8 \,\%$.

**Step 1.** UCL:

$$\text{UCL} \;=\; \bar{x} + 3 \sigma \;=\; 33 + 3 \times 0.8 \;=\; 35.4 \,\%$$

**Step 2.** LCL:

$$\text{LCL} \;=\; \bar{x} - 3 \sigma \;=\; 33 - 3 \times 0.8 \;=\; 30.6 \,\%$$

**Step 3.** Action rule: any batch with TSC outside $[30.6, 35.4]\,\%$ is flagged and not released for dipping.

**Engineering interpretation.** Operating outside these limits statistically correlates with **thickness variation, cratering, and sagging** defects.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Test → Remedy

| # | Defect | Primary Cause | Diagnostic Test | Primary Remedy | Secondary Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinholes | Dust, gel particles | Electrical holiday test, water leak | Filter latex (80-mesh), clean room | Coagulant uniformity |
| 2 | Cratering | Former contamination | Visual at 10× | Clean former, slow dry | TSC check |
| 3 | Foam | Air entrainment | Density / TSC variance | Anti-foam, vacuum de-gas | Mechanical stability time |
| 4 | Orange peel | Rapid gelation | Surface roughness meter | Lower coagulant strength | Maturation time |
| 5 | Streaks | Withdrawal speed | Thickness map | Servo-controlled withdrawal | Conveyor vibration log |
| 6 | Blisters | Leaching water too hot | Visual cross-section | Lower leach T to 60 °C | Steam trap on oven |
| 7 | Tackiness | Under-cure | Swab with toluene | Increase cure time 5 min | Verify ZnO/S |
| 8 | Over-cure | Excess cure | DSC, hardness shore A | Reset oven temp | Conveyor speed |
| 9 | Brittle tear | Prevulcanization too high | Chloroform number | Reduce maturation T by 5 °C | Re-test chloroform |
| 10 | Bead defect | Roller mis-align | Bead diameter gauge | Re-align bead roller | Cuff former inspection |
| 11 | Thickness off | Viscosity / dwell | Gravimetric | Calibrate viscosity | Former wear check |
| 12 | Yellowing | Antioxidant migration | Colourimeter | Switch to phenolic AO | UV-shielded packing |
| 13 | Low tensile | Cure imbalance | Tensile test ASTM D412 | Re-balance accelerator | Verify prevulc |
| 14 | High protein | Inadequate leaching | ASTM D5712 (modified Lowry) | Add 2nd leach stage | Chlorination |
| 15 | Odour | Residual ammonia | Olfactory panel | Post-cure water soak | Carbon-filtered air |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Process Flow with Defect-Generation Hotspots

The mermaid diagram below maps each dipped-goods process step to the defects that originate there. This is the schematic KTU examiners expect students to draw for full marks in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw NR Latex] --> B[Compounding Tank]
    B --> C[Maturation 24 to 72 h]
    C --> D[Viscosity and TSC QC]
    D --> E[Dipping Tank Latex]
    E --> F[Former Entry Dip]
    F --> G[Coagulant Dip Ca NO3 2]
    G --> H[Dwell and Withdrawal]
    H --> I[Leaching Tank]
    I --> J[Drying Oven 80 to 120 C]
    J --> K[Vulcanization 110 to 140 C]
    K --> L[Beading and Stripping]
    L --> M[Post Cure Treatments]
    M --> N[QC Inspection AQL]
    N --> O[Packaging]

    B -.->|Defect Foam| X[Defect Foam]
    C -.->|Defect Orange Peel| Y[Defect Orange Peel]
    F -.->|Defect Pinhole Bubble| Z[Defect Pinhole and Bubble]
    G -.->|Defect Streaking| W[Defect Streaking]
    H -.->|Defect Thickness Variation| V[Defect Thickness Variation]
    J -.->|Defect Blister Over Cure| U[Defect Blister and Over Cure]
    K -.->|Defect Under Cure Tackiness| T[Defect Under Cure and Tackiness]
    L -.->|Defect Bead Tear| S[Defect Bead Tear]
    M -.->|Defect Protein Odour| R[Defect Protein and Odour]
    N -.->|Defect AQL Reject| Q[Defect AQL Reject]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Fishbone (Ishikawa) of Defects in Dipped Goods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT[Defect in Dipped Goods] --> M1[Man]
    ROOT --> M2[Machine]
    ROOT --> M3[Material]
    ROOT --> M4[Method]
    ROOT --> M5[Measurement]
    ROOT --> M6[Environment]

    M1 --> M1a[Operator skill]
    M1 --> M1b[Stripping technique]

    M2 --> M2a[Former wear]
    M2 --> M2b[Conveyor vibration]
    M2 --> M2c[Oven hot spots]

    M3 --> M3a[Latex TSC variance]
    M3 --> M3b[Coagulant contamination]
    M3 --> M3c[Antioxidant batch]

    M4 --> M4a[Withdrawal speed]
    M4 --> M4b[Dwell time]
    M4 --> M4c[Cure schedule]

    M5 --> M5a[Viscometer calibration]
    M5 --> M5b[Thickness gauge]

    M6 --> M6a[Ambient humidity]
    M6 --> M6b[Cleanroom class]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Block Functional Architecture of an Online Defect-Detection Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Former on Conveyor] --> B[Dipping Station]
    B --> C[Coagulation Station]
    C --> D[Leaching Station]
    D --> E[Drying Oven]
    E --> F[Curing Oven]
    F --> G[Beading Station]
    G --> H[Stripping Station]
    H --> I[Vision Camera Station]
    I --> J[Thickness Gauge]
    J --> K[Holiday Detector]
    K --> L{Reject Decision}
    L -->|Pass| M[Packaging Line]
    L -->|Fail| N[Reject Bin with Reason Code]

    I -->|Pinhole Streak Defect| L
    J -->|Out of Gauge| L
    K -->|Electrical Fail| L
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Defect-Cause Relationship Matrix (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CAUSES[Root Causes]
        C1[Viscosity Drift]
        C2[Coagulant Age]
        C3[Oven Hot Spot]
        C4[Prevulcanization Excess]
        C5[Leach Water Hardness]
        C6[Former Roughness]
    end

    subgraph DEFECTS[Observed Defects]
        D1[Thickness Variation]
        D2[Streaking]
        D3[Blister]
        D4[Brittle Tear]
        D5[High Protein]
        D6[Pinhole]
    end

    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
    C5 --> D5
    C6 --> D6
```

## 4.5 Diagram E — Quality Control Decision Tree for a Defective Glove

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gloves from Line] --> B{Visual Defect?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Pinhole or Tear?}
    B -->|No| D{Thickness in Spec?}
    C -->|Yes| E[Reject Critical]
    C -->|No| F[Reject Major]
    D -->|Yes| G{Pass Tensile Test?}
    D -->|No| H[Reject Minor Gauge]
    G -->|Yes| I{Pass Protein Test?}
    G -->|No| E
    I -->|Yes| J[Accept and Pack]
    I -->|No| K[Reject Allergen Limit]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> List and briefly explain **three major surface defects** encountered in dipped latex goods such as surgical gloves.

**Model Answer (3 marks: 1 mark each):**

1. **Pinholes / Holiday defects** — Microscopic holes formed by dust or gel particles trapped between the former and the latex film. They compromise barrier integrity and are detected by an electrical holiday detector or water-leak test (per ASTM D5151).
2. **Cratering** — Small bowl-shaped depressions caused by contaminated former surfaces, high surface tension of the latex, or excessively fast drying. Remedied by proper former cleaning and a controlled drying ramp.
3. **Orange-peel texture** — Rough, dimpled surface caused by overly rapid gelation due to high coagulant concentration. Remedied by reducing calcium nitrate concentration and increasing maturation time.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

> What is **"charring" or over-cure** in dipped goods? Mention its causes and one corrective measure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Charring / over-cure is the condition in which the dipped latex film is exposed to temperatures or durations exceeding the optimum cure schedule, producing a brittle, darkened, and cracked product.
- **Causes (1 mark):** Excess oven temperature, long residence time, drift in conveyor speed, or a malfunctioning thermocouple.
- **Corrective measure (1 mark):** Re-calibrate the oven thermocouples, reset the cure schedule to the standard 110–140 °C for 15–45 min, and conduct a hardness (Shore A) check on the next batch.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for writing only the *name* of the defect without stating its **root cause**.
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for stating a remedy without connecting it to a **specific test or specification**.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for not mentioning the relevant **ASTM / IS / ISO standard**.
> - Always present answers in a **defect-cause-test-remedy** table; this alone gains 2 extra marks versus a plain paragraph.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Classify the major defects encountered in dipped latex goods. With a neat labelled diagram of the dipping process, indicate the stage at which each defect originates. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss in detail the **causes, detection methods, and remedies** for any **five defects** you listed in part (a). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Classification of defects (3 marks):**

| Class | Examples | Stage of origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Compounding defects** | Foam, viscosity drift | Compounding tank |
| **Coagulation defects** | Pinhole, streaking, orange peel | Coagulant dip |
| **Drying / curing defects** | Blisters, tackiness, over-cure | Oven / curing line |
| **Dimensional defects** | Thickness variation, bead failure | Dwell / withdrawal / beader |
| **Post-cure defects** | Protein residue, odour, tearing | Leaching / stripping |

**Neat labelled diagram of dipping process (2 marks):** See Diagram A in Section 4.1 above — students should draw a flow chart showing *Raw latex → Compounding → Maturation → Dipping → Coagulation → Leaching → Drying → Vulcanization → Stripping → QC* with defect-generation hotspots annotated.

**Stage-origin mapping (2 marks):** Show at least five defect-to-stage arrows (e.g., pinhole → coagulation, blister → drying oven, tackiness → vulcanization, bead failure → beading station, protein → leaching).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Choose any five from the matrix of Section 3.5. Model answers below.

1. **Pinholes (1.5 marks):** Caused by dust on former or gel particles; detected by **ASTM D5151 water-leak test**; prevented by 80-mesh filtration and clean-room dipping (ISO Class 7).
2. **Blisters (1.5 marks):** Caused by trapped air or leach water > 70 °C forming steam; detected visually; prevented by angled former entry and reducing leach temperature.
3. **Streaking (1.0 mark):** Caused by uneven withdrawal speed; detected by thickness profile; prevented by servo-controlled withdrawal at 2–5 cm/s.
4. **Tackiness (1.0 mark):** Caused by under-cure; detected by toluene-swab test or DSC; prevented by re-balancing ZnO and S and extending cure time by 5 min.
5. **High protein residue (2.0 marks):** Caused by inadequate leaching; detected by **ASTM D5712 (modified Lowry)**; prevented by two-stage counter-current leaching and enzymatic deproteinization.

(Standards and quantitative parameters cited above contribute the missing 1–2 marks examiners typically award for "extra depth".)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

> **(a)** With the help of a **flow diagram**, explain the **manufacturing process** of dipped latex gloves, identifying the steps at which **typical defects** may arise. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe, with reference to **ASTM / IS standards**, the **quality-control tests** performed on dipped goods to detect **pinholes, tensile failure, and protein residue**. Propose **remedial actions** for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Process flow (4 marks):**

$$\text{Latex} \;\xrightarrow{\text{compounding}}\; \text{Compound} \;\xrightarrow{\text{maturation}}\; \text{Mature Latex} \;\xrightarrow{\text{dip}}\; \text{Wet Film} \;\xrightarrow{\text{coagulant}}\; \text{Gelled Film} \;\xrightarrow{\text{leach}}\; \text{Washed Film} \;\xrightarrow{\text{dry}}\; \text{Dry Film} \;\xrightarrow{\text{cure}}\; \text{Cured Film} \;\xrightarrow{\text{bead and strip}}\; \text{Glove}$$

Annotate each arrow with the **parameter** (TSC, viscosity, dwell time, temperature) and the **defect hotspot**.

**Typical defects at each stage (3 marks):**

| Stage | Defect |
|---|---|
| Compounding | Foam (air entrainment) |
| Maturation | Viscosity drift, orange-peel |
| Dipping | Pinhole, thickness variation |
| Coagulant | Streaking, craters |
| Leaching | Protein residue, odour |
| Drying | Blisters, over-dry |
| Vulcanization | Under-cure / over-cure |
| Beading / Stripping | Bead defect, tear |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Three QC tests (2 marks each = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for tabular presentation):**

1. **Pinhole test — ASTM D5151 / IS 15354:** Fill glove with 1000 mL water, observe for leaks; AQL 1.5 per ISO 2859-1. *Remedy:* filter latex, clean room, holiday detector at 10 kV.
2. **Tensile test — ASTM D412 / IS 3400:** Dumb-bell specimens, must achieve ≥ 14 MPa tensile and ≥ 500 % elongation at break. *Remedy:* re-balance ZnO/S, optimize cure.
3. **Protein test — ASTM D5712 (Modified Lowry) / EN 455-1:** Target < 50 µg/g extractable protein. *Remedy:* two-stage counter-current leaching and chlorination.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Defects in Dipped Goods**

- **Dipped goods** are made by dipping formers into compounded latex; they include gloves, condoms, balloons, catheters, and teats.
- **Four broad classes** of defects: compounding-stage, dipping-stage, drying/curing-stage, and post-cure.
- **Pinholes** — caused by dust/gel; detected by ASTM D5151 water-leak test; prevented by filtration and clean room.
- **Cratering** — caused by former contamination; remedy: clean former, slow drying ramp.
- **Orange peel** — caused by rapid gelation; remedy: lower Ca(NO₃)₂ concentration (5–25 %).
- **Foam / bubbles** — caused by air entrainment; remedy: anti-foam (0.05–0.1 phr), vacuum de-gas.
- **Streaking** — caused by uneven withdrawal; remedy: servo-controlled withdrawal at **2–5 cm/s**.
- **Blisters** — caused by trapped air or hot leach water (>70 °C); remedy: angled former entry, lower leach T.
- **Tackiness** — caused by under-cure; remedy: re-balance ZnO (0.5–1 phr) and S (0.5–1.5 phr).
- **Over-cure / charring** — caused by oven hot spot; remedy: thermocouple calibration, cure schedule reset.
- **Bead defects** — caused by mis-aligned bead roller; remedy: re-align, control TSC at 30–35 %.
- **Thickness variation** — caused by viscosity drift and former wear; remedy: periodic former calibration.
- **Yellowing** — caused by antioxidant migration or UV; remedy: phenolic AO, UV-shielded packing.
- **Low tensile** — caused by under-cure or low NR; target ≥ 14 MPa per ASTM D3578.
- **High protein** — caused by inadequate leaching; target < 50 µg/g per ASTM D5712 / EN 455-1.
- **Odour** — caused by residual ammonia; remedy: post-cure water soak, carbon-filtered air.
- **Tearing at stripping** — caused by over-cure or insufficient powder; remedy: talc slurry or chlorination.
