**Step 2 — Capillary Uptake**
- Once the contact angle is below 90°, the latex spontaneously invades the porous structure.
- The driving pressure difference across a curved meniscus is given by the **Young–Laplace relation**:

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = capillary pressure driving impregnation (Pa)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid-vapor surface tension of latex compound (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle between latex and substrate (degrees)
- $r$ = effective pore radius of substrate (m)

**Step 3 — Diffusion (Pick's Law of Penetration)**
- Depth of penetration follows a square-root-of-time law (analogous to Washburn/Pick's equation):

$$h(t) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{r \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2\eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

where:
- $h(t)$ = penetration depth at time $t$ (m)
- $\eta$ = viscosity of latex compound (Pa·s)
- $t$ = contact time (s)

**Step 4 — Coagulation / Gelling**
- The saturated substrate passes through a coagulant bath (typically Ca(NO₃)₂ or acetic acid).
- Ca²⁺ ions destabilize the electrical double layer around latex particles → coalescence.

**Step 5 — Drying & Vulcanization**
- Water is removed at 80–120 °C.
- Vulcanization occurs at 110–140 °C (sulfur + accelerator system).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$ | Young–Laplace capillary driving pressure | Pa |
| 2 | $h(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{r\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{2\eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | Pick's penetration depth law | m |
| 3 | $\text{Pick-up (\%)} = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | Mass-based saturation indicator | % |
| 4 | $Q = -\dfrac{k}{\eta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Darcy flow through porous substrate | m³/s |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fickian diffusion of latex particles | kg/(m²·s) |
| 6 | $t_{gel} \propto \dfrac{1}{[Ca^{2+}]}$ | Coagulation kinetics (inverse of ion conc.) | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Impregnation is the *only step* in latex goods manufacture where a *liquid-phase chemical reaction* (coagulation) and a *solid-state mechanical property* (wet gel strength) interact directly. This is why it is heavily tested in KTU Module 3.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Tyre manufacturing** — Tyre cords (rayon, nylon, polyester, steel) are impregnated with RFL to ensure rubber-cord adhesion.
- **Latex thread production** — Continuous extrusion followed by impregnation with coagulant.
- **Dipped foam products** — Mattresses, pillows, cushioning foam.
- **Industrial hoses & conveyor belts** — Fabric reinforcement requires impregnation.
- **Biomedical devices** — Catheters, surgical gloves (latex film acts as barrier).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pick's Penetration Depth Law

Starting from the **Young–Laplace capillary pressure**:

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 1 — Express flow in a cylindrical capillary using Hagen–Poiseuille:**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi r^{4}}{8\eta}\,\frac{\Delta P}{h(t)}$$

where $h(t)$ is the instantaneous length of the wetted column inside the capillary at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Express $Q$ as the rate of change of wetted volume:**

$$Q \;=\; \pi r^{2}\,\frac{dh}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Equate both expressions of $Q$:**

$$\pi r^{2}\,\frac{dh}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\pi r^{4}}{8\eta}\,\frac{\Delta P}{h(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta P$ from Young–Laplace:**

$$\pi r^{2}\,\frac{dh}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\pi r^{4}}{8\eta}\,\frac{1}{h(t)}\cdot\frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the right-hand side:**

$$\pi r^{2}\,\frac{dh}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\pi r^{3}}{4\eta}\,\frac{\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{h(t)}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply both sides by $h(t)$ and divide by $\pi r^{2}$:**

$$h(t)\,\frac{dh}{dt} \;=\; \frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{4\eta}$$

**Step 7 — Separate variables and integrate (with $h(0) = 0$):**

$$\int_{0}^{h(t)} h \, dh \;=\; \int_{0}^{t}\frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{4\eta}\,dt$$

$$\frac{h^{2}(t)}{2} \;=\; \frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{4\eta}\cdot t$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $h(t)$:**

$$h(t) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{2\eta}}\cdot\sqrt{t}$$

This is the canonical **Pick's / Washburn equation** governing impregnation kinetics.

### 3.2 Derivation of % Pick-up

Let $m_{dry}$ = dry substrate mass; $m_{wet}$ = mass immediately after impregnation.

By definition, the latex wet-mass gained is $m_{wet} - m_{dry}$. Dividing by the dry substrate mass and scaling to a percentage:

$$\text{Pick-up (\%)} \;=\; \frac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}}\times 100$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A textile cord substrate with effective pore radius $r = 50\,\mu\text{m}$ is impregnated with NR latex compound of surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 0.038\,\text{N/m}$ and viscosity $\eta = 0.012\,\text{Pa·s}$. The contact angle is $\theta = 35°$. Calculate the penetration depth after **t = 8 s**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\cos\theta$:**
$$\cos 35° \;\approx\; 0.819$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Pick's equation:**

$$h(8) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(50\times 10^{-6})\,(0.038)\,(0.819)}{2\,(0.012)}}\cdot\sqrt{8}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$50\times 10^{-6}\times 0.038\times 0.819 \;\approx\; 1.556\times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**
$$2\times 0.012 \;=\; 0.024$$

**Step 5 — Compute the inner fraction:**
$$\frac{1.556\times 10^{-6}}{0.024} \;\approx\; 6.484\times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by $\sqrt{8} = 2.828$:**
$$h(8) \;\approx\; \sqrt{6.484\times 10^{-5}}\times 2.828$$
$$h(8) \;\approx\; 8.05\times 10^{-3}\times 2.828$$
$$h(8) \;\approx\; 2.28\times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{h(8) \;\approx\; 22.8\,\text{mm}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Continuous Latex Impregnation Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Substrate Unwind Roll] --> B[Pre-Treatment RFL Dip]
    B --> C[Drying Oven 1]
    C --> D[Stretching Unit]
    D --> E[Main Impregnation Bath]
    E --> F[Coagulant Tank CaNO3 2]
    F --> G[Wash Tank]
    G --> H[Drying Oven 2]
    H --> I[Vulcanization Chamber]
    I --> J[Curing Oven]
    J --> K[Cooling Drum]
    K --> L[Take-Up Winder Roll]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Impregnation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PreStage[Pre Treatment Block]
        preA[Surface Activator] --> preB[Contact Angle Reducer]
        preB --> preC[Substrate Conditioner]
    end
    subgraph CoreStage[Core Impregnation Block]
        coreA[Latex Compound Reservoir] --> coreB[Viscosity Controller]
        coreB --> coreC[Impregnation Chamber with Capillary Action]
        coreC --> coreD[Pick up Sensor]
    end
    subgraph PostStage[Post Treatment Block]
        postA[Coagulant Ca NO3 2 Tank] --> postB[Neutralization Wash]
        postB --> postC[Drying Tunnel]
        postC --> postD[Vulcanization Reactor]
    end
    PreStage --> CoreStage
    CoreStage --> PostStage
    postD --> out[Finished Composite Roll]
```

### 4.3 Process Interaction Matrix (KTU Analytical Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Viscosity] --> P[Penetration Depth h]
    T[Contact Time] --> P
    S[Surface Tension] --> P
    CA[Contact Angle] --> P
    R[Pore Radius] --> P
    P --> Q[Quality of Saturation]
    CC[Coagulant Concentration] --> G[Gel Strength]
    CT[Coagulant Temperature] --> G
    G --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Decreasing viscosity $\eta$, increasing time $t$, increasing surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$, decreasing contact angle $\theta$, and increasing pore radius $r$ all *increase* penetration depth $h(t)$. Engineers tune these to achieve uniform saturation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define impregnation in latex technology. List any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Impregnation is a process in which a porous substrate is saturated with a liquid latex compound, followed by coagulation and vulcanization to produce a permanently bonded elastomeric composite.
- **Application 1:** Tyre-cord dipping using RFL for rubber-to-fabric adhesion.
- **Application 2:** Latex foam production for mattresses and cushioning.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of wet gel strength in latex impregnation?**

**Model Answer:**
Wet gel strength is the mechanical integrity of the coagulated latex film before drying. It determines whether the impregnated substrate can be handled, stretched, and dried without rupture. Higher wet gel strength improves product uniformity and reduces defects.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

#### ✅ QUESTION A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of latex impregnation in detail. Use Young–Laplace equation and Pick's penetration law.

**Model Solution:**

The mechanism proceeds in **four sequential stages**:

1. **Wetting:** Surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$ and contact angle $\theta$ govern whether the latex spontaneously invades the substrate pores. For $\theta < 90°$, capillary uptake is spontaneous.

2. **Capillary Pressure Generation:** Pressure difference across the meniscus:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$$

3. **Viscous Flow through Pores:** Hagen–Poiseuille flow combined with Pick's law yields:

$$h(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{2\eta}}\cdot\sqrt{t}$$

4. **Coagulation:** Ca²⁺ ions from Ca(NO₃)₂ bath neutralize the zeta potential of latex particles, causing gelation. The substrate picks up uniform rubber mass.

**Engineering significance:** These four steps collectively determine pick-up uniformity, wet gel strength, and final composite adhesion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the effect of viscosity, contact angle, and contact time on the penetration depth during impregnation.

**Model Solution:**

From Pick's law:

$$h(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{2\eta}}\cdot\sqrt{t}$$

- **Viscosity $\eta$ ↑ → $h(t)$ ↓:** Higher viscosity resists flow. Operators thin latex with water or surfactants to control this.
- **Contact angle $\theta$ ↑ → $\cos\theta$ ↓ → $h(t)$ ↓:** Pre-treatments (RFL dip) lower $\theta$, increasing penetration.
- **Contact time $t$ ↑ → $h(t)$ ↑ (square-root dependence):** Doubling time increases depth by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414\times$, showing diminishing returns.

**Numerical demonstration:** Using values $r=50\,\mu\text{m}$, $\gamma_{LV}=0.038\,\text{N/m}$, $\eta=0.012\,\text{Pa·s}$, $\theta=35°$:

$$h(8\,\text{s}) \approx 22.8\,\text{mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation:** Students often write Pick's law without the square root of time. Full marks require showing the **$\sqrt{t}$ dependence** explicitly. Marks are also lost if the **contact angle appears in degrees without conversion** to its cosine value.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the continuous latex impregnation process for textile substrates.

**Model Solution:**

The continuous process comprises the following sequential units (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4):

1. **Substrate unwind roll** — feeds the cord/fabric under controlled tension.
2. **RFL pre-treatment dip** — activates cord surface.
3. **Drying oven-1** — removes excess water from RFL layer.
4. **Stretching unit** — aligns cord fibers for uniform pick-up.
5. **Main impregnation bath** — formulated latex compound is deposited by capillary action.
6. **Coagulant tank (Ca(NO₃)₂)** — gells the latex on the cord.
7. **Wash tank** — removes excess ions.
8. **Drying oven-2** — drives off water (80–140 °C).
9. **Vulcanization chamber** — sulfur crosslinking (110–140 °C).
10. **Cooling drum** — stabilizes the composite.
11. **Take-up winder** — finished impregnated cord is rolled.

Each stage's temperature, dwell time, and tension are precisely controlled for uniform rubber-cord adhesion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of coagulant concentration and bath temperature on wet gel strength.

**Model Solution:**

**Coagulant concentration effect:**
- Higher Ca²⁺ concentration → faster destabilization → finer gel network → **higher wet gel strength** (up to an optimum).
- Beyond optimum, very rapid coagulation forms a non-uniform skin → **lower** wet gel strength.

Mathematical relation:

$$t_{gel} \propto \frac{1}{[Ca^{2+}]}$$

**Bath temperature effect:**
- Higher temperature → faster diffusion of Ca²⁺ ions → rapid gelling → potentially brittle gel.
- Optimal temperature window for NR latex: **30 °C to 60 °C**.
- Beyond 60 °C, premature coagulation destabilizes the bath.

**Combined result:** Wet gel strength peaks at intermediate coagulant concentration and moderate temperature — represented by a 3-D response surface used in KTU Module 3 numerical problems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — KTU Valuation:** Forgetting to mention the **optimum point** (neither too high nor too low) for coagulant concentration will cost 2 marks. Also, students confuse *drying temperature* with *vulcanization temperature*; these are **two distinct thermal stages**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Impregnation (KTU Module 3):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Saturation of porous substrate with liquid latex, followed by coagulation & vulcanization.
- ✅ **Young–Laplace equation:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$ — governs driving capillary pressure.
- ✅ **Pick's penetration law:** $h(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{r\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{2\eta}}\cdot\sqrt{t}$ — square-root-of-time kinetics.
- ✅ **Pick-up (%):** Mass-based saturation metric, typically 50–200 %.
- ✅ **Common coagulants:** Ca(NO₃)₂, acetic acid, ZnCl₂ — destabilize zeta potential of latex.
- ✅ **Wet gel strength range for NR latex:** 0.3–0.8 MPa.
- ✅ **Process line order:** Unwind → RFL → Dry-1 → Stretch → Impregnate → Coagulate → Wash → Dry-2 → Vulcanize → Cool → Wind-up.
- ✅ **Higher viscosity = lower penetration; higher time = greater depth (with diminishing returns).**
- ✅ **Surface tension ↑ and contact angle ↓ favor capillary uptake.**
- ✅ **Industrial uses:** Tyre cords, dipped foam, latex threads, gloves, hoses, conveyor belts.
- ✅ **Valuation tip:** Always mention **both the equation AND its physical meaning** — KTU examiners award CO-mapping marks for the *Apply* and *Analyze* levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spreading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Spreading # MODULE 3: SPREADING IN LaTeX3 (expl3) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SPREADING (x-TYPE EXPANSION)** is the mechanism in **LaTeX3 (expl3)** by which an argument is **fully expanded** — every expandable token (macro, condition, loop, arithmetic) inside the argument is recursively triggered until only **unexpandable tokens** (characters, `\relax`, `\the`-like primitives that have already yielded their meaning, and frozen control sequences) remain. In expl3 syntax, spreading is requested using the argument specifier **`x`** (or its uppercase siblings such as `:x`, `:V`, `:c`). The formal specification is: $$ \text{Spread}(\langle \text{arg} \rangle) \;\longrightarrow\; \langle \text{unexpandable-token-sequence} \rangle $$ For every macro `$M_i$` in the argument, the expl3 engine repeatedly applies: $$ \text{expand}(M_i) \to M_i^{(1)} \to M_i^{(2)} \to \dots \to M_i^{(k)} $$ where `$k$` is the smallest integer such that `$M_i^{(k)}$` contains **no further expandable tokens**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Russian Doll of Macros" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine TeX macros as **Russian nesting dolls**. A macro `\foo` may be defined in terms of `\bar`, which in turn is defined in terms of `\baz`. When you *use* `\foo` without spreading, TeX hands you the **outer doll** (the literal token `\foo`). When you *spread* `\foo`, TeX keeps opening every nested doll — `\foo` → `\bar` → `\baz` → raw characters — until only the innermost solid piece remains. That innermost sequence is what gets stored or passed onward. ### 1.3 Why Spreading Exists — The Engineering Motivation In conventional LaTeX2ε programming, programmers frequently write: ```latex \edef\result{\something \anotherthing \yetanother} ``` to force expansion. However, `\edef` is **fragile**, has surprising edge-cases (it stops at non-expandable primitives, fails with `\protect`, cannot reach inside every construct), and mixes expansion with definition. LaTeX3 introduces **spreading** as a clean, predictable, fully-engineered replacement for the most common `\edef` use-cases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** Whenever an exam question asks about "expansion in LaTeX3" or "x-type arguments", the expected answer is the **spreading mechanism** — a controlled, full, recursive expansion that stops only at unexpandable primitives. --- ### 1.4 Standard Reference Constants & Metrics | Symbol | Meaning | Default Value | |--------|---------|---------------| | `\exp_stop_token:` | Sentinel marking end of expansion (internal expl3 token) | Built-in | | `\q_...` | Quark tokens (never expand further) | Built-in | | `:` | Namespace separator in expl3 | Built-in | | Argument specifier `x` | Spreading specifier | Built-in | | Argument specifier `e` | One-level balanced expansion | Built-in | | Argument specifier `o` | One-step expansion | Built-in | | Argument specifier `n` | No expansion (verbatim) | Built-in | | Argument specifier `V` | Spreading using a variable as the specifier | Built-in | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Family of Argument Specifiers in expl3 LaTeX3 defines a precise taxonomy of *how* an argument is read and processed. Spreading belongs to this family. The table below is the **KTU board-exam canonical reference**. ### 2.1.1 Argument Specifier Reference Table | Specifier | Name | Expansion Depth | Use Case | |-----------|------|----------------|----------| | `n` | **No expansion** | 0 — tokens passed verbatim | Most common; safe default | | `N` | **Single token, no expansion** | 0 — only one token taken | Single-token arguments | | `p` | **Parameter text** | 0 — matched against `#1#2#3` | Custom delimited arguments | | `w` | **Match-to-delimiter** | 0 — reads up to given tokens | Word-boundary parsing | | `o` | **One-step expansion** | 1 — expands only the outermost macro | When one round suffices | | `e` | **Balanced expansion** | Full, but **preserves brace groups** | Common inside `\edef`-like cases | | `x` | **SPREADING** | **Full recursive** — until unexpandable only | When complete meaning is required | | `V` | **Variable spreading** | Full recursive of *value* of variable | `\foo:V \l_my_tl` spreads the value | | `c` | **Control-sequence spreading** | Builds csname then spreads | `\foo:c { myname }` | | `D` | **Do not use** | Deprecated | (removed in modern expl3) | ### 2.1.2 Conversion Logic — Why Each Specifier Acts the Way It Does - **`n`** treats the input as a *braced token list*; nothing is touched. - **`o`** runs the expansion engine **exactly once** on the *first* expandable token, then re-emits the remainder unchanged. - **`e`** differs from `x` in that it allows the result to *still contain inner brace groups*. It is what `\edef` traditionally does. - **`x`** strips brace groups at the outermost level and continues expanding inside them, **indefinitely**, until no more expandable tokens exist. ### 2.2 The Spreading Algorithm — Step-by-Step Engine Logic > [!NOTE] > The following is the conceptual algorithm that `\cs_new:Nn \foo:x` (and similar `x`-type functions) execute internally. **Algorithm `SPREAD(arg)`:** 1. Read `arg` as a brace-delimited token list. 2. Initialise an output buffer `out := []`. 3. While `arg` is non-empty: 1. Take the *first* token `t` from `arg`. 2. **If** `t` is unexpandable (a character, `\relax`, frozen `\protected` control sequence, or a quark) → append `t` to `out`; continue. 3. **Else if** `t` opens an outer brace group `{ … }` → spread *inside* it recursively; the result is **unwrapped** (braces removed) and appended to `out`. 4. **Else if** `t` is an expandable macro `M` with replacement text `R` → - Replace `t` in `arg` by `R`. - Restart step 3 from the beginning of `R`. 4. Return `out`. This algorithm can be formalised as a fixed-point computation: $$ \text{Spread}(T) \;=\; \mu X.\, F(T,X) $$ where `$F$` is the per-token transformation and `$\mu X$` denotes the least fixed point over expansion steps. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Note | |---|---------|-----------------|-------------| | 1 | Spreading stops at | unexpandable primitives only | `\relax`, chars, frozen | | 2 | Spreading preserves | **no** macros — every macro is expanded | one exception: `\exp_stop_token:` | | 3 | Spreading on `{ … }` | recursively spreads inside; **strips outer braces** | unlike `:e` | | 4 | Spreading on `\protected` macros | macro is **not** expanded (protection barrier) | important caveat | | 5 | Notation for spread argument | `\cs_new:Nx \foo { … }` | capital `x` for x-type | | 6 | Notation for spread via variable | `\cs_new:Nx \foo { \l_my_int_tl }` | reads value of `\l_my_int_tl` | | 7 | Equivalent LaTeX2ε | `\edef\foo{…}` (with caveats) | less robust than `:x` | | 8 | Sentinel for stop | `\exp_stop_token:` | explicit expansion stop | | 9 | Depth of expansion | unbounded (recursive) | until fixed point | | 10 | Failure mode | if a non-expandable macro is left, it stays | e.g., `\foo` itself | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Spreading is heavily used in: - **Token-list builders** (`\tl_build`, `\tl_build_put_right`) where the *meaning* of stored tokens must be known at *build time*. - **Symbolic arithmetic** (`\int_eval:n`, `\dim_eval:n`) — the argument is spread so that any internal `\c_int` or `\c_zero` constants are resolved. - **Conditional expansion** (`\bool_if:nTF`) — the test is spread to a Boolean first. - **Mathematics packages** (e.g., `xfp` engine) — every arithmetic operation spreads its arguments. > [!IMPORTANT] > In modern KTU-style question banks, spreading is contrasted with `\edef`. The expected reasoning is: **`x` is preferred because it is type-safe, documented, and does not silently abort on `\protected` macros — instead it propagates them as inert tokens.** # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A Simple Chain of Macros **Setup:** ```latex \cs_new:Nn \foo: { HELLO } \cs_new:Nn \bar: { \foo: WORLD } \cs_new:Nx \baz: { \bar: } ``` **Question:** What is the *fully expanded* content of `\baz:`? **Step-by-step expansion:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{\textbackslash baz:} &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash bar:} \quad &\text{(replace by definition)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash foo: WORLD} \quad &\text{(replace \textbackslash bar: by its body)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{HELLO WORLD} \quad &\text{(replace \textbackslash foo: by its body)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{HELLO WORLD} \quad &\text{(no expandable tokens remain)} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** the token list `[H][E][L][L][O][ ][W][O][R][L][D]` (ten unexpandable character tokens). > [!NOTE] > The colon in `\foo:` is the **expl3 namespace marker**. It is itself a character category, hence **unexpandable**; once a macro body has been spread down to its constituent letters, further spread is impossible. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Interaction with Outer Brace Groups **Setup:** ```latex \cs_new:Nn \scale:n { \fpeval{round(#1,2)} } % from xfp \cs_new:Nx \result: { {\scale:n {3.14159}} } ``` **Question:** What does spreading do to the outer braces around the call? **Expansion trace:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{\textbackslash result:} &\;\longrightarrow\; \{ \texttt{\textbackslash scale:n \{3.14159\}} \} \quad &\text{(substitute definition)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash fpeval\{round(3.14159,2)\}} \quad &\text{(strip outer braces per :x rule)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{3.14} \quad &\text{(recursive expansion of \textbackslash fpeval)} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** the *outer* `{ … }` surrounding the call is **stripped**, but the *inner* `{3.14159}` is consumed as the `n`-type argument to `\scale:n`. The final token list is `[3][.][1][4]`. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the cardinal rule students confuse in exams: **`:x` removes the OUTERMOST brace group surrounding its argument, while `:e` does NOT.** --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Spreading Stops at Protected Macros **Setup:** ```latex \cs_set_protected:Nn \protected_macro: { SECRET } \cs_new:Nn \normal_macro: { \protected_macro: } \cs_new:Nx \result: { \normal_macro: } ``` **Expansion trace:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{\textbackslash result:} &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash normal\_macro:} \quad &\text{(substitute)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash protected\_macro:} \quad &\text{(substitute \textbackslash normal\_macro:)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash protected\_macro:} \quad &\text{(STOP — protected, cannot expand)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final result:** the *literal* token `\protected_macro:` is left in the output. Spreading **does not** breach `\protected` boundaries. This contrasts with `\edef`, which produces a TeX error when it encounters an undefined-but-protected situation. > [!WARNING] > A common KTU valuation trap: students write that "spreading expands everything". The examiner deducts marks unless the student explicitly mentions that **protected macros form an impenetrable barrier**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Numerical Evaluation **Setup:** ```latex \int_new:N \l_my_int \int_set:Nn \l_my_int { 42 } \int_eval:n { \l_my_int + 1 } ``` **Expansion trace using `:n` first then internal spread:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{\textbackslash int\_eval:n \{\textbackslash l\_my\_int + 1\}} &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash int\_eval:n \{42 + 1\}} \quad &\text{(spread :n over the body)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; 43 \quad &\text{(integer arithmetic engine evaluates)} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation:** `\int_eval:n` is *defined* with an `x`-type internal operation, so the *value* of `\l_my_int` is read, not the variable name. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Spreading | Situation | Use `:n` | Use `:e` | Use `:x` (SPREADING) | |-----------|---------|---------|---------------------| | Macro body contains only raw text | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | Body contains internal macros that must remain as macros | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (they get expanded) | | Body contains macros whose *values* are needed | ✗ | partial | ✓ | | Body contains protected macros you wish to keep | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (kept but inert) | | Building arithmetic / Boolean / length | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (mandatory) | | Defining a `\protected` macro | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | > [!NOTE] > The matrix above is a **high-yield KTU exam table**. Memorise it; questions such as *"Why does `\cs_new:Nx` not expand correctly when a protected macro is inside the body?"* appear almost every cycle. --- ## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Iterative Spreading on a Loop Constant **Setup:** ```latex \prg_new_conditional:Npnn \my_check:n #1 { T, F, TF } { \int_compare:nNnTF {#1} > { 10 } { \prg_return_true: } { \prg_return_false: } } \my_check:n { 7 + 5 } ``` **Trace:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{\textbackslash my\_check:n \{ 7 + 5 \}} &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash my\_check:n \{12\}} \quad &\text{(spread inside :n)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{TRUE} \quad &\text{(compare 12 > 10)} \end{aligned} $$ **Insight:** the `:n` argument is *internally* spread by the conditional before the comparison is performed. The user only sees a Boolean answer. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Spreading Pipeline — Token-Stream Architecture The following block diagram shows the journey of a token list from the user input (left) to the fully expanded token stream (right), passing through the **Token Reader**, the **Expansion Engine**, and the **Sentinel Filter**. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[USER INPUT
token list] --> B[TOKEN READER
brace delimited] B --> C[EXPANSION ENGINE
recursive unfold] C --> D{Token type?} D -->|unexpandable| E[SENTINEL FILTER
keep as is] D -->|expandable macro| F[SUBSTITUTE BODY
reinsert into stream] D -->|outer brace group| G[RECURSE INSIDE
strip outer braces] F --> C G --> C E --> H[OUTPUT BUFFER
unexpandable tokens] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The loop `F → C` and `G → C` is the **recursive nature of spreading**. The engine keeps revisiting the front of the stream until the front token is unexpandable. This is why spreading is equivalent to evaluating a fixed-point in denotational semantics. --- ## 4.2 State Diagram of the Spreading Algorithm The Mermaid state diagram below captures the three operational states of the spread engine. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> READ READ --> CHECK : take first token CHECK --> EMIT : unexpandable CHECK --> SUBSTITUTE : expandable macro CHECK --> RECURSE : outer brace group EMIT --> READ : next token SUBSTITUTE --> READ : body inserted RECURSE --> READ : inner stream appended READ --> DONE : stream empty DONE --> [*] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Argument Types This diagram maps each argument specifier (`n`, `o`, `e`, `x`) to its functional role inside the expl3 macro reader. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph READER [Argument Reader Stage] A[Macro Call with Specifier] end A --> B{Specifier?} B -->|n| C[STORE RAW TOKENS
no expansion] B -->|o| D[EXPAND ONCE
outermost macro] B -->|e| E[BALANCED EXPAND
preserve braces] B -->|x| F[FULL SPREAD
strip outer braces] C --> OUT[Bound Parameter] D --> OUT E --> OUT F --> OUT ``` > [!NOTE] > From the diagram, students should observe that **`x` is the only specifier that BOTH (a) recursively expands and (b) strips the outer brace group.** Every other specifier either expands less (`o`, `e`) or not at all (`n`). --- ## 4.4 Comparison Tree — `:x` vs `\edef` ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[When to expand an argument] ROOT --> P1[Use expl3 spread :x] ROOT --> P2[Use LaTeX2e edef] P1 --> P1a[Token-list friendly] P1 --> P1b[Handles protected correctly] P1 --> P1c[Strips outer braces] P2 --> P2a[Stops at undefined] P2 --> P2b[Fragile with protect] P2 --> P2c[Preserves braces] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define *spreading* in the context of LaTeX3 (expl3). Mention the argument specifier used to invoke it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Spreading** is the expl3 mechanism by which an argument is **fully expanded** — every expandable token is recursively replaced by its definition until only unexpandable tokens remain. The specifier used is **`x`** (e.g., `\cs_new:Nx`, `\tl_set:Nx`). The outer brace group surrounding the argument is stripped during spreading. *(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — `:x` specifier; 1 mark — outer-brace stripping rule.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between `:e` and `:x` argument specifiers in expl3 with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `:e` (Balanced Expansion) | `:x` (Spreading) | |--------|---------------------------|-------------------| | Depth | Full, but stops at inner braces | Full, **strips** outer brace groups | | Example `\foo:e { \baz }` | yields `\baz` if `\baz` is unexpandable; otherwise its expansion **inside** the braces | yields the fully-spread meaning of `\baz` without braces | *(1 mark per row; 1 mark for the example.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE** of two module-specific questions. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]* **(a)** Explain the concept of **spreading** in LaTeX3. With suitable examples, describe the difference between `:n`, `:o`, `:e`, and `:x` argument specifiers. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The four specifiers are stages on a *spectrum of expansion aggressiveness*: 1. **`:n` — No expansion:** Tokens are passed verbatim. Example: ```latex \tl_new:N \l_my_tl \tl_set:Nn \l_my_tl { \LaTeX } ``` The value of `\l_my_tl` is the *literal* token `\LaTeX`. 2. **`:o` — One-step expansion:** Only the outermost macro is triggered. ```latex \cs_new:Nn \foo: { HELLO } \tl_set:No \l_my_tl { \foo: \bar: } % \l_my_tl now contains: HELLO \bar: ``` 3. **`:e` — Balanced expansion:** Full expansion but inner braces survive. ```latex \tl_set:Ne \l_my_tl { \foo: { WORLD } } % Yields: HELLO { WORLD } (inner braces kept) ``` 4. **`:x` — Spreading:** Full expansion AND outer braces are stripped. ```latex \tl_set:Nx \l_my_tl { \foo: { WORLD } } % Yields: HELLOWORLD (outer braces stripped, no extra braces) ``` *(1 mark per specifier; 3 marks for examples.)* **(b)** Consider the following expl3 code. **Trace the spreading process step-by-step** and write the final token list produced by `\result:`. **(7 marks)** ```latex \cs_new:Nn \A: { X } \cs_new:Nn \B: { \A: Y } \cs_new:Nn \C: { \B: \A: } \cs_set_protected:Nn \D: { Z } \cs_new:Nx \result: { \C: \D: } ``` **Model Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \texttt{\textbackslash result:} &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash C: \textbackslash D:} &\text{(substitute definition of \textbackslash result:)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash B: \textbackslash A: \textbackslash D:} &\text{(expand \textbackslash C:)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{\textbackslash A: Y \textbackslash A: \textbackslash D:} &\text{(expand \textbackslash B:)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{X Y X \textbackslash D:} &\text{(expand two \textbackslash A: macros)}\\[4pt] &\;\longrightarrow\; \texttt{X Y X \textbackslash D:} &\text{(STOP — \textbackslash D: is protected, immutable)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final token list:** `X` `Y` `X` `\D:` → **the protected macro is preserved as an unexpandable token**. *(1 mark per expansion step; 1 mark for the final answer; 1 mark for recognising the protected-macro barrier.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain how the **spreading algorithm** in expl3 operates on a token list. Discuss the role of the **expansion engine**, the **sentinel filter**, and the **outer-brace stripping rule**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The spreading algorithm executes a recursive fixed-point loop: 1. **Token Reader** fetches the next token from the input stream. 2. **Expansion Engine** classifies the token: - If it is **unexpandable** (character, frozen control sequence, `\exp_stop_token:`, quark), the token is forwarded as-is. - If it is an **expandable macro**, its replacement text is re-inserted at the front of the stream. - If it is an **outer brace group**, the engine recurses into it; once recursion finishes, the outer braces are *stripped* (this is the **outer-brace stripping rule** unique to `:x`). 3. **Sentinel Filter** checks for the `\exp_stop_token:` marker and aborts expansion if found. 4. Loop terminates when the stream is empty. **Neat diagram (reproduced):** ``` [Input] → [Token Reader] → [Classifier] │ ┌────────────────┼─────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ unexpandable expandable macro outer brace │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ [Sentinel] [Reinsert body] [Recurse & strip] │ │ │ └────────────────┴─────────────────┘ ▼ [Output buffer] ``` *(3 marks for the algorithm; 2 marks for the diagram; 2 marks for the outer-brace rule.)* **(b)** Explain the **real-world scenario** where spreading is **preferred over `\edef`** in modern LaTeX3 programming. Provide a code snippet to justify your answer. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Scenario:** building a token list that contains the *value* of an integer variable. **Why `:x` is preferred over `\edef`:** 1. `\edef` does **not** gracefully handle `\protected` macros; it raises an error if one is encountered. 2. `\edef` is fragile when `\protect` is in scope (e.g., inside a moving argument). 3. `\edef` does not strip outer braces, leading to surprise nesting. 4. `:x` is type-checked by the expl3 module system. **Code comparison:** ```latex % ----- LaTeX2e style (error-prone) ----- \edef\result{\myInt + 1} % may fail if \myInt contains \protected % ----- LaTeX3 style (preferred) ----- \int_new:N \l_my_int \int_set:Nn \l_my_int { 41 } \int_eval:n { \l_my_int + 1 } % safely returns 42 ``` The expl3 call `\int_eval:n` internally uses `:x` to spread its argument, evaluates the arithmetic, and returns the integer **42** as character tokens. *(2 marks for scenario; 2 marks for reasons; 3 marks for code justification.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks on Spreading questions:** > 1. **Forgetting that `:x` strips OUTER braces** — students write that `:x` keeps all brace groups intact. *(−2 marks)* > 2. **Confusing `:e` with `:x`** — both are "full expansion", but only `:x` removes outer braces. *(−2 marks)* > 3. **Assuming spreading breaks `\protected`** — it does NOT. Protected macros are returned as inert tokens. *(−2 marks)* > 4. **Equating spreading with `\edef`** without caveat — partial credit at best. *(−1 mark)* > 5. **Not mentioning the fixed-point/recursive nature** of the algorithm. *(−1 mark)* > 6. **Using vertical bars (`|`) inside Markdown tables** when presenting specifier rules — a formatting penalty is applied by the digital-evaluation portal. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Spreading (Module 3)** - **Spreading** = **full, recursive, unexpandable-stop** expansion of an argument. - Triggered by the **`x`** argument specifier in expl3 (e.g., `\cs_new:Nx`, `\tl_set:Nx`, `\int_eval:n`). - Specifier **`:x`** vs others: - `n` → no expansion - `o` → one-step expansion - `e` → balanced expansion (keeps braces) - **`x` → SPREADING (strips outer braces, full recursive)** ✔ - Outer-brace stripping rule is **unique to `:x`**. - Protected macros are **barriers** — they stop the spread and remain as inert tokens. - Equivalent LaTeX2ε operation: `\edef`, but `:x` is **safer, type-checked, and `\protect`-aware**. - Engine components: **Token Reader → Expansion Engine → Sentinel Filter → Output Buffer**. - Mathematical abstraction: **least fixed point** over the per-token expansion function. - Used internally by `\int_eval:n`, `\dim_eval:n`, `\bool_if:nTF`, `\tl_build_*`, and the `xfp` engine. - Default sentinel for halting: **`\exp_stop_token:`**. - **Never** write `\foo` (unbraced) when expecting `x`-expansion; always supply a brace group. - LaTeX3 *does not* support `\noexpand`-style forcing inside `:x`; use `\exp_not:N` instead. - **Common exam trap**: spreading does **not** execute assignments or definitions — it only unfolds macro bodies.

topics.title: Fabric Proofing and Coating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fabric Proofing and Coating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fabric Proofing** is the chemical or physical treatment of textile substrates (woven, knitted, or nonwoven fabrics) to impart **resistance against wetting, penetration, staining, flame, microbial attack, or chemical aggression**, without necessarily sealing the interstitial pores of the fabric structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fabric Coating** is the application of a **continuous polymeric layer** (latex, rubber, PVC, PU, acrylic, silicone, or fluorochemical) onto one or both faces of a textile substrate using a precisely controlled mechanical process, so as to form a **bonded composite film** that modifies surface permeability, mechanical strength, and barrier properties.

The two operations are functionally distinct: **proofing reduces surface energy and prevents wetting (breathable barrier), while coating physically occludes pores (impermeable barrier).**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 3:** *Fabric Proofing & Coating — mechanisms, chemistry of proofing agents, polymer coating systems (latex, PU, PVC, rubber), coating machinery, testing standards, and end-use applications.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a brand-new cotton T-shirt on a rainy day:

- **Proofing** = spraying the shirt with a thin invisible wax film (like waxing a car). Water **beads up** and rolls off, but the fabric still **breathes** (air passes through). You stay dry from the rain shower but don't sweat inside.
- **Coating** = gluing a thin plastic sheet (think of a raincoat) onto the fabric. Now **no water** and **no air** can pass through. You stay dry but feel clammy.

This simple distinction — *beading water versus blocking water* — is the conceptual heart of Module 3.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Metrics

| Term | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrostatic Head** | Water column pressure a fabric resists before leakage | cm of H₂O or mbar |
| **Spray Rating** | Visual rating (1–5) of fabric wetting under sprayed water | AATCC 22 scale |
| **Add-on (%)** | Mass of coating/polymer deposited per unit mass of fabric | % |
| **Coat Weight** | Mass of coating per unit area | g/m² or gsm |
| **Contact Angle (θ)** | Angle formed by a liquid droplet at the three-phase boundary | degrees (°) |
| **Surface Energy** | Excess energy at fabric surface governing wettability | mN/m (dynes/cm) |

### 1.4 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Fabric Proofing** is sub-divided into two major branches:
> 1. **Water Proofing** — pore-sealing, non-breathable, hydrostatic barrier
> 2. **Water Repellency** — surface-energy modification, breathable, droplet beading

> [!NOTE]
> **Fabric Coating** is classified by the **polymer system** used:
> - **Latex-based** (natural rubber latex, SBR, NBR, neoprene)
> - **PVC Plastisols** (polyvinyl chloride + plasticizer)
> - **Polyurethane (PU)** — aromatic and aliphatic
> - **Acrylic emulsions**
> - **Silicone elastomers**
> - **Fluorochemical coatings**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanisms of Fabric Proofing

#### 2.1.1 The Wetting Criterion (Young's Equation)

The **fundamental thermodynamic criterion** governing whether a liquid wets a textile substrate is captured by **Young's Equation**, the starting point of all proofing science:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid–vapor interfacial energy (mN/m)
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy (mN/m)
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid–vapor interfacial energy (mN/m)
- $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle (degrees)

**Interpretation:**
- If $\theta < 90°$ → the liquid **wets** the fabric (hydrophilic surface, no proofing needed).
- If $\theta > 90°$ → the liquid **does not wet** (hydrophobic, proofing effective).
- If $\theta \to 180°$ → **super-hydrophobic** state (lotus effect).

> [!NOTE]
> **Proofing is fundamentally a problem of lowering $\gamma_{SV}$ below that of water (≈ 72.8 mN/m at 25 °C).** Fluorocarbon finishes lower it to ≈ 10–15 mN/m; silicones to ≈ 24 mN/m.

#### 2.1.2 Proofing Mechanisms (Three Conceptual Models)

1. **Surface Energy Reduction** — fluorocarbon or silicone molecules orient their low-energy groups outward, reducing $\gamma_{SV}$.
2. **Pore Blocking / Sealing** — polymeric films fill inter-yarn capillaries (this borders on coating).
3. **Micro-roughness Engineering** — creating nano-structures that trap air, enhancing apparent contact angle (Cassie–Baxter state).

### 2.2 Waterproofing vs. Water Repellency — The Core Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Waterproof:** the fabric will **not allow water penetration under any hydrostatic pressure up to its rated head**. Achieved by **coating, laminating, or dense weave + finish**. *Breathability is sacrificed.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water-repellent:** the fabric **resists wetting and sheds water droplets** but will eventually allow penetration under sustained hydrostatic pressure or prolonged exposure. Achieved by **finishing (proofing) with low-surface-energy agents**. *Breathability retained.*

### 2.3 Chemistry of Major Proofing Agents

| Agent Class | Active Chemistry | Surface Energy (mN/m) | Durability | Breathability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paraffin Wax Emulsions** | Long-chain C₂₀–C₃₀ alkanes | ~25 | Poor (1–5 washes) | High |
| **Silicone Emulsions** | Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) | ~24 | Good (10–20 washes) | High |
| **Fluorochemicals (C8 / C6)** | Perfluorinated acrylates | ~10–15 | Excellent (30+ washes) | High |
| **Pyridinium Compounds** | Stearamidomethyl pyridinium chloride | ~30 | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Dendrimer Finishes** | Multi-arm fluoropolymers | <12 | Excellent | High |

### 2.4 Coating Polymer Systems — Comparative Analysis

#### 2.4.1 Latex-Based Coatings
- **Natural Rubber Latex (NRL)** — cis-1,4-polyisoprene; excellent elasticity; allergy concern.
- **Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR)** — tough, cheap, used in tire-cord dipping and carpet backing.
- **Nitrile Rubber (NBR)** — oil resistance.
- **Polychloroprene (Neoprene)** — chemical and flame resistance.

#### 2.4.2 PVC Plastisols
- PVC resin (emulsion grade, K-value 65–75) + plasticizer (DOP, DINP, ESO) + stabilizer + filler.
- Fused at **160–180 °C** to form a continuous film.

#### 2.4.3 Polyurethane (PU) Coatings
- **One-component moisture-cure** (MDI-based).
- **Two-component** (polyol + isocyanate).
- **Aliphatic PU** — UV stable, used in technical outerwear.
- **Aromatic PU** — cheaper, yellows on UV exposure.

### 2.5 Coating Application Techniques — Master List

| Technique | Principle | Typical Coat Weight (gsm) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knife (Doctor Blade) Coating** | Fixed gap blade scrapes excess | 30–500 | Industrial fabrics, tarpaulins |
| **Roller Coating** | Engraved/smooth roller transfers paste | 5–150 | Apparel interlinings |
| **Dip & Squeeze (Padding)** | Immersion followed by nip rollers | 5–80 | Light finishes |
| **Spray Coating** | Atomized polymer sprayed onto fabric | 2–30 | Garment finishing |
| **Calender Coating** | Heated rollers fuse polymer into fabric | 10–200 | PVC leather cloth |
| **Extrusion Coating** | Molten polymer extruded through die | 20–500 | Tarpaulins, geomembranes |
| **Transfer Coating** | Polymer cast on release paper, laminated to fabric | 30–300 | Synthetic leather (PU leather) |
| **Foam Coating** | Air-incorporated frothed polymer | 10–100 | Carpet backing |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Bank — Module 3 / Fabric Proofing & Coating**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Young's wetting equation | mN/m |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Work of adhesion (Dupré) | mN/m |
| 3 | $A\% = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1}\times 100$ | Add-on percentage | % |
| 4 | $CW = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{A_{fabric}}$ | Coat weight | g/m² |
| 5 | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic head pressure | Pa |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{F \cdot d \cdot A}{Q}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow through fabric pore (resistance proxy) | Pa·s/m³ |
| 7 | $T_{film} = \dfrac{CW}{\rho_{polymer}}$ | Average coating film thickness | m |
| 8 | $v_{prod} = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | Linear production speed | m/min |
| 9 | $M_{wet} = \dfrac{CW \times (100 + S)}{100}$ | Wet pick-up relation | g/m² |
| 10 | $R_{perm} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | Water vapor permeability | g/m²·day |

Where:
- $W_1$ = fabric weight before coating (g)
- $W_2$ = fabric weight after coating (g)
- $A_{fabric}$ = coated fabric area (m²)
- $h$ = hydrostatic water column height (m)
- $S$ = solid content (%) of coating paste
- $L$ = line length, $t$ = time

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defense:** NBC suits, gas-tight PU-coated nylon.
- **Marine:** Inflatable life-rafts (NRL/NBR-coated nylon).
- **Automotive:** Airbag fabrics (silicone/neoprene coated).
- **Construction:** PU-coated fiberglass membranes (tensile architecture).
- **Sportswear:** Gore-Tex-style PTFE/PU laminated breathable membranes.
- **Medical:** Latex examination gloves, antimicrobial-coated hospital textiles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship between Hydrostatic Head and Coat Weight

The **hydrostatic head** ($h$) of a coated fabric is empirically related to coat weight ($CW$) and coating thickness ($T_{film}$). We derive the dependence.

**Step 1:** Start with the pressure exerted by a water column of height $h$:

$$P = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** The coating thickness is related to coat weight by:

$$T_{film} = \dfrac{CW}{\rho_{polymer}}$$

**Step 3:** Empirically, for a defect-free polymer film, the **Laplace pressure** required to push water through a circular defect of radius $r$ is:

$$P_{Laplace} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 4:** For a continuous (pinhole-free) film, the dominant failure mode is **film rupture**. Equating hydrostatic pressure to film burst strength $\sigma_{burst}$:

$$\rho_{water} g h = \sigma_{burst}$$

**Step 5:** Since $\sigma_{burst}$ is proportional to film thickness for a given polymer:

$$\sigma_{burst} = k \cdot T_{film} = k \cdot \dfrac{CW}{\rho_{polymer}}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting:

$$h = \dfrac{k \cdot CW}{\rho_{polymer} \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g}$$

**Conclusion:** *Hydrostatic head is directly proportional to coat weight and inversely proportional to polymer and water densities.* This is why heavy PU coatings (≈ 100 gsm) yield 10 m water-head ratings.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coating Mass Balance (Wet Pick-Up → Dry Coat Weight)

Given a coating paste of **solid content** $S$ (%) applied to a fabric at **wet pick-up** $WPU$ (%), derive the dry coat weight.

**Step 1:** Wet pickup definition:

$$WPU\% = \dfrac{M_{wet} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** The mass of solids deposited equals the mass of wet liquor times its solid fraction:

$$M_{solids} = (M_{wet} - M_{dry}) \cdot \dfrac{S}{100}$$

**Step 3:** Combine the two expressions:

$$M_{solids} = M_{dry} \cdot \dfrac{WPU}{100} \cdot \dfrac{S}{100}$$

**Step 4:** Coat weight (gsm) for a fabric of mass per unit area $m_{fabric}$ (gsm):

$$CW = m_{fabric} \cdot \dfrac{WPU \cdot S}{10{,}000}$$

**Numerical Illustration:**
- Fabric mass: $m_{fabric} = 200 \text{ gsm}$
- Wet pick-up: $WPU = 80\%$
- Solid content: $S = 40\%$

$$CW = 200 \times \dfrac{80 \times 40}{10{,}000} = 200 \times \dfrac{3200}{10{,}000} = 200 \times 0.32 = 64 \text{ gsm}$$

**Verification:** Add-on percentage is:

$$A\% = \dfrac{64}{200} \times 100 = 32\%$$

This is typical for a **medium-weight proofed fabric**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Contact Angle from Surface Energy (Owens–Wendt Approach)

For practical textile proofing, Young's equation is extended using the **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK)** model:

$$\gamma_L (1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p}\right)$$

**Worked Example:**
A fluorocarbon-finished fabric has $\gamma_S^d = 18 \text{ mN/m}$ and $\gamma_S^p = 2 \text{ mN/m}$. Water has $\gamma_L^d = 21.8 \text{ mN/m}$, $\gamma_L^p = 51.0 \text{ mN/m}$, total $\gamma_L = 72.8 \text{ mN/m}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the dispersive term:

$$2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} = 2\sqrt{18 \times 21.8} = 2\sqrt{392.4} = 2 \times 19.81 = 39.62 \text{ mN/m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the polar term:

$$2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 51.0} = 2\sqrt{102} = 2 \times 10.10 = 20.20 \text{ mN/m}$$

**Step 3:** Total right-hand side:

$$39.62 + 20.20 = 59.82 \text{ mN/m}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\cos\theta$:

$$1 + \cos\theta = \dfrac{59.82}{72.8} = 0.8217$$

$$\cos\theta = -0.1783$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(-0.1783) \approx 100.3°$$

**Conclusion:** The contact angle of ≈ 100° confirms effective **hydrophobic proofing** ($\theta > 90°$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Proofing vs. Coating Selection

| Performance Criterion | Best Proofing Route | Best Coating Route |
|---|---|---|
| Breathability retention | Fluorochemical finish | Microporous PU / PTFE laminate |
| Maximum water resistance | Heavily PVC-coated | PU coating (>50 gsm) |
| Oil repellency | C8 fluorocarbon | None (only finishing works) |
| Flexibility | Silicone finish | PU (especially aliphatic) |
| Flame retardancy | Pyrovatex/Proban finish | Neoprene coating |
| Cost economy | Paraffin wax | PVC plastisol |
| UV resistance | Aliphatic PU coating | Acrylic coating |
| Bondability to rubber | RFL (resorcinol-formaldehyde-latex) | Direct NRL dipping |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — General Fabric Coating Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fabric Pay-off Roll] --> B[Accumulative Let-off]
    B --> C[Pre-treater / Corona]
    C --> D[Coating Head]
    D --> E[Oven / Drying Chamber]
    E --> F[Calendaring / Cooling]
    F --> G[Inspection Unit]
    G --> H[Batch Wind-up]
    D --> D1[Knife Blade]
    D --> D2[Roller]
    D --> D3[Slot Die]
    D --> D4[Spray Nozzle]
    H --> I[Finished Coated Fabric Roll]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Proofing vs. Coating Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph proofPath["Fabric Proofing Path"]
        P1[Substrate Fabric] --> P2[Padding / Dipping in Proofing Bath]
        P2 --> P3[Drying Curing 160 C]
        P3 --> P4[Finished Proofed Fabric Breathable Hydrophobic]
    end

    subgraph coatPath["Fabric Coating Path"]
        C1[Substrate Fabric] --> C2[Polymer Paste Preparation]
        C2 --> C3[Coating Head Application]
        C3 --> C4[Oven Fusion 160 to 180 C]
        C4 --> C5[Calendaring Cooling]
        C5 --> C6[Finished Coated Fabric Impermeable Film]
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Coating Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{End Use Requirement}
    S -->|Waterproof only| P1[PVC Plastisol]
    S -->|Waterproof and Flexible| P2[PU Aliphatic]
    S -->|Oil Resistant| P3[NBR Latex]
    S -->|Flame Resistant| P4[Neoprene or Pyrovatex Finish]
    S -->|Breathable yet Waterproof| P5[Microporous PU or PTFE Laminate]
    S -->|Soft Handle| P6[Acrylic Emulsion]
    S -->|High Temperature Resistance| P7[Silicone Elastomer]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Knife-Over-Roll Coating Head

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Fabric In] --> R[Backing Roll]
    R --> K[Doctor Blade Stationary]
    K --> P[Polymer Paste Reservoir]
    K --> O[Oven Entry]
    O --> W[Fabric Out Coated]
    P -. feeds .-> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read-out:** The fabric passes over a rotating backing roll; a stationary knife positioned at a **precisely calibrated gap** above the roll meters the polymer paste onto the fabric. Excess paste returns to the reservoir. Coat weight is governed by gap, paste viscosity, and line speed.

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Testing and Quality Assurance Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coated Fabric] --> B[Hydrostatic Head Test]
    A --> C[Spray Rating Test AATCC 22]
    A --> D[Tensile Strength ASTM D5035]
    A --> E[Coat Weight Gravimetric]
    A --> F[Wash Durability ISO 6330]
    B --> Q{Quality OK}
    C --> Q
    D --> Q
    E --> Q
    F --> Q
    Q -->|Yes| G[Approved for Dispatch]
    Q -->|No| H[Re-process or Reject]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between water-proofing and water-repellency in textile finishing.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Water-proofing** is a treatment that makes a fabric completely impermeable to water, even under hydrostatic pressure, by **sealing all pores** with a polymer film or laminate. The fabric loses breathability. Test method: hydrostatic head (e.g., > 1000 mm).
- **Water-repellency** is a surface treatment that lowers the fabric's surface energy so that water **beads up and rolls off** without wetting. The pores remain open; breathability is retained. Test method: spray rating (e.g., 4–5 on AATCC 22 scale).
- *Proofing agents:* wax, silicone, fluorocarbon for water-repellency; PVC/PU coating for waterproofing.

*(3 marks — keyword hits required: hydrostatic head, surface energy, breathability)*

---

**Q2. List any three common proofing agents used in textile finishing and mention the substrate they are best suited for.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Fluorocarbons (C6/C8)** — best for outdoor apparel, technical textiles; offer both water and oil repellency.
2. **Silicone emulsions (PDMS)** — best for softer handle applications; used on knit fabrics and bedding.
3. **Paraffin wax emulsions** — best for inexpensive, temporary water-repellency (workwear, industrial cloth).

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE – May 2024 / Dec 2024 Modeled Pattern]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B:**
> - Students frequently lose marks by **confusing proofing with coating** — keep the definitions separate.
> - Always quote **standard test methods** (ISO/AATCC/ASTM) wherever applicable.
> - When writing about polymer chemistry, **name the functional group** (e.g., isocyanate in PU) — generic "polymer" wording fetches only 1 mark instead of 2.
> - In numerical problems, **carry units throughout** — a missing unit often costs 1 mark.
> - Diagrams must be **labelled**; an unlabelled flowchart earns partial credit only.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *(Choose either A or B)*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **knife-over-roll coating technique** for fabric coating. Discuss the role of viscosity, blade gap, and line speed on coat weight. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Knife-Over-Roll Coating (7 marks)**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** A stationary, precisely sharpened metal blade (knife) is positioned above a rotating cylindrical backing roll. The fabric is fed between the roll and the knife. Polymer paste is fed from a reservoir ahead of the knife. Excess paste overflows and returns to the reservoir.

2. **Operating Principle (1 mark):** As the fabric passes under the knife at a fixed gap $g$, the paste is **metered** onto the fabric surface. The film thickness after drying equals:

$$T_{film} = \dfrac{CW}{\rho_{polymer}}$$

3. **Process Parameters (3 marks):**
   - **Viscosity (η):** Higher viscosity → thicker coat. Typical 5,000–50,000 cP.
   - **Blade gap (g):** Directly proportional to coat weight; doubling gap roughly doubles wet coat weight (provided paste is not starved).
   - **Line speed (v):** Inversely related to coat weight at constant paste feed; faster line → thinner coat. Typical 5–60 m/min.
4. **Industrial Use (1 mark):** Tarpaulins, conveyor belts, PVC leather cloth.

5. **Sketch** is mandatory — labelled diagram showing fabric, backing roll, knife, paste reservoir, oven.

---

**(b)** Explain **fluorochemical-based water-repellent finishing**. Discuss the chemistry, mechanism, and testing methods for assessing repellency. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Chemistry (2 marks):** Fluorocarbons are acrylate or methacrylate monomers with perfluorinated side chains (C₆F₁₃–C₈F₁₇). They are applied as aqueous emulsions, dried at 110–130 °C, and cured at 150–170 °C to crosslink on the fiber surface.

2. **Mechanism (2 marks):** The perfluoroalkyl groups orient outward, presenting a surface of densely packed CF₃ and CF₂ groups. This reduces surface energy to ≈ 10–15 mN/m, well below that of water (72.8 mN/m), giving contact angles > 100° and producing both **water and oil repellency**.

3. **Test Methods (3 marks):**
   - **AATCC 22 / ISO 4920** — Spray Test (rating 1–5).
   - **AATCC 118** — Oil Repellency (rating 1–8).
   - **ISO 811** — Hydrostatic Head (cm H₂O).
   - **AATCC 135 / ISO 6330** — Wash durability (cycles vs. retained rating).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Discuss **PVC plastisol coating** of fabrics. Cover formulation, processing, and end-use applications. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **What is PVC Plastisol? (1 mark):** A liquid dispersion of PVC resin (emulsion grade, K-value 65–75) in a plasticizer (DOP, DINP, ESO, or DOA), along with stabilizers (Ba–Zn or Ca–Zn), fillers (CaCO₃), pigments, and UV absorbers.

2. **Formulation (2 marks):** Typical recipe:
   - PVC resin: 100 phr
   - Plasticizer: 50–80 phr (controls flexibility and Tg)
   - Stabilizer: 2–3 phr
   - Filler: 0–30 phr
   - Pigment: as required

3. **Processing (3 marks):**
   - Paste spread by knife or calendar onto fabric.
   - **Gelation** at 80–100 °C.
   - **Fusion** at 160–180 °C for 2–3 min — plasticizer solvates PVC particles, forming a continuous film.
   - Cooling and winding.

4. **Applications (1 mark):** Tarpaulins, inflatable structures, automotive upholstery, rainwear, floor coverings, leather cloth.

---

**(b)** A cotton fabric weighing **180 gsm** is padded with a silicone proofing emulsion containing **35% solids** at a wet pick-up of **75%**. Calculate: **(i)** the dry coat weight, **(ii)** the add-on percentage, and **(iii)** the average film thickness if the polymer density is **1.05 g/cm³**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Fabric mass: $m_f = 180 \text{ gsm}$
- Solid content: $S = 35\%$
- Wet pick-up: $WPU = 75\%$
- Polymer density: $\rho = 1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Step 1 — Dry Coat Weight (2 marks):**

$$CW = m_f \cdot \dfrac{WPU \cdot S}{10{,}000}$$

$$CW = 180 \times \dfrac{75 \times 35}{10{,}000} = 180 \times 0.2625 = 47.25 \text{ gsm}$$

**Step 2 — Add-on Percentage (2 marks):**

$$A\% = \dfrac{CW}{m_f} \times 100 = \dfrac{47.25}{180} \times 100 = 26.25\%$$

**Step 3 — Average Film Thickness (3 marks):**

$$T_{film} = \dfrac{CW}{\rho} = \dfrac{47.25 \text{ g/m}^2}{1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

Convert g/m² to g/cm²: $1 \text{ m}^2 = 10{,}000 \text{ cm}^2$, so:

$$CW = \dfrac{47.25}{10{,}000} \text{ g/cm}^2 = 4.725 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/cm}^2$$

$$T_{film} = \dfrac{4.725 \times 10^{-3}}{1.05} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm} = 45 \text{ μm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **CW = 47.25 gsm**
- (ii) **Add-on = 26.25%**
- (iii) **Film thickness ≈ 45 μm**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Fabric Proofing & Coating (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Proofing** = breathable, low surface energy; **Coating** = impermeable, polymer film.
- ✅ Always remember **Young's equation**: $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$.
- ✅ **Contact angle > 90°** = hydrophobic; < 90° = hydrophilic.
- ✅ **Fluorocarbons** → lowest surface energy (~10–15 mN/m), best for oil + water repellency.
- ✅ **Silicones** → soft handle, moderate durability, ~24 mN/m.
- ✅ **Paraffin wax** → cheapest, least durable.
- ✅ **PVC plastisol** → fused at 160–180 °C; plasticizer controls flexibility.
- ✅ **PU coatings** → aliphatic (UV stable) vs. aromatic (yellows).
- ✅ **Latex coatings** → NRL, SBR, NBR, CR (Neoprene).
- ✅ Coating methods to memorize: **knife, roller, dip-squeeze, spray, calendar, extrusion, transfer, foam**.
- ✅ Key tests: **AATCC 22 (spray), AATCC 118 (oil), ISO 811 (hydrostatic head), ISO 6330 (wash durability)**.
- ✅ Formula essentials: $CW = m_f \cdot WPU \cdot S / 10{,}000$; $A\% = (W_2 - W_1)/W_1 \times 100$; $T_{film} = CW/\rho$.
- ✅ Hydrostatic head $h \propto CW / (\rho_{polymer} \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g)$.
- ✅ Production speed (m/min) × line length (m) = residence time (min) in oven — controls fusion/cure.
- ✅ Always specify **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing units.
- ✅ For knife coating: **higher viscosity + wider gap + lower speed → heavier coat**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rubber-Textile Composite Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rubber-Textile Composite Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rubber-Textile Composite Product** is a hybrid engineering material formed by the physical and chemical integration of a continuous **polymeric rubber matrix** (natural rubber latex, polychloroprene latex, nitrile latex, or compounded solid rubber) with a **textile reinforcement phase** (yarns, cords, woven fabrics, knitted fabrics, or non-woven substrates). The textile skeleton carries the primary mechanical load, while the rubber matrix distributes stress, protects the fibres from abrasion and environmental degradation, and provides elasticity, sealing, and damping characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPOT303, Module 3):** *"Rubber-Textile Composite Products are engineered structures in which a textile substrate (natural or synthetic) is embedded, coated, or laminated with a rubber compound to yield a finished product exhibiting synergistic mechanical properties—tensile strength from the textile and elastic recovery from the rubber."*

The composite is governed by two fundamental engineering requirements:

1. **Load Transfer Efficiency** — the rubber matrix must transfer applied stress uniformly to every fibre in the textile.
2. **Interfacial Adhesion** — the bond strength between rubber and textile must exceed the cohesive strength of either phase to prevent delamination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reinforced Concrete" Model

Imagine you are building a **suspension bridge**. The steel cables bear the enormous tensile load (they are strong but flexible), while the concrete deck holds the cables in position and distributes the weight of traffic evenly (it is rigid but weak in tension). Neither material alone can build a bridge — but together, they form a system far stronger than the sum of its parts.

A **rubber-textile composite** works on exactly this principle:

| Component in Concrete Bridge | Equivalent in Rubber-Textile Composite | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Steel cables (high tensile strength) | Textile cords / fabric (cotton, nylon, polyester, aramid) | Carries tensile load |
| Concrete deck (rigid, distributes load) | Rubber matrix (natural rubber, polychloroprene, SBR) | Distributes stress, provides elasticity, seals |
| Bond between steel & concrete | RFL (Resorcinol-Formaldehyde-Latex) dip or brass plating | Transfers load between phases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A pure rubber hose can stretch, but it will balloon under high internal pressure. A pure textile fabric is strong but porous and non-elastic. Combining them gives you a hose that is **flexible, pressure-resistant, and durable** — the synergy is the entire point of composite design.

## 1.3 Classification of Rubber-Textile Composites

Rubber-textile composites are classified based on (a) the **form of the textile phase**, (b) the **method of integration**, and (c) the **end-use product family**.

### 1.3.1 Based on Textile Form

- **Cord / Yarn Reinforced:** Discrete aligned fibres (e.g., tyre cords, V-belt cords).
- **Woven Fabric Reinforced:** Plain, twill, or canvas weaves (e.g., conveyor belts, rubberised canvas shoes).
- **Knitted Fabric Reinforced:** Looped structures offering high elongation (e.g., elastic bandages).
- **Non-Woven Reinforced:** Random fibre mats bonded with rubber (e.g., gaskets, noise-damping sheets).

### 1.3.2 Based on Manufacturing Method

- **Dipped Composites:** Fabric passed through latex compound → coagulant dip → leaching → curing.
- **Calendered Composites:** Rubber sheeted onto textile by heated rollers.
- **Spread / Coated Composites:** Dough / solution coated via knife-over-roller or floating-knife coater.
- **Laminated Composites:** Pre-cured rubber sheets vulcanised onto textile under heat and pressure.
- **Extruded Composites:** Textile braided or spiralled and embedded in extruded rubber (hoses).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard parameters govern rubber-textile composite design:

- **Adhesion Strength:** **4–18 N/mm** (peel test, ASTM D413).
- **Cord Tensile Strength:** **0.5–3.5 GPa** depending on fibre type (nylon ~0.9 GPa; polyester ~1.1 GPa; aramid ~2.8 GPa; steel cord ~2.0–3.5 GPa).
- **Rubber-to-Fibre Volume Ratio:** typically **60:40 to 70:30** in tyres.
- **Cure Temperature:** **140–160 °C** (press cure); **120–140 °C** (continuous cure).
- **Cure Pressure:** **1.5–2.5 MPa** for compression moulded composites.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Rubber-Textile Composite Engineering

For any rubber-textile composite to perform its function, **three engineering requirements** must be satisfied simultaneously. A failure in any one collapses the product.

### Pillar 1 — Fibre (Textile) Selection

The textile phase dictates the **strength, fatigue life, modulus, and thermal stability** of the composite.

| Fibre Type | Tensile Strength (GPa) | Density (g/cm³) | Moisture Regain (%) | Cost (relative) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cotton | 0.25 – 0.50 | 1.54 | 7 – 8 | Low | Dipped goods, footwear |
| Rayon (Viscose) | 0.40 – 0.65 | 1.52 | 11 – 13 | Medium | Tyre cords (older) |
| Nylon 6,6 | 0.80 – 1.00 | 1.14 | 4 – 5 | Medium | Tyre cap ply, hoses |
| Polyester | 0.95 – 1.20 | 1.38 | 0.4 – 0.6 | Medium-High | Tyre carcass, conveyor belts |
| Aramid (Kevlar) | 2.50 – 3.00 | 1.44 | 3 – 4 | Very High | Aerospace, racing tyres |
| Steel (brass-coated) | 2.00 – 3.50 | 7.85 | 0 | High | Radial tyre belts |
| Glass Fibre | 2.00 – 3.00 | 2.55 | 0 | Medium | High-pressure hoses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why fibre choice matters:** A fishing line made of cotton will snap under load; a fishing line made of aramid is what they use for deep-sea trawling. The fibre is the *backbone* of the composite, and choosing the wrong one is the most common cause of premature product failure.

### Pillar 2 — Rubber Matrix Compounding

The rubber phase must be tailored to provide:
- **Elastic recovery** (low hysteresis loss).
- **Ageing resistance** (anti-oxidants: TMQ, IPPD).
- **Processability** (suitable Mooney viscosity, 40–70 ML(1+4) at 100 °C).
- **Adhesion promotion** (compatibilisers like *Resorcinol* in HRH system).

**Typical Rubber Compound Recipe (per 100 phr rubber):**

| Ingredient | Function | Typical Loading (phr) |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Rubber (RSS-3 or Latex) | Base elastomer | 100 |
| Zinc Oxide | Activator | 3 – 5 |
| Stearic Acid | Co-activator | 1 – 2 |
| Sulphur | Vulcanising agent | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| CBS / TBBS (accelerator) | Cure rate control | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Carbon Black (HAF / SRF) | Reinforcement | 20 – 50 |
| Aromatic Oil | Plasticiser | 5 – 15 |
| TMQ / IPPD | Antioxidant | 1 – 2 |
| Resorcinol + Hexa (HRH system) | Adhesion promoter | 2 – 5 + 1.5 – 3.5 |

### Pillar 3 — Interfacial Adhesion (The Hidden Hero)

The single most critical technical challenge in rubber-textile composites is **creating a chemical bond between an organic polymer (rubber) and an organic fibre (textile)** that survives dynamic fatigue, heat, oil, and water exposure.

Three industrial adhesion systems are dominant:

#### (a) **RFL Dip System (Resorcinol-Formaldehyde-Latex)**

This is the **workhorse of the rubber-textile industry**, used universally for tyre cords, V-belts, and conveyor belts.

The RFL bath is prepared by:
1. **Resorcinol (R)** is reacted with **Formaldehyde (F)** under alkaline catalysis (NaOH) → forms a **resol resin** (a phenolic pre-polymer with pendant -CH₂OH groups).
2. This resol is blended with a **Latex (L)** — typically SBR latex or VP latex (Vinyl Pyridine latex).
3. The dip is aged (24 hours at 25 °C) to mature the resin-latex network.
4. The textile is dipped, dried (100–130 °C), and cured (200–240 °C for 30–60 s).

**Bonding mechanism:** During high-temperature cure, the -CH₂OH groups of the resol condense with -OH / -NH groups on the fibre surface (hydrogen + covalent bonding), while simultaneously the latex particles coalesce and interpenetrate with the rubber compound during vulcanisation — creating a **continuous chemical gradient** between fibre and rubber.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VP latex (Vinyl Pyridine)?** The 2-vinylpyridine comonomer provides a **nitrogen lone pair** that strongly coordinates with the resorcinol resin AND is miscible with most rubber matrices, dramatically improving bond durability in humid environments.

#### (b) **Brass Plating (Steel Cord Only)**

Used exclusively for **steel-belted radial tyres**. The brass coating (typically **Cu:Zn = 70:30**) forms a **Cu-Sulphide / Zn-Sulphide** interfacial layer during sulphur vulcanisation. This is a **true chemisorption bond** and is the strongest adhesion system in the rubber industry.

#### (c) **Isocyanate / Epoxy Resin Systems**

Used for specialty hoses and conveyor belts. The -N=C=O group reacts with active hydrogens on both the fibre and the rubber to form **urethane or urea linkages**. Common reagent: **Desmodur R** (triphenylmethane triisocyanate).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the **core analytical toolkit** for rubber-textile composite design and examination problems.

### Table 2.1 — Mechanical & Composite Property Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_r V_r$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (longitudinal modulus) — where $V_f + V_r = 1$ | Pa |
| 2 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_r V_r$ | Composite tensile strength (Voigt model, isostrain) | Pa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_r}{E_r}$ | Reuss model (isostress, transverse) | Pa |
| 4 | $\tau_{adh} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w}$ | Peel adhesion strength — $F_{peel}$ = peel force, $w$ = width | N/mm |
| 5 | $\sigma_{cord} = \dfrac{T}{A_{cord} \cdot n}$ | Cord tensile stress — $T$ = applied load, $n$ = number of cords | Pa |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{elastic}}{W_{total}}$ | Resilience / Elastic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $t_{90} = \dfrac{0.9 (M_H - M_L)}{\text{slope at } t_{90}}$ | Optimum cure time from rheometer curve | seconds |
| 8 | $n_{opt} = \dfrac{L_{cable}}{L_{cord}} \cdot 100\%$ | Cord efficiency in twisted cable | % |
| 9 | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t}{D}$ | Hoop stress in hose — $t$ = wall thickness, $D$ = diameter | Pa |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{ad} = T_{cure} - T_{amb}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise in thick composite | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The **Rule of Mixtures (Equation 1)** and **Peel Adhesion (Equation 4)** appear in approximately **70% of Module-3 numerical questions**. Memorise both — examiners *will* test them.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Equation 1 ($E_c$)** is used by tyre designers to predict the carcass modulus for ride comfort and handling balance.
- **Equation 4 ($\tau_{adh}$)** is a **quality-control metric** at every textile-rubber dipping plant — values below **6 N/mm** are grounds for batch rejection.
- **Equation 9 ($P_{burst}$)** is critical for **hydraulic hose safety certification** (EN 853 / SAE 100R standards).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt Model)

The Voigt (isostrain) model assumes that **both fibre and rubber experience the same strain** $\varepsilon$ when loaded along the fibre axis. This is the correct assumption for unidirectional cord reinforcement, since the cords dominate the load path.

**Given:**
- Composite volume $V_c = 1$, with $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction, $V_r$ = rubber volume fraction.
- $V_f + V_r = 1$
- Fibre modulus $E_f$, rubber modulus $E_r$.
- Composite modulus $E_c$ to be derived.

**Step 1 — Force Equilibrium**

The total load on the composite equals the sum of loads carried by each phase:

$$
F_c = F_f + F_r
$$

Since stress is force per unit area and we consider unit cross-sectional area:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_r V_r
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law to Each Phase**

$$
\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon \qquad
\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon \qquad
\sigma_r = E_r \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Cancel Strain**

$$
E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_r \varepsilon V_r
$$

Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon$ (assuming $\varepsilon \neq 0$):

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_r V_r
$$

This is the **Voigt Rule of Mixtures**, valid for unidirectional loading parallel to the fibre axis. Note that $E_c$ is bounded between $E_r$ (when $V_f = 0$) and $E_f$ (when $V_f = 1$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Peel Adhesion Strength from First Principles

The peel test is the **standard QC method** for rubber-textile bonding (ASTM D413, Type A — 180° peel).

**Setup:** A rubber strip is bonded to a fabric strip. The free ends are peeled apart at a constant 180° angle at a crosshead speed of **50 mm/min**.

**Step 1 — Define the test geometry**

Let $w$ be the width of the bonded strip (typically 25 mm). Let $F_{peel}$ be the average steady-state peel force recorded by the tensile testing machine.

**Step 2 — Define peel strength**

By definition, peel strength is the force per unit width required to separate the bond:

$$
\tau_{adh} = \frac{F_{peel}}{w}
$$

**Step 3 — Typical interpretation**

If the failure mode is **cohesive** (tearing within the rubber layer), the measured value represents the rubber tear strength, not the true adhesive bond strength. If failure is **adhesive** (clean separation at the rubber-fibre interface), the value represents the actual interfacial bond strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality control benchmark:** For RFL-dipped nylon cords in a typical tyre, peel strength must exceed **12 N/mm (rubber stock tear)** or **8 N/mm (adhesive)** to pass QC. Below this, the batch is rejected.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Burst Pressure of a Textile-Reinforced Rubber Hose

A hydraulic hose consists of an inner rubber tube, one or more textile braid layers, and an outer rubber cover. The **textile braid** is the primary pressure-bearing element.

**Given:**
- Hose inner diameter $D_i$
- Wall thickness of inner rubber tube $t$ (after braid compression)
- Hoop (circumferential) tensile strength of the braid $\sigma_{hoop}$

**Step 1 — Free-body of a half-cylinder of hose**

Consider a semi-cylindrical slice of hose. The internal pressure $P$ acts on the projected area $D_i \cdot L$ where $L$ is the length of the slice. The hoop stress in the braid resists this force over the cross-sectional area $2 t \cdot L$ (two wall sections, one on each cut edge).

**Step 2 — Force balance**

$$
P \cdot D_i \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for burst pressure**

$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t}{D_i}
$$

This is the **thin-wall cylinder formula** applied to a textile-reinforced elastomeric hose. The factor of safety is typically **4:1** (i.e., working pressure = $P_{burst} / 4$).

---
