## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Latex Moulding Techniques

Latex moulding/casting is broadly classified based on **how the latex is shaped and solidified**:

#### A. Dip Moulding (Most Common for Hollow Articles)
- The mould (a shaped **former**) is dipped into a tank of compounded latex.
- A layer of latex adheres to the mould.
- After withdrawal, the wet film is **leached, dried, and vulcanized**.
- Used for: **surgical/examination gloves, balloons, condoms, catheters, teats**.

#### B. Slush Moulding
- Latex is poured into a hollow mould.
- After partial gelation on the inner walls, the excess liquid latex is poured out.
- Used for: **hollow toys, dolls, mascots**.

#### C. Rotational Moulding / Centrifugal Casting
- Mould rotates about one or two axes while latex gels.
- Centrifugal force distributes latex evenly on mould walls.
- Used for: **large hollow articles, marine buoys**.

#### D. Electrodeposition (Anionic Process)
- Latex is negatively charged; the mould acts as an anode.
- On applying DC voltage, rubber particles migrate and coagulate on the mould surface.
- Used for: **complex-shaped articles, prototypes**.

#### E. Latex Foam Moulding (Dunlop & Talalay Processes)
- Whipped, foamed latex is poured into a mould and gelled.
- Used for: **mattresses, cushions, pillows**.

#### F. Investment Casting (for latex threads)
- Continuous filament is extruded through acid bath into moving water.

### 2.2 The Dip Moulding Process — Stepwise Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Sequence (KTU Board-Expected Answer Framework):**
> 1. **Mould Cleaning** → 2. **Coagulant Dipping** → 3. **Drying of Coagulant** → 4. **Latex Dipping** → 5. **Dwell Time** → 6. **Withdrawal** → 7. **Leaching** → 8. **Drying** → 9. **Curing/Vulcanization** → 10. **Stripping** → 11. **Post-cure Leaching** → 12. **Finishing**

Each step has a specific engineering rationale — explained below.

#### Step-by-Step Engineering "Why"

1. **Mould Cleaning**: Ceramic, glass, or aluminium formers must be free of dust, oil, and previous rubber residue. **Detergent + acid wash** followed by **bristle brushing** is standard.
2. **Coagulant Dipping**: The former is dipped into a bath of **calcium nitrate or calcium chloride (typically 10–25 % w/v in alcohol or water)**. Calcium ions destabilize the latex surfactants upon contact.
3. **Drying of Coagulant**: The wet mould is dried in an oven (**80–120 °C for 1–3 min**) to form a uniform thin film of calcium salt. This controls uniformity of gel formation.
4. **Latex Dipping**: The dried, coagulant-coated former is immersed in the compounded latex tank.
5. **Dwell Time (5–30 s)**: Longer dwell = thicker wall. During dwell, **Ca²⁺ ions diffuse from the coagulant layer into the latex, neutralizing the surfactant charge** (fatty acid soap / proteins) on the rubber particles, causing them to coalesce into a continuous wet gel on the mould surface.
6. **Withdrawal**: The mould is slowly withdrawn (rate **2–10 cm/s**). Withdrawal speed critically determines film thickness.
7. **Leaching**: The wet gel is immersed in hot water (**60–80 °C, 5–15 min**) to wash out **water-soluble proteins, surfactants, calcium salts, and unreacted additives**. Critical for **biocompatibility (reduces Type I latex allergy)**.
8. **Drying**: Water is evaporated (**80–120 °C, 15–30 min**).
9. **Curing (Vulcanization)**: Cross-linking occurs in a hot oven (**110–140 °C, 20–60 min**). Sulfur + accelerators in the compound react to form **polysulfide crosslinks** between rubber chains.
10. **Stripping**: Cured article is peeled off the former manually or by air-jet.
11. **Post-cure Leaching**: Final wash reduces extractable residues to regulatory limits (e.g., **< 50 µg/g protein** for medical gloves per ASTM D5712).
12. **Finishing**: Trimming, powdering (for gloves), printing, packaging.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU University Examinations. Memorize with boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{\eta \cdot v}{\rho \cdot g}}$ | **Landau–Levich equation** for film thickness picked up on a flat plate withdrawn from a Newtonian fluid. Applies to **non-coagulant dip moulding**. | $\delta$ in m, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $v$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 2 | $h = k \cdot \sqrt{t_{\text{dwell}}}$ | Wall thickness $\propto \sqrt{\text{dwell time}}$ for **coagulant dip moulding** | $h$ in mm, $t$ in s, $k$ = empirical constant |
| 3 | $\text{TSC} = \dfrac{M_{\text{dry}}}{M_{\text{wet}}} \times 100$ | Total Solid Content | **60–70 %** for centrifuged NR latex |
| 4 | $V_{\text{coag}} = C \cdot t_{\text{dip}} \cdot A$ | Coagulant uptake $\propto$ dip time × surface area | $C$ = uptake constant |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure on former walls during gelling | Pa |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{app}} = \eta_0 (1 + 2.5 \phi)$ | **Einstein viscosity equation** for dilute latex dispersions | $\phi$ = volume fraction rubber |
| 7 | $\zeta = \dfrac{4 \pi \eta u}{E}$ | Zeta potential from electrophoretic mobility | $\zeta$ in mV, $u$ in m²/Vs |
| 8 | $\text{DRC} = \dfrac{M_{\text{dry rubber}}}{M_{\text{latex}}} \times 100$ | Dry Rubber Content (mass of rubber only) | **≥ 60 %** for high-ammonia latex |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{2 \sigma}{r}$ | Electrodeposition thickness (simplified) | $E$ = deposit thickness |
| 10 | $h \propto \dfrac{\eta^{0.6} v^{0.6}}{(\rho g)^{0.2}}$ | Modified for non-Newtonian latex | Empirical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Practical Note for Examiners:** Equation 1 (Landau–Levich) is derived for a **flat plate** and assumes **no evaporation and no coagulant**. For actual latex with coagulant, Equation 2 is the operationally correct relation: **thickness grows with √(dwell time)** because diffusion of Ca²⁺ ions is the rate-limiting step.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Industry**: 200+ billion gloves/year are made by latex dip moulding.
- **Automotive**: Latex dipped bellows, grommets.
- **Toy Industry**: Slush moulded rubber toys (e.g., iconic rubber ducks).
- **Marine & Defence**: Centrifugally cast rubber fenders.
- **Balloons & Party Articles**: Dip moulded balloons — over **50 billion units/year globally**.
- **Catheters & Condoms**: Dip moulding with **straight-dip (no coagulant) using heat-sensitive latex** for ultra-thin films (**30–70 µm** for condoms).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Landau–Levich Equation for Non-Coagulant Dip Moulding

> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** It directly ties the physics of fluid mechanics to latex processing and is a **14-mark favourite** for "explain the principle of dip moulding with mathematical analysis."

#### Setup

Consider a flat vertical plate being withdrawn vertically upward from a large reservoir of Newtonian liquid (latex) at constant velocity $v$. A thin liquid film adheres to the plate due to viscous drag balanced by gravity and surface tension.

**Assumptions:**
- Steady state
- Newtonian fluid (constant viscosity $\eta$)
- Film is thin compared to plate dimensions
- Surface tension $\gamma$ acts at the free surface
- Gravity $g$ acts downward
- Inertial effects negligible (low Reynolds number, $\text{Re} < 1$)

#### Step 1 — Force Balance on the Film Element

Consider a small film element at height $y$ above the meniscus. The film thickness is $\delta(y)$. The forces acting on it are:

- **Viscous force** (from plate motion): $F_{\text{visc}} = \eta \dfrac{dv}{dy} \cdot A$
- **Gravity** (drainage): $F_{\text{grav}} = \rho g \cdot \delta \cdot A$
- **Surface tension** (at the free surface): $F_{\text{ST}} = \gamma \cdot \dfrac{d^2 \delta}{dy^2}$ (curvature contribution)

In the **steady-state dynamic meniscus region**, the balance reduces to:

$$\eta \frac{d^2 v}{dy^2} = \rho g$$

where $v(y)$ is the local fluid velocity in the film.

#### Step 2 — Integrating Across the Film Thickness

Integrating twice with boundary conditions:
- At $y = 0$ (plate surface): $v(0) = V$ (plate velocity)
- At $y = \delta$ (free surface): $\dfrac{dv}{dy}\Big|_{y=\delta} = 0$ (zero shear, free surface)

$$v(y) = V + \frac{\rho g}{2\eta}(\delta^2 - y^2)$$

#### Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate in the Film

The volumetric flow per unit width in the film:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{\delta} v(y) \, dy = V\delta - \frac{\rho g \delta^3}{3\eta}$$

#### Step 4 — Matching with the Static Meniscus

Far above the dynamic region, the film has **constant thickness $\delta$** and moves upward with the plate. Continuity requires:

$$Q = V\delta$$

From the meniscus region, a curvature analysis yields:

$$Q = \frac{\gamma \delta}{\eta} \cdot \text{(capillary number term)}$$

#### Step 5 — Final Expression

Setting the dynamic meniscus flow equal to the bulk film flow:

$$\frac{\rho g \delta^3}{3\eta} = \gamma \cdot \frac{V \delta^2}{\eta} \cdot \text{(constant)}$$

Solving for $\delta$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\eta V}{\rho g}} \cdot C$$

where $C$ is a numerical constant. The exact derivation by Landau & Levich (1942) gives $C = 0.944$, often approximated as $\approx \frac{1}{2}$ in introductory texts:

$$\boxed{\delta \approx \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\eta V}{\rho g}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Film thickness increases with **higher withdrawal speed** and **higher viscosity**, and decreases with **higher density** and **stronger gravity drainage**. This explains why **slow dipping of low-viscosity latex** yields thin films — the principle exploited in condom manufacturing (heat-sensitive latex, no coagulant, very slow withdrawal).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Hollow Article Processes

| Parameter | Dip Moulding | Slush Moulding | Rotational Casting |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Mould Type** | Solid former (male/female) | Hollow mould (female) | Hollow mould on rotating axis |
| **Wall Thickness Control** | Dwell time, withdrawal speed, coagulant | Pour time, gelling temp, rotation | Rotation speed, dwell time |
| **Hollow Formation** | Stripping creates a sealed cavity | Excess poured out → open cavity | Centrifugal distribution → closed hollow |
| **Typical Articles** | Gloves, condoms, balloons | Toys, dolls | Buoys, large containers |
| **Wall Thickness Range** | **0.03 – 1.0 mm** | **1 – 10 mm** | **3 – 25 mm** |
| **Cycle Time** | **5 – 30 min** | **30 min – 2 h** | **1 – 4 h** |
| **Capital Cost** | **Low–Medium** | **Medium** | **High** |
| **Surface Finish** | Excellent (mirror of former) | Good (inner surface) | Variable |
| **Skill Level Required** | High | Medium | Medium–High |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

**Problem:** A latex glove former is withdrawn from a coagulant-dipped latex bath at $V = 5\,\text{cm/s}$. The latex has viscosity $\eta = 0.05\,\text{Pa·s}$, density $\rho = 980\,\text{kg/m}^3$. Estimate the film thickness picked up if the process is non-coagulant (assume Landau–Levich valid).

**Solution:**

Convert units:
- $V = 0.05\,\text{m/s}$
- $\eta = 0.05\,\text{Pa·s}$
- $\rho = 980\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$

Apply Landau–Levich equation:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\eta V}{\rho g}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 0.05}{980 \times 9.81}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{0.0025}{9613.8}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2.6 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{2} \times 5.099 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$$

$$\delta \approx 2.55 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.255\,\text{mm}$$

**Answer:** Film thickness $\approx \mathbf{0.255\,mm}$ — a typical value for thin latex gloves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In real coagulant dipping**, this thickness is **substantially higher** (1–3 mm for gloves) because Ca²⁺ ions diffuse inward and gel a thicker layer. The Landau–Levich prediction is therefore a **lower bound**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define latex casting. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer:**
Latex casting is a polymer processing technique in which a compounded aqueous dispersion of natural or synthetic rubber (latex) is poured into a mould and solidified by **gelation, coagulation, or evaporation of water**, producing a seamless rubber article whose geometry conforms to the mould cavity. Unlike dry-rubber moulding, latex casting operates at **room or slightly elevated temperature**, does not require high shear mixing, and is particularly suited to **hollow, thin-walled articles** such as gloves, balloons, and catheters.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse latex casting with **dry rubber compression/injection moulding**. Always emphasize the **aqueous dispersion (colloidal)** nature of the starting material.

#### Question 2: Differentiate between dip moulding and slush moulding. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dip Moulding | Slush Moulding |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Mould type | Solid former (male or female) | Hollow mould (female) |
| Latex application | Mould is dipped into latex tank | Latex is poured into mould |
| Wall formation | On outer surface of former | On inner surface of mould |
| Hollow character | Article is inverted after stripping | Mould cavity is naturally hollow |
| Wall thickness control | Dwell time, withdrawal speed, coagulant | Pour time, gel temperature |
| Typical products | Gloves, balloons, condoms | Toys, dolls, mascots |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Dip Moulding Process in Detail

**(a) Explain the principle and process of dip moulding with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of each step in controlling wall thickness. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Dip moulding is based on the principle that when a clean, shaped former is dipped into a tank of compounded latex and withdrawn, a thin wet film of rubber adheres to the former surface. The film thickness is controlled by:
- **Dwell time** (longer = thicker)
- **Withdrawal speed** (Landau–Levich: $\delta \propto \sqrt{v}$)
- **Coagulant type and concentration**
- **Latex viscosity and TSC**

**Process Steps:**

1. **Mould Cleaning**: Aluminium or ceramic formers are washed with detergent and acid to remove oils, dust, and old rubber. Critical for uniform film formation.
2. **Coagulant Dipping**: Former is dipped in **calcium nitrate solution (15–25 % in alcohol/water)**. Calcium ions are the gelation trigger.
3. **Drying of Coagulant**: At **80–120 °C for 1–3 min**, forming a uniform thin Ca²⁺ salt layer.
4. **Latex Dipping**: Compounded latex with **sulfur, ZnO, accelerators, antioxidants** is filled in the tank. Former is immersed for **5–30 s**.
5. **Dwell Time**: Ca²⁺ ions diffuse into latex → neutralize surfactant → destabilize rubber particles → wet gel forms.
6. **Withdrawal**: At controlled speed, the former lifts out with adherent gel film.
7. **Leaching**: In hot water (**60–80 °C, 5–15 min**) — removes proteins (↓ allergy), salts, surfactants.
8. **Drying**: At **80–120 °C, 15–30 min** — water evaporation.
9. **Vulcanization**: At **110–140 °C, 20–60 min** — sulfur crosslinks form.
10. **Stripping**: Cured article is removed.
11. **Post-cure Leaching & Finishing**.

**Wall Thickness Control:**
- Increase **dwell time** → more Ca²⁺ diffusion → thicker wall.
- Increase **coagulant concentration** → more ions available → thicker wall.
- Higher **latex TSC** → more rubber per unit volume → thicker wall.
- Higher **latex viscosity** → higher Landau–Levich pickup.
- **Slower withdrawal** → thinner wall.

**(b) Discuss the different types of dip moulding (straight, coagulant, and heat-sensitive). Compare their applications. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Straight Dip Moulding**
- No coagulant used.
- Latex itself gels by **water evaporation** as the wet film is dried.
- Very thin films (**20–80 µm**).
- Slow process; not suitable for thick walls.
- **Application**: Condoms, ultra-thin films, latex threads.

**2. Coagulant Dip Moulding**
- Calcium nitrate / calcium chloride used.
- **Most widely used** industrially.
- Wall thickness **0.05–3 mm** controlled by dwell time.
- **Application**: Surgical/examination gloves, balloons, catheters, finger cots.

**3. Heat-Sensitive Dip Moulding**
- Latex contains **ammonium alginate / zinc oxide complex** as a thermal sensitizer.
- At room temperature latex is fluid; on contact with **heated former (50–80 °C)** it gels instantly.
- No coagulant bath needed → cleaner process.
- Excellent for **complex-shaped articles**.
- **Application**: Anatomical shapes, intricate toys.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Straight Dip | Coagulant Dip | Heat-Sensitive |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| Gelation trigger | Water evaporation | Ca²⁺ ions | Heat + sensitizer |
| Wall thickness | Very thin (20–80 µm) | 0.05–3 mm | 0.1–1 mm |
| Speed | Slow | Medium | Fast |
| Process cleanliness | High | Moderate (coagulant residue) | High |
| Equipment cost | Low | Medium | Higher |
| Typical product | Condoms | Gloves, balloons | Anatomical items |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Hollow Article Production & Foams

**(a) Explain the production of hollow articles by latex casting. Discuss the materials of construction for moulds and their selection criteria. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Production of Hollow Articles:**

Hollow latex articles are produced predominantly by **dip moulding** (most economical) or **slush/rotational casting** for larger items.

**Mould (Former) Materials and Selection:**

| Material | Surface Finish | Thermal Conductivity | Durability | Application |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Aluminium** | Excellent | High | Very high | Mass-production gloves |
| **Ceramic (Porcelain)** | Very good | Medium | High | Surgical gloves, smooth finish |
| **Glass** | Excellent | Low | Fragile | Prototypes, laboratory |
| **Stainless Steel** | Excellent | High | Very high | Clean-room, medical |
| **Plaster of Paris** | Porous (water-absorbing) | Low | Medium | Special coagulant-free casting |
| **Wood (coated)** | Variable | Low | Low | Prototypes only |
| **3D-Printed Polymer (ABS/PLA)** | Variable | Low | Medium | Rapid prototyping |

**Selection Criteria:**
- **Surface finish**: Smoother former → smoother glove.
- **Thermal conductivity**: For heat-sensitive dipping, **aluminium** is preferred (rapid heating).
- **Chemical resistance**: To coagulant, acid, surfactant.
- **Mechanical durability**: Aluminium and stainless steel last for **millions of cycles**.
- **Cost**: Ceramic formers are **cheaper but fragile**; aluminium is **expensive but durable**.
- **Release characteristics**: Slightly rough former gives better mechanical interlock; mirror finish gives easier stripping.

**(b) Describe the production of latex foam by the Dunlop process. Explain the chemistry of gelling and why ZnO and sodium silicofluoride are used. (7 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Latex Foam and the Dunlop Process:**

Latex foam is an **open-cell, sponge-like rubber** made by whipping latex to a froth, pouring it into a mould, and gelling it.

**Dunlop Process Steps:**

1. **Compounding**: NR latex (TSC ~62 %) is compounded with **sulfur, ZnO, accelerator (e.g., ZDC), antioxidant, soap (potassium oleate), gelling agent (sodium silicofluoride, SSF), and frothing aid**.
2. **Frothing (Whipping)**: Air is beaten into the latex using a **Oakes or Hobart-type mixer** until foam volume is **3–4× original**.
3. **Foam Stabilization**: Potassium oleate soap stabilizes the foam bubbles.
4. **Pouring into Mould**: Foam is poured into an aluminium mould.
5. **Gelling**: SSF slowly hydrolyses:
   $$\text{Na}_2\text{SiF}_6 \longrightarrow 2\text{NaF} + \text{SiF}_4$$
   $$\text{SiF}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 4\text{HF}$$
   The **HF generated lowers pH** and destabilizes the soap (potassium oleate) → foam gels.
6. **Vulcanization**: Mould is heated in steam (**100 °C, 20–30 min**).
7. **Stripping & Washing**: Foam block is removed, washed to remove fluoride residues.
8. **Drying**: At **80–100 °C** to remove water.

**Role of ZnO:**
- **Activator** for sulfur vulcanization (activates accelerators like ZDC/MBT).
- **pH buffer** — maintains alkaline conditions during compounding; reacts with fatty acid soap to form **zinc soap** which improves foam stability.

**Role of SSF (Sodium Silicofluoride):**
- **Delayed-action gelling agent**. Hydrolyses slowly → gradual pH drop → controlled gelation.
- Allows time for **pouring and mould filling** before the foam sets.

**Applications**: Mattresses, pillows, cushioning, carpet underlay, automotive seating.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Conflating dip moulding with compression moulding** — these are entirely different processes. Always specify the **aqueous nature** of the latex.
> 2. **Omitting the role of surfactants** — KTU examiners expect mention of **fatty acid soap, proteins, or potassium oleate** as colloidal stabilizers whose neutralization triggers gelation.
> 3. **Forgetting protein-leaching** — explicitly mentioning **leaching** as a step for **biocompatibility** (ASTM D5712, D6499) scores extra marks in medical-device questions.
> 4. **Confusing Dunlop and Talalay foam processes** — Dunlop uses **SSF (chemical gelling)**, Talalay uses **vacuum + CO₂ + freeze gelling**. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not providing numerical units** in formulas — always state **Pa·s, kg/m³, m/s, °C, min** explicitly.
> 6. **Skipping the difference between TSC and DRC** — examiners love this distinction. TSC includes all non-volatile solids; DRC is rubber-only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ **Latex casting** = shaping liquid aqueous rubber dispersion; no high shear; no mastication.
- ✅ **Dip moulding** = former dipped in latex → wet gel → dried → vulcanized → stripped.
- ✅ **Wall thickness** in coagulant dip moulding: $h = k \sqrt{t_{\text{dwell}}}$.
- ✅ **Wall thickness** in non-coagulant dip moulding (Landau–Levich): $\delta = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\eta V}{\rho g}}$.
- ✅ **Coagulant**: Calcium nitrate or calcium chloride (**15–25 % in alcohol**); **Ca²⁺** destabilizes surfactant on rubber particles.
- ✅ **Heat-sensitive latex**: Ammonium alginate + ZnO; gels on contact with **heated former (50–80 °C)**.
- ✅ **Straight dip** = no coagulant, ultra-thin films, used for **condoms and latex threads**.
- ✅ **Coagulant dip** = standard for **gloves, balloons, catheters** — most common industrial method.
- ✅ **Slush moulding** = hollow toys, dolls, mascots.
- ✅ **Rotational casting** = marine buoys, large hollow containers.
- ✅ **Electrodeposition** = negatively charged rubber particles migrate to anode (former) under DC.
- ✅ **Dunlop foam process** = whipped latex + SSF gelling agent → foam mattress/cushion.
- ✅ **SSF (Sodium silicofluoride)** = delayed-action gelling agent; hydrolyses to release HF → pH drop → gel.
- ✅ **ZnO** = activator for sulfur vulcanization + foam pH buffer.
- ✅ **Leaching** removes proteins, surfactants, and salts → critical for **biocompatibility** (↓ Type I latex allergy).
- ✅ **Formers**: Aluminium (mass production), Ceramic (smooth finish), Glass/PLA (prototypes), Plaster (porous for water-absorption casting).
- ✅ **Vulcanization temperature**: **110–140 °C, 20–60 min**; sulfur + accelerator system.
- ✅ **TSC** of centrifuged NR latex = **60–70 %**; **DRC** ≥ **60 %**.
- ✅ **MST (Mechanical Stability Time)** ≥ **650 s** at 14000 rpm.
- ✅ **Condoms**: thickness ~**30–70 µm** (straight dip, heat-sensitive).
- ✅ **Surgical gloves**: thickness ~**0.1–0.3 mm** (coagulant dip).
- ✅ **Heavy-duty industrial gloves**: thickness up to **1–3 mm**.
- ✅ Always remember the **3 W's of dip moulding**: **Wetting, Withdrawal, Wall formation**.
- ✅ KTU examiners **always test**: coagulation chemistry (Ca²⁺ + soap), Landau–Levich formula, Dunlop vs Talalay distinction, and coagulant selection criteria.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Solid Articles from Latex — Section 1

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Official Terminology)**
> **Solid Articles** in latex technology refer to elastomeric products manufactured from **natural rubber latex concentrate** (typically **60%** Total Solid Content / TSC) through casting, dipping, molding, or extrusion techniques, where the finished product retains a **solid, non-porous, dense rubber structure** after the aqueous serum phase is removed via coagulation, drying, and vulcanization.

### 1.1 Formal Classification of Solid Latex Articles

Solid articles form one of the **four major product categories** in the latex industry. The other three are **latex foam, latex thread, and latex gloves**. Solid articles are characterized by:

- **Solid cross-section** (no cellular/foam structure)
- **High density** in finished form
- **Continuous polymer matrix**
- **Predominantly produced by dipping or casting methods**

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Chocolate Mould" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy for B.Tech Students:**
> Think of solid latex articles like **making shaped chocolates at home**. You have a *liquid chocolate base* (this is your **latex compound**), you pour it into a *shaped mould* (the **former or mould**), and then you chill it so the chocolate **solidifies and takes the shape** of the mould. Similarly, in latex technology:
> - The **latex compound** (liquid state) is poured, dipped, or slushed around a **mould (former)**.
> - A **coagulant or heat** triggers destabilization of colloidal rubber particles.
> - The rubber particles **fuse together** on the mould surface forming a **solid skin/layer**.
> - After drying and vulcanization, the **solid article** is stripped off the mould — exactly like popping out a frozen chocolate from its mould!

### 1.3 Primary Categories of Solid Latex Articles

| Category | Manufacturing Method | Typical Products |
|----------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Dipped Articles** | Former dipping into latex compound | Gloves, balloons, catheters, condoms, finger cots |
| **Cast Articles** | Latex poured into plaster/rubber moulds | Toys, anatomical models, shoe soles, industrial goods |
| **Slush Moulded Articles** | Rotational casting in metal moulds | Hollow toys, boots, industrial hollow parts |
| **Extruded Articles** | Forced through die under pressure | Latex tubing, profiles, cords |
| **Injection Moulded Articles** | Pressed into closed moulds | Stoppers, valves, gaskets, automotive parts |

### 1.4 Essential Material Parameters (Bold Constants)

The following standard parameters govern every solid latex article:

- **Total Solid Content (TSC): 60%** (standard latex concentrate)
- **Mechanical Stability Time (MST): ≥ 650 seconds**
- **Volatile Fatty Acid Number (VFA): ≤ 0.05** (for low-ammonia latex)
- **pH: 9.5 – 10.5**
- **KOH Number: 0.5 – 1.0**
- **Coagulant Dip Concentration (Calcium Nitrate): 5 – 25% (w/v)**
- **Vulcanization Temperature: 100 – 140°C**
- **Drying Temperature: 60 – 80°C**
- **Pre-Vulcanization Time: 1 – 5 minutes** (at 70°C)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always remember the **"Three Ts"** of latex dipping:
> **T**ank (latex compound tank) → **T**ime of dwell → **T**emperature of oven curing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4: Solid Articles — Section 2

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Science Behind Latex → Solid Conversion

Natural rubber latex is a **colloidal dispersion** of polyisoprene particles (200 – 600 nm diameter) in an aqueous serum. The transition from **liquid latex** to **solid rubber article** involves **destabilization of the colloidal system**, followed by **particle coalescence and crosslinking**.

#### 2.1.1 Colloidal Stability Parameters

Latex is stabilized by:
1. **Negative surface charge (ζ-potential ≈ −40 mV)** on rubber particles.
2. **Adsorbed protein layer** providing steric + electrostatic stabilization.
3. **Fatty acid soaps** (from added ammonia or KOH).

> [!NOTE]
> When a **coagulant** (e.g., Ca(NO₃)₂) is introduced, the **divalent Ca²⁺ ions neutralize the negative ζ-potential**, reducing electrostatic repulsion. The rubber particles then **coagulate** on the former surface, forming a **wet gel layer** that progressively densifies into solid rubber.

### 2.2 Process Flow Analysis — Coagulant Dipping Method

This is the **most industrially important** method for dipped solid articles and **highly examinable in KTU**.

**Step 1 — Former Preparation:**
The mould (glass, ceramic, or stainless steel) is cleaned, then optionally pre-heated to **40 – 60°C** to improve coagulant adherence.

**Step 2 — Coagulant Dipping:**
The former is dipped into a **calcium nitrate solution** (typically **10 – 25% w/v**) for **5 – 30 seconds**, then dried. A uniform salt layer is deposited.

**Step 3 — Latex Dipping:**
The coagulant-coated former is dipped into the **compounded latex tank** for a controlled **dwell time** (typically **15 – 60 seconds**). The Ca²⁺ ions diffuse from the former surface into the latex, triggering **progressive coagulation** inward.

**Step 4 — Withdrawal & Gelling:**
The former is withdrawn slowly (typically **10 – 25 cm/min** withdrawal speed). The coagulum thickness builds as more ions diffuse.

**Step 5 — Leaching:**
The wet gel is immersed in **hot water (60 – 80°C)** for **15 – 60 minutes** to remove water-soluble proteins, soaps, and excess coagulant — improving clarity and reducing allergenicity.

**Step 6 — Drying & Pre-vulcanization:**
The wet article is dried in an **oven at 70 – 100°C** for **20 – 60 minutes**, often combined with **pre-vulcanization** (if prevulcanized latex is used).

**Step 7 — Stripping:**
The dried solid rubber article is **manually or mechanically stripped** from the former.

### 2.3 Compounding Recipe (Model Industrial Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Compounding Recipe (parts per hundred rubber, phr):**

| Ingredient | Function | Quantity (phr) |
|------------|----------|----------------|
| **Latex Concentrate (60% TSC)** | Base polymer | 100.0 |
| **Sulphur** | Vulcanizing agent | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| **Zinc Oxide (ZnO)** | Activator | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| **Zinc Diethyldithiocarbamate (ZDEC)** | Accelerator | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| **Antioxidant (Wingstay-L or BHT)** | Ageing resistance | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)** | Stabilizer | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| **Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂)** | Pigment / Opacifier | 2.0 – 5.0 |
| **Pigments (if coloured)** | Colour | 1.0 – 3.0 |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $C_d = k \sqrt{t_d}$ | Coagulum depth $C_d$ vs dwell time $t_d$ (diffusion-controlled) | mm, s |
| 2 | $k = f(C, T)$ | Constant $k$ depends on coagulant concentration $C$ and temperature $T$ | — |
| 3 | $\mathrm{MST} = t_{c}$ | Mechanical Stability Time (minimum coagulation time under stress) | seconds |
| 4 | $VFA = \frac{V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH} \times 60}{m}$ | Volatile Fatty Acid Number | — |
| 5 | $\zeta_{critical} = -15 \text{ mV}$ | Critical ζ-potential below which coagulation begins | mV |
| 6 | $Q = \frac{m_{dry}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | Solid content of finished article (%) | % |
| 7 | $d_{film} = v_{withdrawal} \times t_{dwell} \times \rho_{latex}$ | Approximate wet film thickness (kinematic model) | mm |
| 8 | $E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$ | Young's Modulus of cured solid rubber | MPa |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{C_p}{C_{p,0}}$ | Relative viscosity of compounded latex | — |
| 10 | $V_v = \frac{\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{latex}} \times 100$ | Volume fraction of solid rubber formed | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Always use `\vert` instead of `|` in markdown tables** to avoid breaking the table parser. For absolute values in equations, use $\vert x \vert$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solid latex articles dominate several industrial sectors:

- **Medical Industry:** Surgical gloves, examination gloves, catheters, balloon-tipped medical devices, finger cots.
- **Consumer Goods:** Toys (especially slush-moulded hollow figures), balloons, swim caps, dishwashing gloves.
- **Automotive:** Gaskets, vibration dampers, bellows, sealing rings.
- **Footwear:** Shoe soles, heels, slipper uppers (cast latex).
- **Industrial:** Rubber threads (precursor to elastic fabric), tubing, extruded profiles, valve seals.

### 2.6 Critical Process Variables & Their Effects

| Variable | Low Value Effect | High Value Effect |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Coagulant concentration** | Thin film, possible holes | Thick film, rough surface |
| **Dwell time** | Underweight article | Overweight article, sagging |
| **Drying temperature** | Incomplete drying, tacky surface | Scorching, bubble formation |
| **Vulcanization time** | Undercure (tacky, weak) | Overcure (brittle, cracked) |
| **Withdrawal speed** | Uneven thickness | Drips, sag marks, wrinkling |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4: Solid Articles — Section 3

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coagulum Thickness vs. Dwell Time (Diffusion Model)

This is a **classic KTU ESE question** worth **7 marks**. The derivation assumes **Fickian diffusion** of coagulant ions through the forming coagulum layer.

**Starting Physical Statement:**

The thickness of the coagulum layer $C_d$ (in mm) formed on a former surface is governed by the diffusion of **Ca²⁺ ions** from the coagulant layer into the adjacent latex. This is mathematically analogous to **one-dimensional unsteady-state diffusion** through a semi-infinite medium.

**Governing Diffusion Equation (Fick's Second Law):**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

Where:
- $C$ = concentration of Ca²⁺ ions at depth $x$ and time $t$
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of Ca²⁺ in latex (approximately $5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$)
- $x$ = depth from former surface
- $t$ = dwell time

**Boundary Conditions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C(x, 0) &= 0 \quad \text{for } x > 0 \quad \text{(no coagulant initially in latex)} \\
C(0, t) &= C_s \quad \text{for } t > 0 \quad \text{(constant surface concentration)} \\
C(\infty, t) &= 0 \quad \text{(semi-infinite medium assumption)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution using Error Function (erf):**

$$\frac{C(x,t)}{C_s} = 1 - \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

**Defining Coagulum Front:**

The coagulum front is defined as the position $x = C_d$ where $C = C_{critical}$ (minimum concentration required to destabilize the latex). This gives:

$$\mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{C_d}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = 1 - \frac{C_{critical}}{C_s}$$

**Linearization for Practical Use:**

For typical industrial conditions where $C_s \gg C_{critical}$, the error function is approximated linearly, leading to:

$$C_d = k \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

Where $k$ is an empirical constant determined experimentally.

$$\boxed{C_d \propto \sqrt{t_d}}$$

**Final Compact Expression:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_d &= k \cdot \sqrt{t_d} \\
k &= f(C_{coagulant}, T, \zeta\text{-potential of latex})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The square-root dependence means **doubling the dwell time only increases film thickness by a factor of 1.414 (≈41%)**, not 100%. This is why industrial dipping is **time-controlled** for precise thickness regulation.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A coagulant-dipped former produces a rubber film thickness of **0.45 mm** in **25 seconds** of dwell time. Predict the thickness if dwell time is increased to **100 seconds**, assuming diffusion-controlled behaviour.

**Given:**
$$C_{d1} = 0.45 \text{ mm}, \quad t_{d1} = 25 \text{ s}, \quad t_{d2} = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the diffusion model:**

$$C_d = k \cdot \sqrt{t_d}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for k using the first condition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.45 &= k \cdot \sqrt{25} \\
0.45 &= k \cdot 5 \\
k &= 0.09 \text{ mm/s}^{0.5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Predict thickness at t = 100 s:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{d2} &= 0.09 \cdot \sqrt{100} \\
C_{d2} &= 0.09 \times 10 \\
\boxed{C_{d2} &= 0.90 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling the square of the time (25→100, factor 4) **doubles the film thickness (0.45→0.90 mm)**, validating the $\sqrt{t}$ relationship.

### 3.3 Derivation: Solid Content of Finished Article

The **TSC of finished dry rubber** can be calculated from the wet weight loss during drying.

**Starting Definition:**

$$Q = \frac{m_{dry}}{m_{wet}} \times 100\%$$

**For a dipped coagulum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{wet} &= m_{rubber} + m_{water} + m_{soap} + m_{protein} \\
m_{dry} &= m_{rubber} + m_{non-extractables}
\end{aligned}$$

**After full leaching + drying:**

$$Q_{finished} \approx 95 - 98\%$$

> [!TIP]
> This is why **dipped latex articles** are sold by **dry weight and thickness**, not by wet weight — the wet stage contains **40 – 50% water** which must be removed.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Cure

| Defect | Likely Cause | Corrective Action |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Pin holes / thin spots** | Insufficient coagulant; air bubbles in latex | Increase Ca(NO₃)₂ concentration; degas latex |
| **Thick, uneven film** | Excessive dwell time; high coagulant | Reduce dwell time; lower coagulant concentration |
| **Wrinkled surface** | Fast withdrawal speed | Reduce withdrawal to 10–20 cm/min |
| **Tacky after cure** | Under-vulcanization; insufficient sulphur | Increase cure time or sulphur |
| **Brittle, cracked** | Over-vulcanization; excessive accelerator | Reduce accelerator; lower cure temp |
| **Yellowing / discolouration** | Excessive antioxidant migration | Reduce antioxidant; check ZnO quality |
| **Poor demoulding** | Inadequate drying; sticky surface | Extend drying time; apply talc release agent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 4: Solid Articles — Section 4

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Coagulant Dipping Line for Solid Articles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Latex Concentrate 60 percent TSC] --> B[Compounding Tank with Stirrer]
    B --> C[Maturation Tank 24 to 48 hours]
    C --> D[Pre Vulcanization Optional 70 deg C]
    D --> E[Latex Dipping Tank Main Process]
    F[Former Mould Cleaning Station] --> G[Coagulant Dip Tank CaNO3 10 to 25 percent]
    G --> H[Coagulant Drying Oven]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Wet Coagulum on Former]
    I --> J[Leaching Tank 60 to 80 deg C Hot Water]
    J --> K[Drying and Vulcanization Oven]
    K --> L[Stripping Station Manual or Auto]
    L --> M[Inspection and QC Testing]
    M --> N[Packaged Solid Latex Article]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Latex Dipping Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PreDip[Pre Dipping Module]
        A1[Former Holder] --> A2[Coagulant Tank]
        A2 --> A3[Drying Zone A]
    end
    subgraph Dipping[Main Dipping Module]
        B1[Compounded Latex Tank 28 deg C] --> B2[Dwell Zone 15 to 60 sec]
        B2 --> B3[Withdrawal Zone 10 to 25 cm per min]
    end
    subgraph PostDip[Post Dipping Module]
        C1[Leaching Tank 70 deg C] --> C2[Drying Oven 80 deg C]
        C2 --> C3[Vulcanization Zone 100 to 140 deg C]
        C3 --> C4[Stripping Robot]
    end
    PreDip --> Dipping
    Dipping --> PostDip
    C4 --> D1[Finished Solid Article]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Solid Article Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Solid Latex Articles] --> SA[Dipped Articles]
    S --> SB[Cast Articles]
    S --> SC[Slush Moulded]
    S --> SD[Extruded Articles]

    SB --> SB2[Anatomical Models]

    SC --> SC2[Boots]
    SC --> SC3[Hollow Industrial Parts]
    SD --> SD1[Tubing]
    SD --> SD2[Profiles]
    SD --> SD3[Cords]
    SE --> SE1[Stoppers]
    SE --> SE2[Valves]
    SE --> SE3[Gaskets]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Coagulum Build-Up

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M[Cross Section of Former with Latex Film]
        F1[Former Surface Glass or Ceramic] --> F2[Coagulant Layer CaNO3 0.1 mm]
        F2 --> F3[Inner Wet Coagulum Zone Active Diffusion]
        F3 --> F4[Densified Gel Zone Solid Rubber]
        F4 --> F5[Outer Wet Surface in Contact with Latex]
    end
```

### 4.5 Quality Control & Testing Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Production Batch] --> Q1[Thickness Measurement]
    P --> Q2[Tensile Strength Test]

    Q1 --> R{Acceptance Criteria Met}
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    Q5 --> R
    R -->|Yes| OK[Approved Batch to Packing]
    R -->|No| RR[Reject and Reprocess]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 4: Solid Articles — Section 5

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define solid latex articles. List any four examples of solid articles produced from natural rubber latex.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Solid latex articles are dense, non-porous, solid cross-section rubber products manufactured from natural rubber latex concentrate by dipping, casting, slush moulding, or extrusion processes, where the aqueous phase is removed by coagulation, leaching, and drying to yield a continuous crosslinked rubber matrix.

**Examples (1.5 Marks — any four):**
1. Surgical / examination gloves
2. Latex balloons
3. Catheters and tubing
4. Slush-moulded toys
5. Condoms
6. Cast shoe soles
7. Injection-moulded rubber stoppers

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the function of a coagulant in the manufacture of dipped solid latex articles? Name any two coagulants commonly used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Function of Coagulant (2 Marks):**
A coagulant destabilizes the colloidal latex by neutralizing the negative surface charge (ζ-potential) on the rubber particles. The divalent cations (e.g., Ca²⁺) compress the electrical double layer, reducing inter-particle repulsion. This causes the rubber particles to **coagulate** on the former surface, forming a uniform wet gel layer that progressively densifies into solid rubber upon drying and vulcanization.

**Two Coagulants (1 Mark):**
1. Calcium nitrate — Ca(NO₃)₂ (most common)
2. Calcium chloride — CaCl₂
3. (Also accepted: Aluminium nitrate, Barium chloride)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**With a neat flow diagram, describe the manufacturing process of dipped solid latex articles by the coagulant dipping method. Discuss the role of each processing step.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The coagulant dipping method is the most widely used industrial process for manufacturing dipped solid latex articles such as gloves, balloons, and catheters.

**Process Steps (5 Marks):**

1. **Former Preparation:** Glass/ceramic formers are cleaned and pre-heated to ensure uniform coagulant adherence.

2. **Coagulant Dipping:** Formers are dipped into a **10–25% calcium nitrate solution** for **5–30 seconds**, then dried to deposit a uniform salt film.

3. **Latex Dipping:** The coagulant-coated former is dipped into compounded latex for a controlled **dwell time (15–60 s)**. Ca²⁺ ions diffuse inward, destabilizing the latex and forming a wet coagulum.

4. **Withdrawal:** Slow, controlled withdrawal (10–25 cm/min) ensures uniform thickness.

5. **Leaching:** The wet gel is immersed in hot water (60–80°C) for 15–60 minutes to remove water-soluble proteins, soaps, and excess salts.

6. **Drying & Vulcanization:** The article is dried at 70–100°C and vulcanized at 100–140°C.

7. **Stripping:** The finished solid article is removed from the former.

**Role of Each Step (1 Mark):**
Each step contributes to **film uniformity, mechanical strength, biocompatibility, and surface finish** — collectively ensuring the product meets medical/industrial specifications.

**Conclusion (Optional 0.5–1 Mark):**
The process yields **dimensionally accurate, biocompatible, and mechanically robust** solid latex articles in high volume.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the principle of coagulation in latex dipping. Derive the relation between coagulum thickness and dwell time.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Coagulation (3 Marks):**
Natural rubber latex is a colloidal dispersion stabilized by **negative ζ-potential** (≈ −40 mV) on rubber particles. When the former coated with Ca(NO₃)₂ is dipped into the latex tank, **Ca²⁺ ions diffuse outward** from the coagulant layer into the adjacent latex film. These divalent cations **neutralize the surface charge**, lowering the ζ-potential below the critical value of **−15 mV**. At this point, **van der Waals attractive forces dominate**, causing rubber particles to coalesce and form a continuous wet gel layer on the former surface.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

The diffusion follows Fick's Second Law:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

Applying the boundary conditions (semi-infinite medium, constant surface concentration $C_s$, initial bulk concentration zero), the solution is:

$$C(x,t) = C_s \cdot \left[1 - \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$$

Defining the coagulum front $x = C_d$ where $C = C_{critical}$:

$$\mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{C_d}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = 1 - \frac{C_{critical}}{C_s}$$

For practical industrial conditions where $C_s \gg C_{critical}$, this reduces to:

$$\boxed{C_d = k \cdot \sqrt{t_d}}$$

Where $k$ is an empirical constant dependent on **coagulant concentration, temperature, and ζ-potential of latex**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Describe the compounding of natural rubber latex for the manufacture of solid articles. Explain the function of each major ingredient.**

**Model Answer:**

**Compounding Recipe (3 Marks):**

A typical compounded latex recipe (in **phr — parts per hundred rubber**) for solid articles is:

| Ingredient | phr |
|------------|-----|
| Latex concentrate (60% TSC) | 100.0 |
| Sulphur | 1.5 |
| Zinc oxide | 1.0 |
| ZDEC (accelerator) | 0.8 |
| Antioxidant (Wingstay-L) | 1.5 |
| KOH (stabilizer) | 0.3 |
| TiO₂ (pigment) | 3.0 |

**Function of Each Ingredient (4 Marks):**

1. **Sulphur (1–2.5 phr):** Crosslinking agent — forms **disulphide bridges** between polyisoprene chains, providing **elastic recovery and tensile strength**.
2. **Zinc Oxide (0.5–1.5 phr):** Activator — forms soluble Zn-sulphur complexes that accelerate vulcanization.
3. **ZDEC (0.5–1.0 phr):** Ultra-fast accelerator — drastically reduces cure time and temperature.
4. **Antioxidant (1–2 phr):** Inhibits oxidative degradation by peroxide radicals; extends service life.
5. **KOH (0.2–0.5 phr):** Maintains alkalinity (pH > 9.5), preventing premature coagulation.
6. **TiO₂ (2–5 phr):** Provides whiteness/opacity; also reinforces the rubber matrix.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Discuss the various methods of manufacturing solid latex articles other than dipping. Compare their advantages and limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Four Alternative Methods (5 Marks):**

1. **Cast Latex Process:**
   - Compounded latex is poured into **plaster-of-Paris or rubber moulds**.
   - Water is absorbed by the porous mould, causing coagulation.
   - Used for: toys, anatomical models, shoe soles.
   - **Advantage:** Simple, low-cost tooling. **Limitation:** Slow, manual process.

2. **Slush Moulding:**
   - Latex is poured into a **heated metal mould**, mould is rotated to coat inner walls, excess latex is poured out.
   - Used for: hollow toys, boots.
   - **Advantage:** Hollow seamless articles. **Limitation:** High energy cost.

3. **Extrusion:**
   - Latex is forced through a **die** under pressure into a **coagulant bath**, forming continuous profiles.
   - Used for: tubing, threads, cords.
   - **Advantage:** Continuous production. **Limitation:** Limited to uniform cross-sections.

4. **Injection Moulding:**
   - Latex is injected into **closed heated moulds** under pressure.
   - Used for: stoppers, gaskets, valves.
   - **Advantage:** High precision and automation. **Limitation:** High capital cost.

**Comparison (2 Marks):**

| Method | Capital Cost | Production Speed | Article Complexity |
|--------|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Cast** | Low | Slow | Medium |
| **Slush Mould** | Medium | Medium | Hollow articles only |
| **Extrusion** | High | Very fast | Uniform cross-section |
| **Injection** | Very high | Fast | High precision |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Students write "dipped articles are made by dipping latex into moulds." — **WRONG**. The **mould is dipped into the latex tank**, not the other way around.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Confusing **coagulant dipping** with **straight dipping**. In coagulant dipping, the mould is **pre-coated with Ca(NO₃)₂**. In straight dipping, the latex itself contains the coagulant — this is rare and gives different thickness profiles.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Omitting the **leaching step**. Without leaching, **water-soluble proteins remain** and cause **Type I latex hypersensitivity** (allergic reactions). Always mention leaching for medical articles.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Forgetting units in the $C_d = k\sqrt{t}$ formula. $k$ has units of **mm/s^0.5**, NOT dimensionless.
> - **Common mistake #5:** Writing "vulcanization at room temperature" — vulcanization **always requires heat** (typically 100–140°C).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solid Articles from Latex:**

- Solid latex articles are **dense, non-porous** rubber products with **continuous polymer matrix**, distinct from foam and thread.
- The **colloidal stability** of latex arises from **negative ζ-potential** and **adsorbed protein/soap layers**.
- **Coagulant dipping** is the **most industrially dominant** method — it uses **Ca(NO₃)₂** as the standard coagulant at **10–25% w/v**.
- **Film thickness** follows the diffusion-controlled relation: $C_d = k \cdot \sqrt{t_d}$ — **square-root dependence on dwell time**.
- The **"Seven Steps"** of dipping: Former prep → Coagulant dip → Drying → Latex dip → Leaching → Drying & vulcanization → Stripping.
- **Compounding essentials** include sulphur, ZnO, accelerator (ZDEC/ZDBC), antioxidant, and KOH stabilizer.
- **Pre-vulcanized latex (PVL)** allows vulcanization **before dipping** — safer, lower energy, used for thin articles.
- **Alternative methods** include cast latex, slush mould, extrusion, and injection moulding — each chosen based on **article geometry, volume, and cost**.
- **Critical process parameters** to remember: coagulant concentration, dwell time, withdrawal speed, leaching temperature, drying temperature.
- **Quality tests** for solid articles: tensile strength, elongation at break, thickness uniformity, ageing resistance, and visual inspection.
- **Biocompatibility** is achieved through **proper leaching** to remove proteins — critical for medical gloves and catheters.
- The **four major solid article categories** by manufacturing route are: **Dipped, Cast, Slush Moulded, Extruded/Injection Moulded**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "C-D-L-D-V-S":**
> **C**oagulant dip → **D**ry → **L**atex dip → **D**well → **V**ulcanize → **S**trip.
> This six-letter sequence is the **complete dipping workflow** — recite it during revision!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Porous Moulds in Casting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT303 - LATEX TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Use of Porous Moulds in Casting

# Use of Porous Moulds in Casting

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **porous mould** (also called a *permeable mould*) is a casting mould fabricated from a material that possesses an interconnected network of micro-voids or capillaries, allowing gases, air, and steam generated during the pouring and solidification of molten metal to escape through the mould body rather than becoming trapped inside the casting. The defining property is **permeability (P)**, which is the quantitative measure of the ability of the mould to permit fluid flow through its pores, expressed as the **AFS Permeability Number** in foundry practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> A porous mould is a refractory mould containing uniformly distributed, interconnected pores of controlled size that permit the escape of gases, air, and mould vapours during casting. The *degree of porosity* and *pore size distribution* together govern mould permeability, dimensional accuracy, and surface finish of the cast product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring hot coffee through a tightly woven cotton cloth versus through a loose-weave jute sack:

- The **cotton cloth (low porosity)** traps steam and resists flow — just like a *green-sand mould* would if over-rammed, causing gas blows and porosity defects in the casting.
- The **jute sack (high porosity)** allows air and steam to escape freely — analogous to a *plaster or ceramic porous mould*, which breathes out gases harmlessly, yielding a sound casting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** A porous mould is the foundry equivalent of breathable fabric — it lets the mould "exhale" trapped gases so the metal inside can solidify into a defect-free shape.

### 1.3 Why Porous Moulds are Used in Casting

During metal pouring, several gases evolve simultaneously:

- Air displaced from the mould cavity by incoming molten metal.
- Steam from residual moisture in the mould.
- Combustion gases from organic binders.
- Gases dissolved in molten metal that come out of solution upon cooling.

If these gases cannot escape, they form **blow-holes, pinholes, subsurface porosity, and misruns** in the final casting. Porous moulds solve this by providing pre-engineered escape pathways.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| AFS Permeability Number | $P$ | **80 – 140** (plaster); **120 – 200** (shell) | Quantifies gas-flow capacity |
| Pore Size | $d_p$ | **5 – 50 µm** | Controls surface finish |
| Open Porosity | $\phi$ | **30 % – 45 %** | Fraction of interconnected voids |
| Mould Permeability Constant | $k$ | $10^{-13}$ to $10^{-11}$ m² | Darcy-law parameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **AFS Permeability Number** is the single most important parameter KTU examiners test. Always remember it is dimensionless and measured by passing $1000 \ cm^3$ of air through a standard $1 \times 1 \times 2$ inch mould specimen under a pressure of $10 \ cm$ of water.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Darcy's Law for Mould Permeability

The flow of gas through a porous mould is governed by **Darcy's Law**, which describes laminar viscous flow through a porous medium. For a foundry mould, this translates to:

$$v = -\frac{k}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Where:
- $v$ = superficial gas velocity (m/s)
- $k$ = intrinsic permeability of the mould (m²)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the gas (Pa·s)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference driving flow (Pa)
- $L$ = thickness of mould wall (m)

### 2.2 AFS Permeability Number (Experimental Definition)

In foundry practice, the AFS number is measured by the standard test:

$$P_{AFS} = \frac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot t \cdot p}$$

Where:
- $V$ = volume of air passed through specimen ($cm^3$)
- $h$ = height of specimen (cm)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of specimen ($cm^2$)
- $t$ = time of flow (min)
- $p$ = pressure of air (cm of water)

### 2.3 Mass Conservation for Gas Escape

The rate at which gas must be removed equals the rate at which it is generated. If $\dot{V}_g$ is the volumetric gas-generation rate and $\dot{V}_e$ is the escape rate through mould:

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P_{crit}}{\mu \cdot L_{eff}} \geq \dot{V}_g$$

For defect-free casting, the **escape rate must always exceed or equal the generation rate**.

### 2.4 Bubble-Formation Criterion (Bohacek's Relation)

A gas bubble will nucleate inside the solidifying metal only if the local partial pressure exceeds the sum of atmospheric, metallostatic, and surface-tension pressures:

$$P_{gas} \geq P_{atm} + \rho_{metal} \cdot g \cdot h + \frac{2\sigma}{r_{crit}}$$

Where:
- $\rho_{metal}$ = density of molten metal (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($9.81$ m/s²)
- $h$ = depth of metal above bubble (m)
- $\sigma$ = surface tension of molten metal (N/m)
- $r_{crit}$ = critical bubble radius (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Higher mould permeability lowers $P_{gas}$ inside the cavity by providing rapid escape, which raises $r_{crit}$, suppressing harmful micro-bubble nucleation. This is the *thermodynamic foundation* for using porous moulds.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{k}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Darcy flow in porous mould |
| 2 | $P_{AFS} = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot t \cdot p}$ | Standard permeability number |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_e = \dfrac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P_{crit}}{\mu \cdot L_{eff}}$ | Required gas-escape rate |
| 4 | $P_{gas} \geq P_{atm} + \rho g h + \dfrac{2\sigma}{r_{crit}}$ | Bubble-nucleation criterion |
| 5 | $R_{a} = f(\phi, d_p)$ | Surface roughness as function of porosity |
| 6 | $k = \dfrac{\phi^{3}}{C \cdot S^{2} \cdot (1-\phi)^{2}}$ (Kozeny-Carman) | Permeability from pore geometry |
| 7 | $q_{rad} = \epsilon \sigma (T_{mould}^{4} - T_{amb}^{4})$ | Heat loss through porous mould |
| 8 | $\rho_{apparent} = \rho_{true}(1 - \phi)$ | Apparent density from open porosity |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Porous-mould technology underpins several modern precision-casting routes:

- **Aerospace super-alloy turbine blades** (investment casting with porous ceramic shells).
- **Dental and jewellery castings** (lost-wax with gypsum-bonded plaster).
- **Automotive cylinder heads and intake manifolds** (shell moulding / Croning process).
- **Pattern-less short-run castings** (V-process / vacuum moulding uses unbonded sand held by a porous plastic sheet).
- **Investment casting of medical implants** (Ti-alloy hip stems made in porous zircon moulds).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gas-Escape Rate through a Porous Mould Wall

We start from **Darcy's Law** for incompressible viscous flow through a homogeneous porous medium:

$$\vec{v} = -\frac{k}{\mu} \nabla P$$

For a one-dimensional mould wall of thickness $L$, cross-section $A_{eff}$, and pressure drop $\Delta P = P_{cavity} - P_{outside}$:

**Step 1:** Apply Darcy's law in 1-D form:

$$\frac{Q}{A_{eff}} = \frac{k}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply to obtain the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to time to get the rate of gas escape:

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{dQ}{dt} = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P_{crit}}{\mu \cdot L_{eff}}$$

**Step 4:** Express permeability in terms of pore geometry (Kozeny-Carman):

$$k = \frac{\phi^{3}}{C \cdot S^{2} \cdot (1-\phi)^{2}}$$

Where:
- $\phi$ = porosity (fraction)
- $S$ = specific surface area of pores (m²/m³)
- $C$ = Kozeny constant (≈ 5 for spherical pores)

**Step 5:** Substitute (Step 4) into (Step 3):

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{\phi^{3} \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P_{crit}}{C \cdot S^{2} \cdot (1-\phi)^{2} \cdot \mu \cdot L_{eff}}$$

**Step 6:** For defect-free casting, set $\dot{V}_e = \dot{V}_g$ (gas generation rate), and solve for required porosity:

$$\phi = \left[ \frac{C \cdot S^{2} \cdot (1-\phi)^{2} \cdot \mu \cdot L_{eff} \cdot \dot{V}_g}{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P_{crit}} \right]^{1/3}$$

This expression gives the **minimum porosity** required in the mould design to evacuate all generated gases.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> A steel foundry uses a plaster mould of thickness $L = 50 \ mm$ to cast a bronze bearing. The cavity cross-section is $A_{eff} = 200 \ cm^2$. During pouring, gases are generated at $\dot{V}_g = 25 \ cm^3/s$. The pressure differential available is $\Delta P = 5 \ kPa = 5000 \ Pa$. Air viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$. Determine the **minimum intrinsic permeability** required.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$\dot{V}_g = 25 \ cm^3/s = 25 \times 10^{-6} \ m^3/s$$

$$A_{eff} = 200 \ cm^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} \ m^2 = 0.02 \ m^2$$

$$L = 50 \ mm = 0.05 \ m$$

$$\Delta P = 5000 \ Pa, \quad \mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$$

**Step 2 — Apply the gas-escape equation:**

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $k$:**

$$k = \frac{\dot{V}_e \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$k = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (0.05)}{(0.02) \cdot (5000)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$(25 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (0.05) = 2.25 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$(0.02) \cdot (5000) = 100$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$k = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-11}}{100} = 2.25 \times 10^{-13} \ m^{2}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**

$$k_{required} = 2.25 \times 10^{-13} \ m^2 \approx 225 \ darcies$$

Since plaster moulds typically exhibit $k \approx 100 - 500$ darcies, the design is feasible. If a denser mould material were chosen (e.g., silica with $k < 50$ darcies), the casting would show gas-blow defects.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Types of Porous Moulds

| Mould Type | Binder | Permeability Range (AFS) | Typical Surface Finish (Ra µm) | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Green-sand mould** | Bentonite clay + water | 80 – 120 | 12 – 25 | General engineering castings |
| **Dry-sand mould** | Clay + air-dried | 90 – 130 | 10 – 20 | Medium-size ferrous castings |
| **Shell mould (Croning)** | Phenolic resin over sand | 120 – 200 | 2.5 – 6 | Automotive, small precision parts |
| **Plaster (gypsum-bonded)** | Gypsum + silica | 80 – 140 | 0.8 – 2.5 | Non-ferrous, jewellery, dental |
| **Ceramic (investment)** | Colloidal silica + fused silica | 60 – 110 | 0.4 – 1.5 | Aerospace turbine blades |
| **V-process (vacuum)** | Unbonded sand + plastic film | 100 – 150 | 5 – 12 | Large pattern-less castings |
| **Sodium silicate-CO₂** | Sodium silicate hardened by CO₂ | 70 – 100 | 8 – 18 | Large steel castings |

### 3.4 Process Steps for a Plaster (Porous) Mould Casting

**Step 1:** Prepare the **plaster slurry** by mixing gypsum (CaSO₄·½H₂O), silica flour, water, and additives in precise proportions (typical ratio: 100 plaster : 60 silica : 40 water by weight).

**Step 2:** Pour the slurry over the pattern placed in the flask; allow it to set (≈ 30 – 45 minutes) and develop controlled micro-porosity as water evaporates.

**Step 3:** Strip the mould from the pattern; oven-dry at **150 – 200 °C** for 24 – 48 hours to remove free moisture.

**Step 4:** Assemble mould halves; bake at **250 – 400 °C** to remove combined water and preheat to the desired pouring temperature.

**Step 5:** Pour molten metal (typically aluminium or copper alloys at 700 – 1100 °C) into the cavity.

**Step 6:** After solidification, break the mould (since plaster is **not reusable**), retrieve the casting, and perform fettling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key process insight:** The micro-porosity in plaster forms because the water of crystallization, when driven out, leaves a network of interconnected capillaries ~ 5 – 20 µm in diameter. This controlled porosity is precisely what enables gas escape.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow of Porous-Mould Casting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pattern Preparation] --> B[Mix Plaster Slurry with Gypsum + Silica + Water]
    B --> C[Pour Slurry over Pattern in Flask]
    C --> D[Allow Setting 30 to 45 minutes]
    D --> E[Strip Mould from Pattern]
    E --> F[Oven Dry at 150 to 200 degrees C]
    F --> G[Assemble Mould Halves]
    G --> H[Preheat Mould to Pouring Temperature]
    H --> I[Pour Molten Metal into Cavity]
    I --> J[Gas Escapes through Pores]
    J --> K[Solidification of Casting]
    K --> L[Break Mould to Retrieve Casting]
    L --> M[Fettling and Inspection]
    M --> N[Finished Porous Mould Casting]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Gas Escape Mechanism in Porous Mould

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAVITY[Casting Cavity]
        M1[Molten Metal Pool]
        G1[Generated Gases]
        M1 --> G1
    end

    subgraph MOULD[Porous Mould Wall]
        P1[Pore Network with Diameter 5 to 50 micrometres]
        P2[Interconnected Capillaries]
        P1 --> P2
    end

    subgraph AMBIENT[Outside Ambient]
        A1[Atmosphere]
    end

    G1 -->|Pressure Differential Delta P| P1
    P2 -->|Darcy Flow v equals k by mu times Delta P by L| A1
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Permeability in Different Mould Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PM[Porous Mould Systems]

    PM --> P1[Gypsum Plaster Mould]
    PM --> P2[Investment Ceramic Shell]
    PM --> P3[Shell Mould Croning]
    PM --> P4[Vacuum V Process Mould]

    P1 --> Q1[Permeability 80 to 140 AFS]
    P1 --> Q2[Used for Aluminium Bronze Castings]
    P1 --> Q3[Pore Size 5 to 20 micrometres]

    P2 --> R1[Permeability 60 to 110 AFS]
    P2 --> R2[Used for Superalloy Turbine Blades]
    P2 --> R3[Pore Size 2 to 10 micrometres]

    P3 --> S1[Permeability 120 to 200 AFS]
    P3 --> S2[Used for Automotive Precision Parts]
    S3[Pore Size 10 to 30 micrometres]

    P4 --> T1[Permeability 100 to 150 AFS]
    P4 --> T2[Used for Large Patternless Castings]
    P4 --> T3[Pore Size 15 to 40 micrometres]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Pressure Distribution Across a Porous Mould

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Molten Metal Side] -->|P cavity High| B[Mould Wall Thickness L]
    B -->|Linear Pressure Drop| C[Ambient Side P atm Low]
    C --> D[Gas Discharged to Atmosphere]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The pressure falls linearly across the mould wall from $P_{cavity}$ (high, inside) to $P_{atm}$ (low, outside) only because Darcy flow assumes laminar viscous regime. If porosity is too low, the gradient becomes steep and gases back up into the metal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a porous mould and state its primary function in foundry practice.**

**Model Answer:**
A porous mould is a casting mould made of refractory material containing an interconnected network of micro-pores (typically 5 – 50 µm diameter) that permit gases, air, and steam to escape from the mould cavity during pouring and solidification of molten metal. Its primary function is to provide a controlled permeability pathway for evolved gases, thereby preventing defects such as blow-holes, pinholes, and subsurface porosity in the finished casting. Materials commonly used include gypsum-bonded plaster, ceramic-shell compositions, and resin-bonded sands, with AFS permeability numbers typically ranging from 80 to 200 depending on application.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the AFS Permeability Number? How is it experimentally determined?**

**Model Answer:**
The AFS Permeability Number is a dimensionless foundry metric that quantifies the ease with which a gas passes through a standard mould specimen. It is experimentally determined by passing $1000 \ cm^3$ of air at standard temperature through a $5.08 \ cm \times 5.08 \ cm$ (i.e., $1 \times 1$ inch) cross-section, $5.08 \ cm$ (2 inch) high specimen under a pressure of $10 \ cm$ of water, and recording the time taken. The permeability is calculated using the formula $P_{AFS} = (V \cdot h) / (A \cdot t \cdot p)$, where $V$ is volume, $h$ is specimen height, $A$ is cross-sectional area, $t$ is time in minutes, and $p$ is air pressure in cm of water. Higher values indicate greater permeability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of gas evolution during casting and describe how a porous mould controls these gases. Discuss the role of Darcy's Law in governing gas flow through the mould wall. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plaster mould of wall thickness $L = 40 \ mm$ and cavity area $A_{eff} = 250 \ cm^2$ is used to cast an aluminium bronze bearing. The gas-generation rate during pouring is $\dot{V}_g = 30 \ cm^3/s$ and the available pressure differential is $\Delta P = 6 \ kPa$. The intrinsic permeability of the plaster is $k = 3.5 \times 10^{-13} \ m^2$. Air viscosity is $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$. Calculate whether the mould permeability is sufficient to prevent gas defects. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Mechanism of gas evolution:**

During pouring of molten metal into a mould cavity, gases evolve from multiple sources:

1. **Displaced air** — the rising metal column pushes air ahead of it; without permeable walls this air becomes trapped.
2. **Steam generation** — residual moisture in green-sand or plaster moulds vaporises on contact with molten metal, producing high-pressure steam.
3. **Combustion gases** — organic binders (e.g., phenolic resin in shell moulds, dextrin in green sand) pyrolyse and release CO, CO₂, and hydrocarbons.
4. **Outgassing from melt** — hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen dissolved in the molten metal come out of solution as the metal cools and solidifies.

If the gas generation rate $\dot{V}_g$ exceeds the gas escape rate $\dot{V}_e$, gases back up into the metal and form **blow-holes, pinholes, and subsurface porosity**, degrading mechanical strength and pressure-tightness.

**Role of porous mould:**

A porous mould provides an interconnected capillary network of micro-pores. The driving force is the **pressure differential** between the cavity (high) and atmosphere (low). Gases follow the path of least resistance through these pores.

**Darcy's Law in mould permeability:**

For laminar viscous flow through a homogeneous porous medium:

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $k$ = intrinsic permeability (m²)
- $A_{eff}$ = effective cross-sectional area through which gas escapes (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = cavity-to-ambient pressure difference (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of escaping gas (Pa·s)
- $L$ = mould-wall thickness (m)

For defect-free casting, the design condition is:

$$\dot{V}_e \geq \dot{V}_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L} \geq \dot{V}_g$$

The mould designer can adjust three variables to meet this condition:
1. **Increase $k$** by selecting more porous materials or adding pore-formers.
2. **Reduce $L$** by thinning the mould wall.
3. **Increase $A_{eff}$** by providing vents and chills.

If $\dot{V}_e < \dot{V}_g$, the gas pressure inside the cavity exceeds the metallostatic head, forcing bubbles into the solidifying metal — a classic gas-blow defect.

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$L = 40 \ mm = 0.04 \ m, \quad A_{eff} = 250 \ cm^2 = 0.025 \ m^2$$

$$\dot{V}_g = 30 \ cm^3/s = 30 \times 10^{-6} \ m^3/s = 3 \times 10^{-5} \ m^3/s$$

$$\Delta P = 6 \ kPa = 6000 \ Pa, \quad k = 3.5 \times 10^{-13} \ m^2$$

$$\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's Law to compute $\dot{V}_e$:**

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{(3.5 \times 10^{-13}) \cdot (0.025) \cdot (6000)}{(1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (0.04)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$3.5 \times 10^{-13} \times 0.025 = 8.75 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$8.75 \times 10^{-15} \times 6000 = 5.25 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.04 = 7.2 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 6 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$\dot{V}_e = \frac{5.25 \times 10^{-11}}{7.2 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.29 \times 10^{-5} \ m^3/s$$

**Step 7 — Convert to $cm^3/s$ for comparison:**

$$\dot{V}_e = 7.29 \times 10^{-5} \ m^3/s = 72.9 \ cm^3/s$$

**Step 8 — Compare with gas-generation rate:**

$$\dot{V}_e = 72.9 \ cm^3/s \quad \text{vs.} \quad \dot{V}_g = 30 \ cm^3/s$$

Since $\dot{V}_e \gg \dot{V}_g$, the mould permeability is **more than sufficient** to evacuate all generated gases. The design margin is:

$$\text{Safety Factor} = \frac{\dot{V}_e}{\dot{V}_g} = \frac{72.9}{30} \approx 2.43$$

> The casting will be **defect-free** with respect to gas porosity. The mould design is acceptable.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **plaster-mould casting process**. List at least four advantages and three limitations of using plaster moulds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shell mould (Croning process) of thickness $L = 8 \ mm$ has a porosity $\phi = 0.38$ and pore specific surface area $S = 5 \times 10^{5} \ m^{2}/m^3$. Use the **Kozeny-Carman equation** to compute its intrinsic permeability, and verify whether it lies in the typical range for shell moulds (100 – 400 darcies; 1 darcy $= 9.87 \times 10^{-13} \ m^2$). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a):

**Process steps for plaster-mould casting:**

1. **Pattern preparation:** A metal or wooden master pattern is mounted on a match-plate, treated with a release agent (soap solution or silicone spray).

2. **Slurry mixing:** A plaster slurry is prepared by blending gypsum (CaSO₄·½H₂O), silica flour (SiO₂), talc, and water in the ratio 100 : 60 : 40 by weight. The slurry is vacuum-de-aerated to remove entrained air bubbles.

3. **Pouring slurry:** The slurry is poured into a flask containing the pattern. It is left to set (≈ 30 – 45 minutes) as the gypsum hydrates to CaSO₄·2H₂O, forming the rigid porous matrix.

4. **Stripping:** After setting, the hardened mould is carefully separated from the pattern. Sharp edges are trimmed and the mould is dried.

5. **Drying:** The mould is oven-dried at **150 – 200 °C** for 24 – 48 hours to drive off free water. Bound water is removed by further baking at **250 – 400 °C**.

6. **Assembly & preheating:** Mould halves are clamped together and preheated to **150 – 250 °C** to prevent thermal shock on pouring.

7. **Pouring:** Molten metal (typically Al, Cu, Mg alloys; pouring temperature 700 – 1100 °C) is poured into the cavity.

8. **Cooling & knock-out:** After solidification, the mould is broken (since plaster is **single-use**) and the casting is retrieved for fettling.

**Advantages of plaster moulds (any four):**

1. Excellent surface finish (Ra 0.8 – 2.5 µm) — among the best of all mould types.
2. High dimensional accuracy (± 0.05 mm or better).
3. Superior reproduction of fine details such as textures, lettering, and thin sections.
4. Capability to cast thin-wall sections (down to 1 mm) without misruns.
5. Low gas-defect incidence due to controlled permeability (AFS 80 – 140).

**Limitations (any three):**

1. **Single-use** — mould is broken to retrieve casting, leading to higher per-unit cost.
2. **Long cycle time** — drying and baking takes 24 – 48 hours.
3. **Restricted to non-ferrous metals** — plaster cannot withstand temperatures above ~ 1200 °C; unsuitable for steel and cast iron.
4. **Lower mould strength** — prone to damage during handling.

##### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Recall the Kozeny-Carman equation:**

$$k = \frac{\phi^{3}}{C \cdot S^{2} \cdot (1-\phi)^{2}}$$

For nearly spherical pores, the Kozeny constant $C \approx 5$.

**Step 2 — Substitute given values:**

$$\phi = 0.38, \quad 1 - \phi = 0.62, \quad S = 5 \times 10^{5} \ m^{2}/m^3, \quad C = 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\phi^{3}$:**

$$\phi^{3} = (0.38)^{3} = 0.054872$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(1 - \phi)^{2}$:**

$$(1 - \phi)^{2} = (0.62)^{2} = 0.3844$$

**Step 5 — Compute $S^{2}$:**

$$S^{2} = (5 \times 10^{5})^{2} = 25 \times 10^{10} = 2.5 \times 10^{11} \ m^{4}/m^{6}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C \cdot S^{2} \cdot (1 - \phi)^{2}$:**

$$5 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{11}) \cdot (0.3844) = 1.25 \times 10^{12} \cdot 0.3844 = 4.805 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 7 — Divide $\phi^{3}$ by the result of Step 6:**

$$k = \frac{0.054872}{4.805 \times 10^{11}} = 1.142 \times 10^{-13} \ m^{2}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to darcies:**

$$1 \ darcy = 9.87 \times 10^{-13} \ m^2$$

$$k_{darcies} = \frac{1.142 \times 10^{-13}}{9.87 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 0.116 \ darcies$$

**Step 9 — Comparison with typical shell-mould range:**

The typical AFS permeability of shell moulds is 120 – 200, which corresponds roughly to 100 – 400 darcies depending on resin content and grain size. Our computed value of **0.116 darcies** is **far below** the typical range, indicating that the assumed parameters ($S = 5 \times 10^{5}$, $\phi = 0.38$) represent a much denser structure than a real shell mould. To achieve realistic shell-mould permeability, the pore specific surface area should be closer to $S \approx 5 \times 10^{4} \ m^{2}/m^{3}$ (i.e., larger pores), which gives:

$$k \approx \frac{(0.38)^{3}}{5 \cdot (5 \times 10^{4})^{2} \cdot (0.62)^{2}} \approx 1.14 \times 10^{-10} \ m^{2} \approx 115 \ darcies$$

This falls comfortably within the expected shell-mould range, confirming the model.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing units of permeability** — Always quote intrinsic permeability $k$ in m² or darcies, and AFS permeability number as dimensionless. Mixing them up costs **2 – 3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $cm^3/s$ to $m^3/s$** in numerical problems — leads to wrong answers by a factor of $10^{6}$.
> 3. **Skipping the comparison $\dot{V}_e \geq \dot{V}_g$** — examiners specifically look for this concluding statement; without it, **1 – 2 marks** are docked.
> 4. **Not stating Darcy's Law explicitly** before using it — always write the formula and define each variable.
> 5. **Forgetting the Kozeny constant** $C \approx 5$ — a frequent error in Part (b) numerical questions.
> 6. **Mixing up plaster mould and green sand mould applications** — plaster is for non-ferrous, green sand is for general/ferrous work.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
