# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] — Define road rubberisation. Name any two advantages of using natural rubber latex in bitumen modification.**

**Model Answer (for valuation):**

Road rubberisation is the process of incorporating natural rubber latex (NRL) or crumb rubber derived from latex products into bituminous road binders to enhance mechanical performance and durability.

**Two advantages (any two):**
1. Improved **elastic recovery** and fatigue life → reduced rutting and cracking.
2. Enhanced **skid resistance and noise reduction** (3–7 dB) due to poroelastic surface.
3. **Sustainable recycling** of waste latex products (tyres, gloves).
4. Better **ageing resistance** due to natural antioxidants (tocopherols) in NR.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] — Differentiate between the wet process and dry process of road rubberisation.**

**Model Answer (tabular form preferred):**

| Feature | Wet Process | Dry Process |
|---|---|---|
| Raw form | Liquid NRL | Crumb rubber granules |
| Addition point | Into hot bitumen | Into hot aggregate |
| Particle size | Colloidal (~0.1 µm) | 0.4–4.0 mm |
| Mixing equipment | Low-shear blender | High-shear mill |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice as per KTU ESE Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model, Module 4]**

> *Answer both parts (a) and (b).*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the wet process of road rubberisation using natural rubber latex. With a neat flow diagram, describe the preparation and addition of latex into hot bitumen. List the engineering properties that are enhanced.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The wet process involves **direct blending of natural rubber latex (NRL) with hot bitumen** (160–180 °C) under low-shear mixing to produce a polymer-modified bituminous binder.

**2. Latex preparation (2 marks):**
- Field latex is filtered through 40-mesh screens and preserved with **0.2 % ammonia**.
- Optionally concentrated to **60 % DRC** by centrifugation.
- Dosage calculated as **5 %–10 %** of bitumen weight (dry-rubber basis).

**3. Addition procedure (2 marks):**
- Bitumen heated to **160–180 °C** in a stirred tank.
- Latex slowly dosed via metering pump.
- Mixing continued for **20–30 minutes** to allow water evaporation and polymer dispersion.
- Temperature carefully controlled to avoid **foaming** (water → steam → bubbles).

**4. Properties enhanced (2 marks):**
- **Elastic recovery** increases from ~30 % to ~70 %.
- **Softening point** rises by 6–12 °C.
- **Penetration** drops by 20–40 %.
- Improved **ageing resistance** (natural antioxidants).
- Reduced **temperature susceptibility**.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**A 1000 kg batch of bitumen is to be modified using field latex with TSC = 30 % and DRC = 60 %. If the target rubber content is 5 % of bitumen weight, calculate the mass of latex required. Also state the verification step.**

**Model Answer (stepwise, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mass of dry rubber needed (2 marks):**
$$
M_{\text{rubber}} = \frac{5}{100} \times 1000 = 50 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of total solids (2 marks):**
$$
M_{\text{TS}} = \frac{50 \times 100}{60} = 83.33 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of field latex (2 marks):**
$$
M_{\text{latex}} = \frac{83.33 \times 100}{30} = 277.78 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{277.78 \times 0.30 \times 0.60}{1000} = 5.00\% \;\checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{latex}} \approx 277.8 \text{ kg}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model, Module 4]**

> *Answer both parts (a) and (b).*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Describe the dry process of road rubberisation using crumb rubber. List its advantages over the wet process. Mention the relevant Indian specification.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Process description (3 marks):**
- End-of-life latex products (tyres, gloves, foam) are shredded, granulated, and screened to **0.4–4.0 mm**.
- Steel belts removed by **magnetic separation**.
- Crumb rubber blended with **hot aggregate (170–200 °C)** in a high-shear pugmill for **45 min–2 hours**.
- Crumb rubber absorbs maltenes, swells, and partially depolymerises.
- Bitumen added afterwards → **bitumen-rubber asphalt concrete**.

**2. Advantages over wet process (3 marks):**
- Uses **recycled waste** → circular economy.
- Better **noise reduction** (poroelastic effect).
- Higher **modified binder viscosity** → suitable for open-graded friction course (OGFC).
- Suitable for **high-axle-load corridors**.

**3. Indian specification (1 mark):**
**IRC:SP:53-2010** and **MoRTH specifications** endorse crumb-rubber-modified bitumen (CRMB) for surface courses, SAMI, and stress-absorbing interlayers.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**A bituminous binder has base penetration 80 (0.1 mm) and softening point 48 °C. After modification with 5 % NR latex (wet process), the modified binder shows penetration 55 and softening point 56 °C. Compute the penetration ratio and softening point rise. Comment on the modification effect.**

**Model Answer (stepwise):**

**Step 1 — Penetration Ratio (3 marks):**
$$
\text{PR} = \frac{P_{\text{mod}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100 = \frac{55}{80} \times 100 = 68.75\%
$$

**Step 2 — Softening Point Rise (2 marks):**
$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{mod}} - T_{\text{base}} = 56 - 48 = +8 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3 — Comments (2 marks):**

- **Penetration drop of 31.25 %** indicates the binder is **stiffer** — improves **rutting resistance**.
- **Softening point rise of 8 °C** is within the expected range (+6 to +12 °C) for 5 % latex.
- The combination confirms **successful modification** → binder is more **thermally resistant** and **elastic**.
- Suitable for **high-temperature regions** of India (Kerala, Tamil Nadu).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic (KTU 2024 pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing TSC with DRC** — TSC includes non-rubber solids (proteins, lipids); DRC excludes them. Wrong use loses 1–2 marks in Part B numericals.
> 2. **Penetration units forgotten** — Penetration value is in *0.1 mm*, not mm. Examiner deducts marks if units are written as "mm" instead of "(0.1 mm)".
> 3. **Elastic Recovery formula error** — Using $L_0$ instead of $(L_{\max} - L_0)$ in the denominator (see Section 3.3 derivation).
> 4. **No flow diagram in wet-process answer** — Examiner's checklist always demands a flow diagram (mandatory 1–2 marks).
> 5. **Forgetting IRC/MoRTH references** — Always quote **IRC:SP:53-2010** and **ASTM D6114** for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing wet & dry process in one answer** — Examiner marks against "irrelevance". Stay strictly on the asked process.
> 7. **Storage stability criterion** — Students forget $\Delta T_{\text{soft}} \leq 2.5$ °C (ASTM D5892) is a **critical acceptance parameter**.

---

> - ⭐ Penetration drop of **20–40 %** after modification → stiffer binder.
> - ⭐ Softening point rise of **+6 to +12 °C** → better high-temp performance.

> - ⭐ Noise reduction: **3–7 dB** (poroelastic effect).
